title: Academic Registry
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Academic Registry > Annual Monitoring and Academic Review Academic Registry Annual Monitoring and Academic Review The University’s approach to assuring standards and driving up quality through annual monitoring and academic review is: Data-informed Transparent Risk-based Proportionate Timely Action-focused Inclusive Our approach to annual monitoring and academic review provides an internal level playing field to assess and drive up quality, facilitating the best possible opportunities for learning, progression and achievement for all students. Annual Monitoring Please find below a timeline which outlines when each step is required to be completed. As a point of reference, the term QPP is used in replacement of Quality Priority Plan and CSAR in replacement of Course Self-Assessment Report (Specific to Degree Apprenticeships). Please note that these QPPs will now replace EQuIPs. Key Dates and Timeline As demonstrated in the timeline above, the following key stages will take place: Degree apprenticeship (DA) CSARs have already been launched to course leaders and AD(A)s for completion. CSARs remain the same as in previous years and are due to be completed by Tuesday 26th August 2025. Please note that course leaders completing CSARs for DA courses will not be required to complete an additional QPP as good practice and action plans are included within the CSAR documentation. These internal reports provide a comprehensive view of key trends and performance indicators across the student lifecycle, supporting evidence-based decision-making and continuous enhancement of academic quality. Purpose and Use These reports are intended for internal stakeholders only, including academic schools and senior leadership teams. They serve as a vital resource for monitoring performance, supporting quality assurance processes, and driving continuous improvement. These reports must not be circulated or reproduced externally, either in whole or in part, without prior permission of the author. Bringing It All Together The culmination of the annual reports below, covering student outcomes, student experience, quality assurance, and Collaborative Provision and Degree Apprenticeships, alongside other monitoring and enhancement activities, provides the evidence base for the University’s Board of Governors Assurance Statement on Quality. This statement confirms that robust processes are in place to maintain academic standards and continuously improve the student experience. Access and Participation Annual Report The annual Access and Participation Report (APP) evaluates the University’s progress against its Access and Participation Plan commitments. The information contained on these pages is designed to facilitate the design, delivery and continuous improvement of apprenticeships, and to ensure the quality of the apprentice experience from application to completion of the full apprenticeship. If you have any questions about the information within this page, please email us as academicregistry@port.ac.uk What is a Degree Apprenticeship? A Degree Apprenticeship is a programme that is funded either by an apprentice’s employer and/or the Government. To be eligible for an apprenticeship, the learner must be employed and have at least 6 hours of training per week. These hours must be during the apprentice’s normal working hours and are referred to as Off-the-Job Training (OTJ). All apprentices must be enrolled on a specific apprenticeship standard, which is relevant to their job role. These standards outline the required Knowledge, Skills and Behaviours that all apprentices must provide evidence to demonstrate competence. Once an apprentice has completed their required period of training, they will complete their End Point Assessment (EPA). This is a mandatory requirement for all apprenticeship standards. This section is specifically designed to assist our staff and collaborative partners in navigating and applying the University’s Assessment Regulations. Here, you will find the comprehensive University Assessment Regulations and accompanying documents. These regulations are categorised into three main groups, each tailored to different aspects of our academic offerings: 1. Undergraduate and Postgraduate Taught Courses: A unified set of regulations governs all taught courses up to and including Level 7. These ensure consistency and clarity across the University’s undergraduate and postgraduate courses. 2. Partner-Specific Assessment Regulations: These are specialised regulations designed for specific collaborative courses and partnerships, recognising that some partners have unique needs for certain courses. 3. Research Programmes: Tailored specifically for research-based programmes, these regulations guide the research journey and ensure that academic standards are upheld throughout the process leading to research degrees. Student-Centric Assessment Regulations Our Assessment Regulations are designed to support the student experience and promote academic success for all students equally while preserving academic integrity and safeguarding academic standards. Written in clear, accessible language these regulations are structured to be easily navigable, with direct links to additional resources and support. The role of an External Examiner is primarily one of quality assurance, ensuring that awards granted are comparable in standard to those of other higher education institutions, that national subject threshold standards are complied with, and the treatment of students is equitable and consistent. External Examiners are independent and impartial advisers, providing informative comment on standards and quality and student achievement in relation to those standards. This is achieved by sampling students’ work and reporting on the operation of procedures and processes. Specific responsibilities are to: Confirm the assessment process is appropriate, fair, consistent and in line with policies and procedures Ensure the aims, objectives and learning outcomes are appropriate and comparable to threshold requirements Confirm standards and student performance are comparable with that of other institutions Confirm the currency, relevance and coherence of the curriculum Report on academic standards and quality and highlight good practice and innovation Appointment Appointment of External Examiners are considered for approval by the External Examiner Approval Panel, who meet on a monthly basis, on behalf of Academic Council. The proposal will be considered in line with the External Examiners Regulations and Procedures Academic Registry Acade

body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Academic Registry > Professional, Statutory and Regulatory Bodies Academic Registry Professional, Statutory and Regulatory Bodies (PSRBs) What is a PSRB? Professional, Statutory and Regulatory Body (PSRB) accreditation is a general term used to describe organisations which are authorised to accredit, approve or recognise specific programmes in the context of the requirements of the PSRB. Why are they important? A valued feature of the University's undergraduate and postgraduate provision is the number of degree programmes which are accredited by a PSRB. The University aims to ensure that all its programmes which are eligible for PSRB accreditation secure and retain the accreditation. This is just one of many ways the University ensures that professional standards and quality are maintained and that students gain the skills and knowledge required by employers. A PSRB normally accredits a programme for a specific number of years after which they return to review and re-accredit the programmes for the next period. All PSRB accreditation and review reports are monitored by the Board of Governors. University of Portsmouth PSRB Activity All new PSRB accreditations, amendments to existing accreditations, and outcomes of visits and renewals need to be reported to Academic Registry to ensure the PSRB database is updated. PSRB accreditations will not be publicised on the course pages or any other location until they have been reported to Academic Registry. After the PSRB accreditations have been added to the database. The term ‘accreditation’ is used in this context to describe any link with a professional body that results in recognition of our courses. Please u

body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Academic Registry > Student Casework Academic Registry Student Casework The Student Casework Team is responsible for the delivery of a high-quality, effective and efficient customer-focused service to all stakeholders in relation to complaints, student conduct and appeals. Complaints The Student Casework team are responsible for three complaints procedures: The Student Complaints Procedure This procedure allows students to complain about the majority of things that the University has/has not done, aside from complaints about academic decisions (which are handled as Appeals), and other matters of academic judgement. The Student Complaints procedure has three stages: Stage 1 (Informal Complaint) - students can raise their concerns with staff locally, or with the Student Casework team. The recipient should consider whether there is evidence of a failure to properly follow University policies, or another failure such as incorrect information given. Where an issue is identified, the individual should take steps to have the situation rectified. The outcome should be communicated to the student within 10 working days. Stage 2 (Formal Complaint) - where an informal resolution is not possible, students can request a formal investigation of their complaint by contacting the Student Casework team. This team will appoint an Investigating Officer to undertake a full review of the issues raised. Stage 3 (Review Stage) - if the student remains unhappy after the completion of the formal investigation, they may apply to the Executive Director of Corporate Governance for a review of the findings and conclusions of that investigation. The Applicant Complaints Procedure Th

From January 2026, we are proud to introduce Student Quality Representatives (SQRs). Our SQRs are current students, typically one per Faculty and London Campus, employed on a paid basis to collaborate with faculties and central teams, including Academic Registry, CADI, Academic Services, the Library etc. on Faculty and University-wide initiatives that drive quality enhancement. SQRs play a pivotal role in shaping improvements that matter most to our students. They actively contribute to projects that strengthen teaching, learning, and assessment practices, academic regulations and policies, ensuring that the student voice is embedded in our development activities. By bringing their lived experience and perspective, SQRs help us identify opportunities for innovation and continuous improvement. Working alongside Course Representatives, and in close liaison with the Students’ Union, SQRs act as an important additional quality assurance link between students and the University. For more information about Student Quality Representatives and what they are currently contributing to please contact academicregistry@port.ac.uk Meet our current team of Student Quality Representatives: Jakub Roj Raiyan Rahman Wealth Unegbu Jessie Rosser Explore Academic CommitteesAcademic Roles and ResponsibilitiesAcademic Year DatesAnnual Monitoring and Academic ReviewAnnual ReportingApprenticeships Quality ManagementAssessment RegulationsCollaborative Pro

body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Academic Services > Faculties Administration Academic Services What do the Faculty Administrative Service Centres do? The Faculty Administrative Service Centres (FASCs) provide a comprehensive service in support of academic administration. They operate across two academic administration centres: Portland Building and the University Learning Centre. Each Centre has a dedicated management team focused on the development and delivery of a high-quality administrative support service. Centre staff work collaboratively to ensure a consistent, efficient and effective approach to student and academic administration. Key Responsibilities The FASCs handle a wide range of activities, including: Curriculum Management: coordination and administration of course structures and module changes, including the validation of information supplied by academic colleagues and liaison with Academic Registry, Central Timetabling Unit (CTU) and Centre for Academic and Digital Innovation (CADI). Student Records Management: maintaining and validating student information through the University’s student records system (SITS), including updating students details and status i.e. withdrawn, interrupted, transferred and validating student data to confirm eligibility to graduate. Assessment and Examinations: Organising coursework submissions, in person and online. Managing the extenuating circumstances process. Coordinating examinations and assessments, including Examination Boards, confirmation of results, and progression decisions. Monitoring and assisting with marks entry. Compliance: Ensuring adherence to academic and assessment regulations, meeting professional, statutory, and regul

title: Corporate Governance
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Corporate Governance > Internal Audit Corporate Governance Internal Audit This page has been created to provide a short guide aimed at staff who are hosting or contributing to an audit undertaken by the University’s internal audit function. The guide is intended to explain some background about the audit process, what you can expect during the course of an audit and to advise on how you can make the audit run smoothly and as useful as possible for improving and enhancing the work of your area. In general, the most effective audits are those where the relevant University staff engage most fully. We are committed to making our internal audits a positive and collaborative experience. That means starting early with key stakeholders and keeping communication clear and open every step of the way. Who are the key contacts? The University’s day-to-day relationship with the Internal Audit partners (Deloitte) is managed by the Corporate Governance Department. If you need any support or have queries, feel free to reach out Anita Randell or Sarah Hare from the Risk and Resilience Team at internal-audit@port.ac.uk. What is the purpose of an Internal Audit? Internal Audit provides independent assurance that the University’s risk management, governance and internal control processes are operating effectively. Why it matters? Through systematic evaluations, Internal Audit plays a key role in identifying and mitigating potential risks, ensuring compliance with statutory and regulatory requirements, and safeguarding the University’s assets. It supports informed decision-making, promotes accountability and transparency, and fosters a culture of continuous improvement—help

The way in which the University manages this personal information is governed by the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR). These web pages aim to explain how the University manages information in line with the GDPR, and the implications for both staff and students. There is a general explanation of the most relevant parts of the GDPR that are applicable to everyone, followed by information for students and staff on how both groups are affected by the legislation - your rights and responsibilities. title: Freedom of Information | University of Portsmouth
body: Skip to main content <iframe src="https://www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-WHX6C84" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Freedom of Information (FOI) promotes more openness among public authorities in England, Wales and Northern Ireland. This includes the University. The Freedom of Information Act (2000) gives anyone the right to request to see the information that we hold. You can get information about the University in 2 ways: Our publication schemes Our publication schemes hold lots of information about us and our subsidiary companies. Publishing and maintaining publication schemes is a requirements of section 19 of the Freedom of Information Act. Our publication scheme includes information that all Higher and Further Education institutions must make publicly available. View our publication schemes Freedom of Information requests If the information you want isn't covered by our publication schemes, you can make a freedom of information request. We'll usually send you the information you request within 20 working days. If we can't give you some or all of the information you ask for, we'll tell you why. How to make a Freedom of Information request Complaining about a response to your freedom of information request If you're unhappy with our response to your Freedom of Information request, you can complain or request a review of the decision in writing to the Vice-Chancellor. In your request you need to explain why you think we should provide the information you want. Email vice-chancellor@port.ac.uk with your request or send it to: The Vice-Chancellor University of Portsmouth Mercantile House Hampshire Terrace Portsmouth PO1 2EG Appealing a decision If you're not satisfied with the response to your complaint, you can contact the Information Commissioner's Office, the regulatory body for information governance matters. | We use cookiesWe may use cookies to analyse visitor data, improve our website, show personalised content an

title: Corporate Governance
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Corporate Governance > Information Governance Corporate Governance Information Governance Information governance is a framework of policies, procedures and controls to manage information in a joined-up way. It involves knowing: what information is held where it is held who is reponsible for it how long to keep it Information governance also involves establishing effective procedures for all aspects of information management, including creation, maintenance, storage, use, retention and destruction. Three areas - namely, Data Protection, Records Management and Information Security - are working together to develop a coherent approach towards information governance across the University. Resources for IG Champions New! - Resources by Role BenefitsThe University's information is its lifeblood: it it the basis on which decisions are made and services provided. The aim of information governance is to ensure that this information - whether in paper or electronic format - is handled efficiently and effectively at all times. In particular, information governance should help to: maintain confidence in the reliability of information protect the privacy of students and staff comply with legal and regulatory requirements support decision-making by ensuring that relevant, accurate information can be readily located increase cost-effectiveness by making sure data is disposed of when no longer needed minimise the risk of information security breaches We can provide advice, guidance and training on all aspects of information management. title: Prevent duty | University of Portsmouth
body: Skip to main content <iframe src="https://www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-WHX6C84" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> The Counter-Terrorism and Security Act 2015 placed a responsibility upon all universities to have ‘due regard to the need to prevent people from being drawn into terrorism,’ with effect from 28 September 2015. This has become known as the ‘Prevent duty’. The Government’s Prevent duty guidance states that relevant higher education institutions need to balance their legal duties in terms of both freedom of speech and academic freedom, while also protecting student and staff welfare. The approach at Portsmouth is particularly focused on student and staff welfare. The University’s Prevent duty activities are overseen by the Safeguarding Board, which is chaired by the Deputy Vice-Chancellor Global and Student Life – who leads on the Prevent agenda, with the support of Director of Student Services as Prevent Coordinator. For further information please email kevin.child@port.ac.uk | We use cookiesWe may use cookies to analyse visitor data, improve our website, show personalised content and give you a great website experience. body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Corporate Governance > Register of interests Corporate Governance Register of Interests Declarations of Interest The University encourages its staff to participate in a wide range of external activities that are complementary to its strategic goals and serve the public interest. Such activity brings benefits for the individual, for the University and for the public interest. These may include, for example, governorships, trusteeships and voluntary work. However, such activities may sometimes give rise to actual, potential or perceived conflicts of interest and staff must ensure that these are properly and fully declared to the University. Refer to Declarations of Interest Policy. Declarations should be made via the online form available on the Hornbill System. Declarations of Interest FAQs. Declarations of Corporate Hospitality and Gifts It is a requirement of the Financial Regulations that you complete this form if you have received Corporate Hospitality or Gifts. Refer to Corporate Hospitality and Gifts: Procedures for Receipt of Corporate Hospitality and Gifts by Members of Staff and Governors of the University. A declaration should be made where the value of a single gift or hospitality event is above £50, or where a repeated gift cumulatively exceeds £150 in a period of twelve months, or where repeated hospitality cumulatively exceeds £150 in an annual year. Any queries about the policy should be addressed to Adrian Parry, Executive Director of Corporate Governance. Declarations should be made via the online form available on the Hornbill System. body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Corporate Governance > DBS Checks Corporate Governance Frequently Asked Questions ‌‌Everything you ever wanted to know about DBS Checks...! 1, What is a DBS Check? A Disclosure and Barring Services Check, DBS check for short, is a check of a person's criminal convictions and cautions to help ensure it is appropriate for a person to work with vulnerable groups, including children. 2, Do I need a DBS check? If you are a member of staff and you have queries regarding DBS checks, please contact the HR Service Centre. If you are a student and you have queries regarding DBS checks, please contact your department. 3, How do I complete a DBS check? The University uses an umbrella company, Due Diligence Checking (DDC), to complete our DBS checks. Should you need a DBS check and you are a member of staff, HR will be able to administer your check for you. If you are a student, your department will be able to administer your check. 4, What information do I need to complete a DBS check? If a DBS check is required for your role, as either staff or student, you will be sent an email from either HR or your department. This email will contain a list of the information required for the ID check and address checks. 5, I do not have the ID/address requirements requested by my department. What do I do? If you do not have the information requested by your department, please contact them directly so they can work with you to arrange a suitable alternative. 6, Do I need to pay for my DBS check? If your DBS check is required for your studies or your role, the University will assume the cost of the DBS check. 7, What if I am not using my birth name; can I still get my check? Of

body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Estates and Campus Services > General information Estates and Campus Services General Information Definition of Works Repairs and Maintenance Long Term Maintenance Rechargable Capital Repairs and Maintenance These works comprise all aspects of the built estate that break and wear out, excluding Long Term Maintenance. Currently, anyone in the University can report faults and request repairs and maintenance. The procedure for so doing can be found on the Repairs and Maintenance page. A large proportion of this category is planned maintenance generated by the Estates Department. Long Term Maintenance These works comprise specific maintenance contracts that have been identified by the Estates Department as being required to bring the estate up to an acceptable level of condition. Projects are scheduled over a number of years and prioritized by urgency of need and amount of funding available in any given financial year. Rechargeable These works comprise those items specifically requested by Faculties and/or departments and which they will fund. All types of work are catered for from minor items such as putting up shelves etc, to relatively major adaptions and redecoration. The procedure for requesting such works can be found on the Rechargeable Works page. Capital These are one off contracts generated by the needs of the University strategic plans and funded centrally. They generally comprise new build or major refurbishment projects but can also include a number of smaller projects brought together to enable a series of events to take place. Such events could include departmental relocations requested centrally which would require alteration and adaption wo

body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Estates and Campus Services > Engineering maintenance and management Estates and Campus Services Engineering, Maintenance and Management Within the mechanical section of the Estates Department, we are responsible for the operation maintenance and development of plant equipment and services associated with the built environment. Our long term aim is to provide the users throughout the University with comfortable working conditions whilst at the same time minimising our impact on the global environment in terms of energy consumed and control of pollution. At the core of our operational strategy is the Satchwell BAS 2800+ Building Energy Management System which incorporates the latest technology and software. This allows us to control and monitor plant operation and status from a central location at our Guildhall Walk offices which ultimately provides an efficient and focussed response to faults and breakdowns. In our approach to maintenance we use a proven combination of in house staff, supplemented by specialist sub contractors and currently have a schedule of maintenance for all plant and equipment throughout the University. title: Estates and Campus Services
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Estates and Campus Services > Property records and database Estates and Campus Services Property Records and Database The Estates Department operates two corporate systems supporting the maintenance of the University's estate in both Help Desk and Work Order Tracking. MAXIMO This is the maintenance system at the heart of our operation and provides the main support for our Help Desk, Work Order tracking, preventative maintenance, time logging and asset management. Requests to our Help Desk are passed electronically to our supervisors who prioritise and allocate the work to the appropriate tradesmen. When jobs are complete, details of materials and time are entered back into the system for subsequent analysis and financial processing. ARCHIBUS Details of all space owned by the University are held in the Archibus database, which is linked to digitised plans held in an AutoCAD database. The space database is used to provide statistical returns to the Funding Council and is the basis of calculations for space charging, while the AutoCAD plans are maintained to provide up to date details of room and floor layouts, as well as design changes for refurbishment. Each year a validation exercise is carried out to ensure that space is correctly assigned to departments, and throughout the year amendments are made to reflect changes in ownership, room use, door numbers and areas of rooms. The department's link with Space Management ensure consistency of data between Archibus and Facilities CMIS, the University's timetabling system. title: Global
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Global > Admin Team Global Admin Team The Admin Team deliver outstanding customer service to enquiring students and colleagues around the University (as well as external agents and partner organisations) and providing comprehensive administrative support to the wider department, ensuring all policies and procedures are carried out in accordance with University rules and regulations and aligning with best practice. What do we do? Schengen visa letters General enquiries and signposting Management of global@port.ac.uk mailbox Freight Management; (eg, stock material for overseas offices, material(s) for overseas recruitment events etc) Administration of Overseas Representative Agreements PA support to the Director/Deputy Director or UoP Global Financial Administration (processing and reconciliation of Purchase Orders, processing invoices for payment, reconciling expense claims etc) HR Administration (casual employee timesheets, preparation of itineraries for new starters etc) Processing of Agent Commission relating to September and January intake Management of agentadmin@port.ac.uk mailbox Corporate Social Responsibility (CSR) related projects (eg, Reverse Advent Calendar etc) Contact Information: E: global@port.ac.uk T: 02392 843488 Administration Team: Paul Langford Global Administration Manager paul.langford@port.ac.uk Ext 3525 Anna Voke Global Administrative Officer anna.voke@port.ac.uk Ext 5114 Hayley Wheeler Global Administrator hayley.wheeler@port.ac.uk Ext 3446 Eloise Bouron Administrator eloise.bouron@port.ac.uk Ext 3488 Explore Global UniversityofPortsmouth Home Students Staff Contact and departments Home Staff services Departments Resources © Uni

title: Global
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Global > Exchanges and Study Abroad Global Exchanges and Study Abroad The Exchanges and Study abroad team provides advice and support to all students interested in studying or working abroad as part of their degree, as well as providing opportunities for Academic and Professional staff to undertake teaching or training activities with the Erasmus+ programme. The team is also responsible and the first point of contact for all incoming exchange and study abroad students to the University. What do we do? Erasmus+ programme for students to study or work abroad Erasmus+ graduate traineeships Erasmus+ programme for staff for teaching and/or training Erasmus+ inter-institutional agreements International student exchanges Study Abroad programme (short-term academic study) Advice on all study and work abroad opportunities Contact Information: General study abroad enquiries: studyabroad@port.ac.uk General Erasmus+ enquiries: erasmus@port.ac.uk University of Portsmouth students going out on an Erasmus+ placement: erasmus.outgoing@port.ac.uk Erasmus+ students coming in to the University of Portsmouth: erasmus.incoming@port.ac.uk For staff interested in Erasmus+ staff mobility: erasmus.staff@port.ac.uk For Erasmus+ agreement requests or enquiries:erasmus.agreements@port.ac.uk Opening hours: Monday to Friday 10am-3pm Exchanges and Study Abroad team: Chrissy Hadjipanteli Exchanges and Study Abroad Manager and Erasmus+ Institutional Coordinator chrissy.hadjipanteli@port.ac.uk Ext 3465 Dan Wightman Exchanges and Study Abroad Officer daniel.wightman@port.ac.uk Ext 3149 Joanna Rogasz Exchanges and Study Abroad Adviser joanna.rogasz@port.ac.uk Ext 3515 Explore Global Unive

title: Global
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Global > International Student Advisers Global International Student Advisers The International Student Advice team provide immigration advice and support to international students studying at the university. They are are the only team in the university who are qualified to give visa advice to students. They also manage the International Orientation Programme and social and cultural events. What do we do? Advice on Tier 4 student visas, Short-term Study visas and signposting for visa options after studies Lost and stolen visas and passports for students Police Registration Cultural and social events throughout the year including annual Global Week, Festival of Cultures, and a full programme of coach excursions to cities and attractions in the UK which are open to all students Deliver the Orientation Programme for new arriving International and EU students in September and January Publish a regular newsletter for International and EU students called WeAreGlobal Manage the UoP Global Community Facebook Page Provide general support and signposting for International and EU students We are open to students Monday - Friday 10am -3pm. Students do not need an appointment to see us, they can just drop in. Contact information: Current students contact: international.student.adviser@port.ac.uk Prospective students/applicants contact: Tier4-visa@port.ac.uk Telephone: 02392 845121 or 3524 International Student Advisers: Alana Holland International Student Advice Manager alana.holland@port.ac.uk Ext 5116 Anna Vaernes International Student Adviser anna.vaernes@port.ac.uk Ext 5121 Laura Ford International Student Adviser (Mon, Weds, Thurs) laura.ford@port.ac.uk Ext 352

The Conversion Team assist with converting offer holders into students by providing details of outstanding conditions and answering applicant questions. What do we do? Manage UoP Global Social Media channels Plan and deliver Global Marketing strategy - including online nurture campaigns, international agent marketing support. Recruitment Team Marketing Support Support applicants to help them convert to students Produce a range of marketing material for the global offices. This involves assessing the applicant meets the University entry requirements for each course. The Team also produces offer letters, liaising with Agents and Partners directly over the admissions cycle. What do we do? Process applications through Agents and Partners from all over the world and issue offers Liaise with Agents and Partners directly on individual applications during the admissions cycle. Collaborate with colleagues in the University Admissions Centre (UAC) to align processes and coordinate on production of CAS Process offers for Joint CAS LSI (Pre-sessional English) applicants Coordinate with colleagues in UAC, UoP Global Regional offices, and the UoP Global Recruitment team on reviewing international qualifications and equivalency mapping Liaise with sponsors and faculties to provide progress reports for Sponsored students Contact Information: Emma Scott-Ward - Global Admissions Team Leader Marion Forgeot - Global Admissions Administrator Alexandra Aspinall - Global Admissions Administrator Lorraine Fernandes - Global Admissions Administrator Ashley Wang - Global Admissions and Marketing Administrator Esther Fan - Global Admissions and Marketing Administrator Generic Email: globaladmissions@port.ac.uk Explore Global UniversityofPortsmouth Home Students Staff Contact and departments Home Staff services Departments Resources © University of Portsmouth Cookie policy Modern Slavery Statement Privacy policy Website terms and conditions

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Hybrid Working Human Resources Hybrid Working Support and Guidance for Staff and Managers The University has agreed to adopt a hybrid approach to working arrangements and the ability for staff in specific roles to be able to work both on campus and at home. The University recognises that some roles require 100% attendance on campus, and this guidance does not apply to those roles. Some roles, whilst partially student facing, may have elements of the role that can be delivered from home. Whether a role is to be worked fully on campus or on a hybrid working basis is determined by the business needs of the University, and will be a decision made by the Head of Department/Faculty Manager/Director of Professional Service. Hybrid working will be evolving as it is implemented over the 2021/22 academic year and it will be reviewed regularly as we learn together how to best work with our new arrangements. Guidance for staff can be found under My responsibilities. Guidance for managers can be found under Ensuring team health, safety and wellbeing. For information, the guidance document links are also linked below: Hybrid Working Guidance Hybrid Working Guidance for Managers Hybrid Working Flowchart Hybrid Working FAQs Explore HR HomepageMy RoleLeaving My RoleManaging My TeamHR Business Partnering TeamLearning and DevelopmentHR SystemsMy Rewards and BenefitsPensionsPayrollOccupational Health ServiceEquality and DiversityContact usStaff Surveys HR A-Z UniversityofPortsmouth Home Students Staff Contact and departments Home Staff services Departments Resources © University of Portsmouth Cookie policy Modern Slavery Statement Privacy policy Website t

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Recruitment, Screening and Hiring Human Resources Checking an Individual's Right to Work in the UK Right to Work Document Checks As an employer, we have a duty to prevent illegal working. The Immigration, Asylum and Nationality Act 2006 says that employers should conduct document checks which show that your potential employee has the right to work in the UK and is permitted to do the type of work you are offering. You are required to undertake the right to work check at least 5 days before the employment commences. This is to ensure a smooth transition and set up in our HR and IT systems, and to ensure the University remains compliant with Home Office Statutory requirements. You should not make assumptions about a person’s right to work in the UK or their immigration status on the basis of their colour, nationality, ethnic or national origins. The best way to ensure that you do not discriminate is to treat all job applicants fairly and make the same document checks.. The current UKVI checklist provides information and guidance about checking an individual's Right to Work in the UK. Guidance on employing Student Visa Sponsored Students If you will be employing students who are in the UK under Student visa Sponsorship, including Undergraduate, Postgraduate Taught and Postgraduate Research students. As part of the visa conditions, there are restrictions on the number of hours sponsored students can work. Sponsored students with restrictions undertaking variable hours/casual seasonal contracts can work up to 20 hours per week during term time. The following should be noted: Undergraduate students are restricted to working 20 hours per week

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > My Role Human Resources Employee Assistance Programme (EAP) Supporting the mental health and wellbeing of all our colleagues is incredibly important to us, and our new Employee Assistance Programme will help us do just that. Whether you’re facing problems at work or at home, you can access impartial, independent and entirely confidential advice from qualified counsellors for any issues you may be facing, such as Anxiety; Bereavement; Stress; Depression; Workplace Issues; Trauma; Relationships; Finances and debt; and Family Difficulties. Accessing the Helpline Confidential 24/7 Helpline: The telephone helpline is available 24/7, 365 days a year and is completely confidential and to anyone needing mental health support. To access the helpline, simply call 0800 023 9324 from any standard UK landline or mobile phone Digital Resources and Self-Help:A wide offering of resources is also available and includes downloadable self-help workbooks, debt advice and more. Visit the EAP Platform to find out more: www.port.ac.uk/myeap Is it confidential? The University does not know who uses the service unless the individual personally chooses to tell someone about their contact with Vivup. Vivup do provide statistics to the University to show how many employees use the service and the broad types of issues raised, for example; ‘relationship breakdown at home’ or ‘bullying and harassment in the workplace. No information is ever passed on which could potentially identify an individual. title: Recruitment, Marketing and Communications
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Recruitment, Marketing and Communications > Merchandise Recruitment, Marketing and Communications University Merchandise Staff are able to order branded University merchandise via the Students’ Union website to ensure brand quality and provide a more efficient way of ordering products. A full range of branded products can be found in the online shop and bespoke products (that are supported by a strong business case and approved by Marketing and Communications) can be ordered using an online order form. Here are some things to remember: Ordering in bulk may offer you better prices so be sure to think of your future needs to allow you to place larger orders for better value. If you are ordering for a specific event such as an Open Day, make sure to do this enough in advance before the event. The Union strives to keep all core range products in stock, but if you are placing a large order be aware you may need to allow up to three weeks for the stock to be delivered to the Union. Lead times and delivery options vary depending on what’s ordered, but you should leave around 3 working days for small standard orders, 3 weeks (15 working days) for standard orders of over 50 items and 8 weeks (40 working days) for bespoke orders to allow for quality checks and delivery. You can refer to the Merchandise Catalogue for more detailed lead times on standard items. Products that are in stock will have quicker lead times and can be collected from the Students’ Union shop. Design for bespoke products need to be approved by the Brand team through the online order form. The University of Portsmouth has strict quality and ethical standards to ensure merchandise appropriatel

body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Recruitment, Marketing and Communications > Social media Recruitment, Marketing and Communications Social Media Guidelines The purpose of the social media guidelines are: to support best practice to protect the University’s reputation to promote effective use of social media as part of the University's marketing strategy The guidelines apply to you if you’re a University of Portsmouth employee or student creating or contributing to any kind of social media which uses the University’s name or department therein. This includes all course, faculty, research and support service accounts. This encompasses Twitter, Facebook, LinkedIn, Instagram, YouTube and Snapchat channels that you use to represent the University for the purpose of information sharing and promotion. Other channels will be assessed as they are released and social media platforms which post-date the creation of these guidelines should be assumed as included between document updates. Students using social media should do so in line with the student code of conduct to not bring the University into disrepute, and are not covered therefore in this document as they are not representing the University directly. The University expects all who participate in social media representing the University of Portsmouth to understand and to follow these guidelines. Failure to do so could put the University’s reputation at risk and put your future participation in social media activity on the behalf of the University at risk. As new technologies and social networking tools emerge, these guidelines will evolve and it is your responsibility to check this information regularly if you contribute to social media a

The Staff Essentials website (staff.port.ac.uk) is managed by a different team and uses a different model of operation. The Digital Content team are not responsible for the staff website. Jump to: University websites Website content standards The role of the Digital Content team The role of devolved content/website editors Web management processes Additional requests Resources Positioning of the website A successful website is a tool that enables visitors to find and action information efficiently, with minimal stress. Minimising the stress of accessing and actioning information has many parts, including making sure that: content is findable - so users can reach the information content is understandable - so users know what to do content is actionable - so there are clear next steps for them to complete upon digesting the website information Good webpages are not created in isolation, so all “good” website content must also link to the goal or purpose of the overall website. University websites Our main University websites are divided by audience. Each website’s goal aligns with its audience. Most content will easily fit into one of these categories. title: Recruitment, Marketing and Communications
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Recruitment, Marketing and Communications > Institutional statements in response to conflict, crisis or natural disaster Recruitment, Marketing and Communications Institutional statements in response to conflict, crisis or natural disaster We are proud to welcome a global community of students and staff to the University of Portsmouth; our institution and our city is enriched by this diverse range of cultures, interests and beliefs. With this global outlook comes the responsibility to understand how events around the world can have an impact locally and to respond appropriately. Our first priority will always be to support and uphold the safety and security of our staff and student body and to communicate this at times of crisis. We provide a comprehensive set of well-being and support services that are designed to help at times of individual or collective need. Our challenge is in judging when it is appropriate to issue communication in response to troubling events, and what kind of response to take. To identify and respond to all crises is neither reasonable nor feasible. To respond to none overlooks our position as a global institution that cares about our staff and students. We are also mindful that, especially in times of conflict or political strife, taking a stance on an issue could do more harm than good by unintentionally creating division within our community. Furthermore, the university needs to respect the Charity Commission guidance on political activity and campaigning that applies to universities. External communication The University will not issue an institutional statement, or provide a public comment, on every global, national or loca

title: Vice-Chancellor's Office
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Vice-Chancellor's Office > Vice-Chancellor Vice-Chancellor's Office Graham joined the University of Portsmouth in September 2013. Previous to this he had held the sole Deputy Vice-Chancellor role at the University of Hertfordshire since 2008 and the role of Pro Vice-Chancellor (International) at Glasgow Caledonian University which he joined in 1993. He gained a BSc (1st class) in Environmental Engineering, an MSc, Mechanical Engineering and a PhD from the University of Strathclyde. He has a wealth of management experience and a strong entrepreneurial outlook which, combined with a substantial record of academic achievement and a thorough understanding of the international and HE sectors, puts him in an ideal position to lead the University through its next stage of development. Graham has responsibility for the strategic direction of the University working with the Board of Governors and the senior management team which comprises the Provost, Deputy Vice-Chancellor (Education), Deputy Vice-Chancellor (Global Engagement & Student Life), Deputy Vice-Chancellor (Research, Innovation & External Relations), Chief People Officer, Executive Director of Corporate Governance and Chief Financial Officer who all report directly to him. He is passionate about HE and its power to transform the life choices of individuals from a wide variety of backgrounds. He is committed to leading the University to provide the very best student experience underpinned by internationally excellent research and world class business engagement. His primary research interests are within the area of Building Physics and Material Science with emphasis on moisture within building componen

title: Vice-Chancellor's Office
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Vice-Chancellor's Office > Chancellor Vice-Chancellor's Office ‌ Our Chancellor, Karen Blackett OBE, is a former University of Portsmouth student A proven and experienced business leader, Karen Blackett has a record of accomplishment in creating vibrant cultures, energising teams, and consistently delivering business growth. Karen is known for her transformative work with large, multi-disciplined and diverse teams delivering consistent commercial success. She began her media career in 1993 at CIA MediaNetwork and was quickly head-hunted by The Media Business Group, where she received many promotions, starting in the Board of Directors in 1999 and moving to Chairwoman of MediaCom UK & Ireland in 2016. While at MediaCom, she created and launched an Apprenticeship Scheme for 18-24-year olds, one of her proudest achievements. Under Karen’s leadership as CEO, MediaCom was voted Agency of the Year by industry magazine Campaign for an unprecedented two years in a row, 2013 and 2014. She was appointed Chancellor of the University of Portsmouth in 2017. She featured among the Top 100 Great Black Britons in 2020 and, in 2021, was chosen to feature in the book The Female Lead Volume II: We Rise by Lifting Others. Karen mentors people across her industry and further afield, including clients, internal staff, other businesspeople and students. She takes pride in helping them and learns a great deal from the process herself. Karen drives inclusion as a key contributor to business success in all the organisations she works with. In her 5 years as CEO of MediaCom, ethnic diversity transformed from 11% to 19%. Women in senior management roles increased to 44%. In Septem

body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Vice-Chancellor's Office > Equality and Diversity Vice-Chancellor's Office Equality and Diversity This is the Equality and Diversity mini site. Visit our full website for more detailed information, help and guidance around equality and diversity at the University of Portsmouth. How do the Equality and Diversity Policy and other University of Portsmouth policies and procedures affect members of the University? The University of Portsmouth affirms its commitment to fair and equitable treatment for all its students, potential students and members of staff. It seeks to encourage, support and value diversity in the student population. It aims to create the conditions whereby all students and staff are treated on the basis of their merits, abilities and potential, regardless of gender, colour, ethnic or national origin, age, disability, religious or political beliefs, family circumstance or sexual orientation. It is firmly opposed to any form of unfair discrimination based on these attributes and values. Visit the various Equality and Diversity policies and guidelines for more information. To better understand your rights and responsibilities as a manager and/or member of staff use the University Diversity elearning resource. The University considers the effect of its policies and procedures. Find out about the University?s approach to Equality Impact Assessment. body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Vice-Chancellor's Office > Environmental Information Regulations 2004 Vice-Chancellor's Office Environmental Information Regulations 2004 The Environmental Information Regulations came into force on 1 January 2005 and provide a right of access to specific environmental information held by public authorities - the University included - which it is not possible to access under the Freedom of Information Act 2000. Some general environmental information is already available on the University's Publication Scheme under the Physical Resources classification. Requests for any other environmental information should be made to the University's Information Disclosure Officer who has 20 working days after the receipt of a request in which to provide the information. An extension to 40 working days may be possible though, if the complexity and volume of the information requested makes it impracticable to provide the information earlier. Contact the University's Information Disclosure Officer. Definition of Environmental Information: Specifically, the type of environmental information available under these Regulations falls into the following categories: the state of the elements of the environment such as air, water, soil emissions and discharges such as noise, energy and radiation measures and activities (e.g. policies) that affect or are likely to affect the state of the elements of the environment reports, cost-benefits and economic analyses used within the policies that might affect the state of the elements of the environment the state of human health & safety, including the contamination of the food chain the state of cultural sites and built structures. Register Now! Just register online at www.port.ac.uk/loyalty, you can then present your student/staff card to a loyalty scanner at any of the Foodon Campus tills (excluding the Waterhole), and collect points against your purchases. You will collect five points for every £1 you spend, and each point will be worth 1p which you can redeem against your future purchases. The scheme will also provide students and staff the chance of collecting double points, seasonal bonus points, and if you buy any hot drink you can get your ninth one for free! View Food on Campus Loyalty (.pdf). If you have any queries or questions, please see our frequently asked questions (.pdf) or email loyalty@port.ac.uk. body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Catering Services > One Water Catering Services One Water Did you know that 1 billion people in the world don’t have access to clean water? Or that 2 million people die each year as a result of water related diseases? When we found this out we wanted to do something to change those figures. We recognise that it's a massive challenge, but we can change the lives of people – one person, one day at a time. Simply buy a bottle of One Water – all profits, every last drop, go to building unique PlayPump™ water pumping systems overseas which will improve people’s lives by providing free, clean water. With your help it can make a difference. Want to see more? You can! Follow this link to see videos of people's efforts in action Want to do more? You can! Support our just for fun "One Water in the World" competition. All you have to do is: Get yourself a bottle of One Water Go on holiday and take a picture of yourself with One Water somewhere interesting in the world Share your photo by posting it on our competition photo centre on Facebook It's as simple as that! Or just visit the One Water photo centre and give us your inspirational thoughts on how else to promote this excellent project. body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Estates and Campus Services > Soft Services (Campus Environment) Estates and Campus Services Jump to: Latest car parking closures What you need to know about parking Parking charges How the system works FAQs Our car parks Car parking policy Latest car park disruptions Occasionally, we may need to temporarily close car parks, either for essential maintenance or for ground works related to the Estates Masterplan. We'll list upcoming closures here, and communicate this in the weekly Staff Newsletter. Disabled bays in Eldon relocated From Friday 27 March, the 3 disabled bays parallel with St. James’s Road within Eldon car park will be relocated adjacent to the current plantroom. The bays along the far perimeter edge will be occupied by the contractor relocating the plantroom. Read more. What You Need to Know About Parking The University's parking system is managed through an online permit system and ANPR enforcement. Here’s what staff need to know: Permit Renewal: Staff must renew their permit annually. Reminders will be sent via the CPP Permits system. Applying & Managing Permits: Staff can apply for or amend their permit details through the CPP Permits portal: CPP Permits System. Payment Setup: Staff must register on Evology to set up their payment method for the ANPR Pay As You Go system: Register on Evology. This site also provides service details and terms & conditions. Vehicle Registration: You can register up to two vehicles on your permit and Evology account for University car parks. Updating Vehicle Details: If you change your vehicle, you must update your permit and Evology accounts to avoid receiving a Parking Charge Notice (PCN). Update your CPP

body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Estates and Campus Services > Soft Services (Campus Environment) Estates and Campus Services Our Commitment to the Environment We're committed to continuously improving our environmental impact by reducing the amount of waste sent to landfill. Since 2011, we've increased our recycling rate from 40% to 70%, and we're aiming for better—with your help! Waste Collection We’re reviewing how waste is collected across university buildings to make recycling: Simpler Clearer More sustainable Key Improvements we’re working on: Fewer bins – smarter layout Fewer plastic bin liners Clear signage to reduce confusion and contamination How to Sort Your Waste Recycling Bins – What Can Go In? Paper (including plain, printed, magazines) Cardboard (flat packed) Envelopes (no plastic windows) Plastic bottles, pots, tubs, trays (clean and empty) Glass bottles and jars Cans (e.g. drinks cans) Tetrapaks (note: check signage for updates) Do not put in: Food, liquids, tissues, or coffee cups Food Waste Bins – What’s Accepted? Fruit and veg Leftovers Coffee grounds Compostable tea bags Meat, fish, and bones Egg shells Don’t have a food bin nearby? Let us know at sustainability@port.ac.uk General Waste – What Belongs Here? Coffee cups (these can’t be recycled!) Polystyrene Tissues and contaminated packaging Plastic bags Crisp/sweet wrappers Blister packs (e.g. paracetamol) Hard plastics (e.g. spray nozzles) Sandwich packets (e.g. supermarket meal deals) Special Waste Items – What Goes Where? Item What to Do Aerosols, Chemicals, Paint Tins Email: estates.helpdesk@port.ac.uk Batteries (domestic) Battery buckets in office receptions/caretaker areas Batteries (mobile/non-domestic) Ema

title: Estates and Campus Services
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Estates and Campus Services > Soft Services (Campus Environment) Estates and Campus Services Lost/Found Property When items are found on campus they should be transferred to the nearest Lost Property Point within the University. On receiving lost property, staff at the below points will be required to log items onto the shared lost property spreadsheet on Google Drive. This must be done at the time of the item being handed in. Student cards Should be sent to Myport in Liongate. Myport should contact the student to let them know where their card can be collected. Staff cards The staff member should be contacted and asked to collect their card from where it is being held. The card should be destroyed if not claimed within 1 month. Contractor & white cards Should be sent to Adrian Partington in Milldam LC who will endeavour to find out who they belong to so they can be reunited with their owners. The cards will be destroyed if not claimed within 1 month. Bank cards, chequebooksand items containingthese like purses orwallets Should be logged onto the spreadsheet. If the owner can be identified then they should be contacted and asked to retrieve their item. Items containing personal information like bank cards, etc to be destroyed if not claimed within 30 days. Everything else to be given to charity if not claimed within 30 days. Library books Should be returned to the Library. Passports If unclaimed after 1 month should be sent to the Passport Office (HM Passport Office, Globe House , 89 Eccleston Square , London, SW1V 1PN) or the relevant embassy. UK driving licences If unclaimed after 1 month should be sent back to Drivers Customer Services Correspondence

title: Equity, Diversity & Inclusion
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Equity, Diversity & Inclusion > EDI in Action Equity, Diversity & Inclusion As part of our commitment to equality and diversity, we have signed up to two national Higher Education equality charters – Athena SWAN, which focuses on gender equality and the Race Equality Charter. Run by Advance HE, the charters enable us to apply for awards recognising our commitment to, and progress on, equality and diversity. They aim to drive forward the cultural and systemic changes we need to remain competitive and attractive to talented staff and potential students in a global market. They also support our University values and strategies. More information about our approach and progress can be found on the Race Equality Charter page. body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Equity, Diversity & Inclusion > EDI in Action Equity, Diversity & Inclusion Fire Alarm Digital Messaging Service Information In our drive to continually improve our Equality, Diversity and Inclusion, Estates and Campus Services have launched a campus wide fire alarm digital messaging service. The Digital Messaging Service (DMS) is a fire alarm warning system which alerts people who are deaf or have hearing loss when the fire alarm sounds. The system uses text messages, Whatsapp messages, phone calls and push notifications to relay the fire alarm signal to the mobile phone user. Example Message On the activation of the building's fire alarm, notifications will be sent to your mobile phone immediately, in the following format. The system will ensure that anyone who has difficulty in hearing the existing fire alarms or works in an area without a beacon can be notified by an alternative method. However, anybody can use the system as an alternative method of fire alarm alert. How to sign-up for Digital Messaging Service? There are three methods for registering to the service: 1. Send a text message with the relevant building code to the DMS number 07480 486966. *Recommendation: Save 07480 486966 as ‘Fire alarm’ in your contacts so you recognise the number in an emergency. 2. Download the free Fireco Insite app for both Android and IOS devices which gives you instant access to your user preferences, without having to use the hyperlink in your confirmation message. 3. Scan the QR code on the DMS signage which will be located in building entrances. For further details, please see the Fireco DMS installation and user guide. Any questions should be directed to th

title: Equity, Diversity & Inclusion
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Equity, Diversity & Inclusion > EDI in Action Equity, Diversity & Inclusion University EDI Consultative Group & Steering Group The University’s EDI Team, in consultation with the EDI Steering Group and the EDI Consultative Group, is responsible for developing policy and best practice in order to support meeting the legal requirements. We commit to sharing the EDI Framework objectives with all new students and colleagues when they arrive at Portsmouth, and to refresh this as required, to underpin our desire to give students and staff the best possible experience whilst working and studying here. The EDI Consultative Group is made of individuals with useful opinions in EDI matters, meanwhile, the EDI Steering Group consists of those at the University with the authority and power to enact changes required to make necessary changes to meet our EDI objectives. For any further questions or queries please don't hesitate to contact us at equality@port.ac.uk. body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Equity, Diversity & Inclusion > EDI in Action Equity, Diversity & Inclusion As an international and widening participation University, the University of Portsmouth is committed to ensuring that our diverse student body experience equitable student experience and outcomes. As part of Global Week, an annual event that celebrates the diversity of our staff and students, Academic Development is running an EDI mini-fest on Wednesday 13th March 2024. Global Week is a prime opportunity for colleagues to showcase best practice strategies and research on equity, culturally responsive pedagogies, and the wider challenges impacting student engagement through a global lens. We particularly welcome submissions from international colleagues and students, and submissions that offer practical applications in the learning space. Call for Papers To contribute a case study, research output or discussion piece for this online event, please see the link below to submit an abstract to present a 20 minute session (with 10 minute Q&A). title: Equity, Diversity & Inclusion
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Equity, Diversity & Inclusion > EDI in Action Equity, Diversity & Inclusion Sense of Belonging & Representation Paper This paper looks at University of Portsmouth students’ perceptions of culture, representation, and barriers to success in higher education (HE) for People of the Global Majority (PGM). Our students face many challenges whilst at university, and Students of the Global Majority (also referred to as BAME or Black, Asian and minority ethnic) have experiences specific to their culture and ethnicity. Students of the Global Majority find that their experiences are impacted by the level of representation and belonging in their university community. This includes: Their broad university experiences, both inside and outside of the classroom and in their university city; The diversity and representation of the curriculum; Student success metrics such as awards, continuation, and progression; Approaches to learning and teaching. This paper focuses specifically on representation and sense of belonging for Students of the Global Majority at the University of Portsmouth and shares the results of a survey of 441 students, framed by Critical Race Theory (CRT). The paper is part of a larger research project that covers representation of culture in the curriculum; cultural sensitivity in learning environments; the impact of culture and student backgrounds on higher education study; experiences of different teaching methods; and prior educational experiences. Data is available for all of these sections, and has been shared with Associate Deans. All colleagues are welcome to read the paper. If you have any questions about this research, please contact fiona.

title: Equity, Diversity & Inclusion
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Equity, Diversity & Inclusion > EDI in Action Equity, Diversity & Inclusion SPICE up your events Inclusion in events may seem like a no-brainer, but there are always improvements we can make to improve the environments and spaces of our events to invite a broader range of audiences and ideas. Below you’ll find five easily actionable ideas to implement when making events and activities of your own. Just remember to add some SPICE to your events! S: Spaces – Check space accessibility and suitability. The University has many resources available to us that allow us to ensure spaces we’re hosting events in are as accessible as possible, and to check for this easily. Two that the Equality, Diversity & Inclusion (EDI) team likes to use include the UoP Room Check tool; and the AccessAble Access Guides. Across these tools, you can get room pictures, details of building access, a view of seating facilities and other general room details. However, if possible, we recommend visiting venues before events to ensure they suit all of your intended audience. Make sure to also consider digital spaces. For example, ensure that there is a live-captioning function and that attendees can spotlight speakers, i.e. for lip reading. Also, you should ensure that meeting materials are in screen-reader friendly fonts. P: Partnerships – Pick partner organisations that align with our values. The University has three values that underpin everything we do. We are ambitious, responsible, and open. As such, when organising events with outside collaborators, try to remember these values and pick partners that are compatible with our values. I: Inclusion – Diversify the event organisers. S
