title: Academic Registry
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Academic Registry > Assessment Regulations Academic Registry Assessment Regulations Share | Thu, 22 Aug 2024 12:46:00 BST Introduction to Assessment Regulations Welcome to the Academic Registry’s Assessment Regulations pages. This section is specifically designed to assist our staff and collaborative partners in navigating and applying the University’s Assessment Regulations. Here, you will find the comprehensive University Assessment Regulations and accompanying documents. These regulations are categorised into three main groups, each tailored to different aspects of our academic offerings: 1. Undergraduate and Postgraduate Taught Courses: A unified set of regulations governs all taught courses up to and including Level 7. These ensure consistency and clarity across the University’s undergraduate and postgraduate courses. 2. Partner-Specific Assessment Regulations: These are specialised regulations designed for specific collaborative courses and partnerships, recognising that some partners have unique needs for certain courses. 3. Research Programmes: Tailored specifically for research-based programmes, these regulations guide the research journey and ensure that academic standards are upheld throughout the process leading to research degrees. Student-Centric Assessment Regulations Our Assessment Regulations are designed to support the student experience and promote academic success for all students equally while preserving academic integrity and safeguarding academic standards. Written in clear, accessible language these regulations are structured to be easily navigable, with direct links to additional resources and support. While students have access

title: Academic Registry
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Academic Registry Academic Registry About Us and Who to Contact Welcome to the Academic Registry! The Academic Registry plays a central role in our institution’s commitment to maintaining academic excellence. Our role is to develop and support an academic quality regulatory landscape which supports the highest standards of academic quality, through academic regulations and policies that support a fair, transparent, and effective learning environment. Our Mission Our Mission is to ensure the University’s academic quality environment complies with mandatory regulatory requirements, upholds academic standards, enhances the student experience, and supports all students, regardless of their background, to succeed. Our Values Academic Excellence: Promoting the highest standards of education and quality of student experience Integrity: Ensuring fairness, transparency, and accountability in all our actions. Collaboration: Partnering with students, staff and external stakeholders to uphold and improve academic quality. Responsiveness: Adapting to the changing landscape of higher education through innovation and foresight. Our Objectives Working collaboratively across the University, and informed by sector leading-practice we will: Ensure all academic policies, regulations and procedures meet mandatory regulatory requirements whilst being efficient and maintaining a forward-thinking approach. Provide an assessment regulatory framework which supports student achievement, and eliminates unwarranted barriers to learning and success; Create a student and staff-centric academic quality environment that promotes high standards and continuous improvement; Generate and a

title: Academic Registry
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Academic Registry > Course Lifecycle Academic Registry Portfolio Approval Overview The formal approval of award bearing academic provision and collaborative partnership provision is an essential part of the University’s quality assurance framework which links to the University’s strategy. Course and partnership approval are underpinned by a structured strategic portfolio approval cycle, informed by strategic market research and employer insight into the skills needed for graduate roles. The process is designed to be strategic, robust, supportive, inclusive and flexible. The overall structure of the process revolves around three distinct gateways, serving as pivotal points to guide and streamline the approval process. These are: Strategic Approval Gateway Resources Approval Gateway Curriculum Approval Gateway Process Strategic Approval Gateway The Strategic Approval Gateway comprises of two key elements: strategic consideration and planning of the University-wide academic portfolio, and the approval of individual courses and collaborative partnerships. Both these elements are conducted through the University Portfolio Approval Group (UPAG) who considers proposals on a monthly basis. By granting strategic approval, UPAG ensures that each course and partnership proposal contributes to the broader goals of the University. Please see the Submission Deadlines and UPAG Meeting Dates 2025/26 and Recruitment and Marketing UPAG timescales Marketing Insights Report UPAG considers the University Marketing Insights Report on an annual basis. The report is prepared by the Marketing and Communication department and is reviewed by UPAG in the spring, with an update pro

body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Academic Registry > Course Lifecycle Academic Registry Course Modifications and Closures It is expected that courses will develop and change during their lifecycle and for some of these changes they need to be approved by the University Portfolio Approval Group (UPAG). Changes to course structure and module modifications are managed via the Faculties Key Activities and Business Processes. Approval by UPAG is required for the following changes: Change of title Change of start date for proposed courses Change of intakes Change of pathways and/or exit awards Change to placement year Change of mode of study Change of duration Addition of new location for a collaborative partner course Closure Schools must be aware of the impact of the timing and type of changes for both applicants and students, who must be consulted or informed as appropriate with adequate notice. This is in accordance with the expectations of the Competition and Markets Authority (CMA) Consumer Law Advice for Providers and Students. Please ensure you take the required action detailed in the Course and Module Changes and Student Consultation document. If you are suspending recruitment to a course due to either the course being full or pending closure, a suspension must be approved by Planning in advance of approval by UPAG. Please ensure you follow the Course and Recruitment Suspension process. The proposal and relevant supporting documentation must be sent to the Faculty Quality Officer for submission to UPAG. If the proposal is approved Academic Registry will liaise with Student Systems and the webteam to make the required changes on SITS and the course web pages. It is the responsibility

To request a course exemption please complete the Request for Exemption form below. If the request is for an exemption from the academic calendar, please also complete the Academic Year Calendar template below, setting out the key dates of teaching, assessment, examination board, referral boards, and graduation. This form must be submitted with the Request for Exemption form. Please refer to the Academic Year Dates webpage for key academic year dates and activities. title: Academic Services
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Academic Services > Faculties Administration Academic Services What does CCPD do? The Centre for Continuing Professional Development (CCPD) offers short course support to the University. Part of the School of Education, Languages and Linguistics (SELL), and staffed by a mix of AS and SELL colleagues, the Centre provides administrative, logistical, project and operational support for all departments across the University in the delivery of non-standard provision and short courses. The CCPD is responsible for: Summer Schools for students Continuing Professional Development programmes for career professionals Non-standard provision support Pre-Sessional English In-Sessional English Subject Knowledge Enhancement for teacher training Pre-induction Postgraduate programmes CPD workshops, events and conferences Visiting Scholars for FHSS Information about the CCPD and the courses on offer can be found here and the Team can be contacted either via email - ccpd@port.ac.uk or telephone - 02392 846 190 Explore Faculties Administration Centre for Continuous Professional Development Enquiry Centre Cross Faculty Support Office Central AdministrationStudent Systems UniversityofPortsmouth Home Students Staff Contact and departments Home Staff services Departments Resources © University of Portsmouth Cookie policy Modern Slavery Statement Privacy policy Website terms and conditions

title: Academic Services
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Academic Services > Faculties Administration Academic Services What does the Enquiry Centre do? The Enquiry Centre offers a triage service for initial enquiries on behalf of a number of teams at the University, including the University Admissions Centre (UAC), Global Admissions, Academic Services and the University’s Income team particularly, but no enquiry goes unanswered or undirected. Enquiries are managed through a mixture of phone calls, emails and live chat functions. The Enquiries Centre also assists with various administrative tasks in support of other departments throughout the year. Contact us: 02392 844444 Explore Faculties Administration Centre for Continuous Professional Development Enquiry Centre Cross Faculty Support Office Central AdministrationStudent Systems UniversityofPortsmouth Home Students Staff Contact and departments Home Staff services Departments Resources © University of Portsmouth Cookie policy Modern Slavery Statement Privacy policy Website terms and conditions

title: Academic Services
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Academic Services > Faculties Administration Academic Services What does the Cross Faculty Support Office (CFSO) do? The Team is responsible for all partner liaison and course administration of specific partners and courses, managing the relationship with all stakeholders. The courses administered by this Team span the five Faculties and they are working with CEG to deliver Portsmouth Online. CFSO operates a business to business model and therefore does not have a student facing reception. The CFSO Team is responsible for administering the student journey from Registration through to Graduation, and includes assessment and examination boards. These Boards are often held at the location of the Partner institution. The Team can be contacted via telephone - 02392 8366975 or email - cfso@port.ac.uk For further information, please use this link. title: Academic Services
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Academic Services > Central Administration Academic Services What do the Fees and Funding Team do? The Fees and Funding Team are responsible for the setting and invoicing of tuition fees and supporting and allocating financial support. This includes the production of tuition fee schedules, raising tuition fee invoices, confirming registration and attendance to the Student Loan Company to ensure students receive their maintenance loan payments and the University receives tuition fee loan payments. They also administer other funding such as American and Canadian Loans, Scottish Awards Agency and Channel Islands funding. The Team allocate tuition fee discounts, bursaries and scholarships, ensuring students receive the funding they are entitled to. The Fees and Funding Team can be contacted by emailing feeenquiries@port.ac.uk or by using Hornbill Useful Links Undergraduate Fees and Student Finance Useful external and Government websites – Direct Gov – Student Finance US Federal Loans Professional and Career Development Loans Portsmouth City Council Student Loans Company Student Finance England Student Finance Wales Student Finance Northern Ireland Student Awards Agency Scotland Explore Faculties AdministrationCentral Administration Fees and Funding Registration Awards, Ceremonies and Collaborative Partnerships Research Degrees Student Systems UniversityofPortsmouth Home Students Staff Contact and departments Home Staff services Departments Resources © University of Portsmouth Cookie policy Modern Slavery Statement Privacy policy Website terms and conditions

title: Academic Services
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Academic Services > Central Administration Academic Services What does the Registration Team do? The Registration Team is responsible for the registration of all students to the University, in addition, they monitor their continued status at the University. This includes the online registration of all new and continuing students, in person and online ID checks and the distribution of Biometric Residence Permits (BRP) to international students. They process a student’s change in circumstances once approved by the relevant Faculty i.e. transfer, interruption and withdrawal and inform the appropriate regulatory body, such as the UKVI or Student Loan Company (SLC) of the change in the student’s status at the University. Students who hold a student visa or receive funding from a third party, are continuously monitored to ensure they are meeting the conditions of their visa or funding, and report appropriately. The Team can be contacted by emailing registration@port.ac.uk Useful Link UKVI Student Route Compliance Policy Explore Faculties AdministrationCentral Administration Fees and Funding Registration Awards, Ceremonies and Collaborative Partnerships Research Degrees Student Systems UniversityofPortsmouth Home Students Staff Contact and departments Home Staff services Departments Resources © University of Portsmouth Cookie policy Modern Slavery Statement Privacy policy Website terms and conditions

The Team is responsible for planning and delivering a high quality Graduation service, held at the Guildhall, for the University and for the issuing of award documentation and transcripts. The Team is also responsible for supporting the University’s UK and Transnational collaborative partners and providing effective administration. The Team can be contacted via telephone - 02392 844444 or the following email addresses - for graduation queries - graduation@port.ac.uk for qualification/study confirmation - academicverifications@port.ac.uk for a replacement certificate or transcript - parchments@port.ac.uk Useful Links Awards and Certificates Graduation Replacement Certificate/Transcript Explore Faculties AdministrationCentral Administration Fees and Funding Registration Awards, Ceremonies and Collaborative Partnerships Research Degrees Student Systems UniversityofPortsmouth Home Students Staff Contact and departments Home Staff services Departments Resources © University of Portsmouth Cookie policy Modern Slavery Statement Privacy policy Website terms and conditions

title: Academic Services
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Academic Services > Central Administration Academic Services What do the Research Degrees Team do? The Research Degrees Team are responsible for the administration relating to all research students studying PhD, MPhil, PhD by Publication and Professional Doctorates (research phase) within the University of Portsmouth. Our Team works closely with all Faculties and the Graduate School to ensure student progress reviews and examinations are undertaken in line with University regulations. We are also responsible for processing changes of status for students, including interruptions to study, extensions and amendments to the Supervisory Team. We also advise and support the Academic staff within Research and sit on the Graduate School Management Board and the Faculty Research Degree Committees. For more information on our work and for links to the relevant regulations please visit our web pages or contact us at researchdegrees@port.ac.uk. The Central Timetabling Unit is responsible for the planning, organising and production of teaching, examination (first and second attempt assessment periods) and Induction Week timetables. Full information about teaching rooms at the University is available on the UoP Teaching Rooms site. They also coordinate timetabling issues, end user training and CMIS user support. title: Corporate Governance
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Corporate Governance > Data Protection Corporate Governance The General Data Protection Regulation ‌The Legislation The General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) came into effect on 25th May 2018. The GDPR, which has many similarities to the Data Protection Act 1998 which it replaced, is designed to improve transparency in data processing and to protect the rights and freedoms of individuals, in particular their right to privacy with respect to the processing of their personal data. The GDPR is technologically neutral and therefore applies to personal and special category data in whichever form it is held, for example, in electronic or paper documents. The following sections include information on some of the changes in the GDPR: Definitions used in the GDPR The Principles relating to processing The Rights of the Data Subject When are we allowed to process personal data? What is Privacy by Design? The importance of dealing with data breaches under the GDPR For the full text of the General Data Protection Regulation, go to the following link. Definitions used in the GDPR Personal Data Personal data is any information that can identify a living person either by itself e.g. a person's name, or when linked with other information e.g. an address plus a name, and includes unique reference numbers, location data and online identifiers. Special Category Data The GDPR defines some personal data as special category data. Special category data must be processed more securely with respect to the purposes for which it is collected and who will have access to that information, than other data collected. Special category data is defined in the legislation as data refe

title: Corporate Governance
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Corporate Governance > Data Protection Corporate Governance Subject Access Requests ‌The General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) gives individuals the right to request (and receive) a permanent copy of any personal data that an organisation – in this case, the University – holds about that individual. This is called a Subject Access Request. If you are considering making a Subject Access Request to the University, you may find it helpful to read the following information before completing the Subject Access Request form. Subject Access Request Process Do you need to make a Subject Access Request? If you only want information about a specific incident you may find that you can get that information by asking for it directly from the department holding it, without having to go through the Subject Access Request procedure. It is possible that the department may have to check whether the data can be released, but that shouldn’t take too long. If the department is happy to release your data to you in this way, it will be a much shorter and less formal process than the Subject Access Request process. However, if you want information from a number of areas of the University the best way is still to use the Subject Access Request. How to make a request All Subject Access Requests must be made in writing to the University. The University provides a form for such requests that is designed to collect the information needed to identify the data you are requesting. Download and complete the form and then send it, with the appropriate identification documents, either to the postal address given in the link above, or by email to samantha.hill@port.ac.uk. You may wish

Special category data Use of your personal data Disclosure of your personal data Your rights as a data subject How long will the University keep your personal data? Your responsibilities as a data subject Much of the information on this page relates to all data subjects. Where there is specific information just for UoP students, or just for UoP staff, this is clearly highlighted. What personal data do we process and why? Personal data may be kept in both paper and electronic formats but it is kept securely in all cases, either in locked cabinets or avvess controlled in the case of electronic records. Students only... The University needs to process (that is, collect, use, store and ultimately destroy) personal information about you as a student to be able to record your application to the University, your progress through your course, to classify your final award(s) and to maintain contact as an alumni of the University. The University is also obliged in some cases to collect information in line with legal requirements, for instance the University has to provide details of your attendance to the Higher Education Funding Council for England (HEFCE). The personal data the University collects from you at registration includes: Your name Contact details (address, e-mail, telephone numbers) Emergency contact details Date of birth Country of nationality and of birth Academic qualifications Details of any disability Details of any criminal convictions Fee information and sponsorship details During your time at University we wi

body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Corporate Governance > Data Protection Corporate Governance Staff as Data Processors As a member of the University staff it is quite possible that you will be handling personal data relating to other people - whether that data relates to students or staff. The University's Data Protection policy sets out the University's responsibilities under the DPA and encompasses the responsibilities of staff. Your responsibilities Disclosure of information Special category data Retention of personal data Your Responsibilities It is your responsibility to treat personal data confidentially. You must ensure that computer records are password protected and papers locked away when not in use. You must not disclose a student or staff member's personal data to any one without that person's consent, except in the instances mentioned below. All members of staff must be aware of the data protection principles and must adhere to them whenever processing data. Importantly, you must not look at any more data than you need to, to carry out your work. Failure to follow the Data Protection policy can result in a breach of the GDPR and may result in disciplinary proceedings within the University. Disclosure of Information Members of staff - and particularly those dealing with students - will often receive enquiries from third parties asking for information. You should not give out information to anyone, or confirm any information given to you by the third party, except in the following instances: to state what qualification a graduate has received (this information is already in the public domain through the graduation programme and so can be confirmed) where a student or member o

body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Corporate Governance > Data Protection Corporate Governance Frequently Asked Questions for the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) These FAQs have been produced in response to the information we believe it is helpful for staff and students to know, and in response to questions already asked. These FAQs will be added to as and when more information becomes available, or when staff ask more questions. 1. What is the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR)? The General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) is a new regulation from the European Union dealing with the processing of personal data and the free movement of that data. The GDPR repeals the directive on which the current data protection legislation is based (Directive 95/46/EC) and therefore will repeal the Data Protection Act 1998 itself. As an EU Regulation, this piece of legislation is implemented directly into UK Law. 2. When does it come into effect? The Regulation came into effect on 25 May 2018, as did the new Data Protection Act 2018 (see FAQ 4 for more detail). 3. Will we still have this Regulation after the UK leaves the EU? We will still keep this regulation once the UK leaves the EU for two reasons: i) this Regulation comes into effect before we leave the EU so we need to implement it for the time we are in the EU, and ii) when the UK is no longer part of the EU it will be necessary for the UK to prove that our standards for processing personal data are at least as good as those throughout the EU, for the remaining countries to be able to transfer data to the UK, without having to meet any further legal obligations. One way of proving the UK data processing standards is to retain t

The value of well managed, accurate, timely and secure information can be overlooked, but it underpins so many of the services we provide and activities we undertake. Often, just a small improvement in the way information is treated can have a significant, positive effect on a student's experience of the University, or on the working lives of ourselves and colleagues. For more information, visit our Information Governance Homepage. If you require a new shared drive, please complete this form. If you have any questions about clearing out your Google storage, we are still able to offer: Face to face meetings A guide containing hints and tips on clearing Google and a list of quick win emails Retention Schedule advice A walk through of the digital tools available in Google and Windows Explorer If you would like more information on any of the above, please contact us at recordsmanagement@port.ac.uk Please remember, LIS also offer guidance for clearing out your Google accounts and their page can be found here. body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Corporate Governance > Records Management Corporate Governance Electronic Records Management - eRecords Electronic Records Management is the process of managing the storage, organisation, access and retention of records using computer based systems. To support this process University of Portsmouth is currently using the eRecords system provided by Objective. It consists of two main components: a web client used by the majority of users and a desktop client called Navigator, both of which allow access to the eRecords system itself. eRecords provides the University with a structured, auditable method of managing its electronic records and ensuring we are using our electronic storage space in the most effective way. Who has an eRecords account? Prior to July 2025 all permanent and fixed-term contract staff were provided with an eRecords account as part of their overall University account profile. From July 2025 onwards, eRecords accounts are now only created upon request to the LIS Service Desk. Line managers can request an account for new starters prior to the joining date, so that it is available from the day they join the University. New and existing staff members requiring access can also request an account by contacting LIS Service Desk directly. eRecords access levels Read-only access - provided by default upon eRecords account creation Edit access - this can be provided upon successful completion of eRecords online training. Super User access - this level of access is provided to staff who require to undertake additional responsibilities and/or tasks within the system. This access is only provided after prior discussion with the eRecords team. How d

body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Corporate Governance > Records Management Corporate Governance ‌Departments that need to retain physical records, but do not have sufficient storage space, can transfer them off-site to ReStore Records Management (aka FileStore). FileStore is based in Southampton and provides a range of services, including: secure and confidential storage retrieval and delivery service confidential destruction For more information about using FileStore, please contact Sarah Arnold (University Records Manager) via recordsmanagement@port.ac.uk. Key Account Contacts Every University of Portsmouth account with FileStore has a designated key account contact. That person is responsible for: informing the University Records Manager when staff who have access to their department's FileStore account leave, or when new staff join who will need access disseminating FileStore-related communications from the University Records Manager to their colleagues as appropriate keeping account details (such as collection/delivery address(es) and log-ins) up to date with the University Records Manager keeping their account's holdings well managed and GDPR compliant To request that a new member of staff be given access to one of UoP's FileStore accounts, the key contact must email recordsmanagement@port.ac.uk with the person's name and UoP email address. If the account has more than one collection/delivery address associated with it, the preferred address for the new member of staff should also be stated. body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Corporate Governance > Records Management Corporate Governance ‌‌Weeding Starter Pack This is an intranet only page aimed at staff planning a local weeding exercise in the near future. If you require assistance with this, please contact recordsmanagement@port.ac.uk From March 2020 guidance links on this page will now take you into the eRecords system. If you do not yet have an eRecords account, this can be requested from IS Service Desk, ext-7777. Why weed? Click here to read our paper on the benefits of weeding your files. Paper or electronic? Most of this starter pack relates to the weeding of paper files. However, the logic and principles also apply to weeding your K:drive, N:drive and other electronic storage locations, including email. Your weeding project - step by step Step 1 - Define your scope Try to get a rough idea of the problem. What volume of files do you have? What type(s) of files are they? Where are they currently stored? Not only will this help you estimate how long the weeding will take and what resources you will need; but it can also help you to predict savings and benefits. A template for scoping is available from recordsmanagement@port.ac.uk Take note of lessons learnt from other teams who've already carried out a weeding project. A list of lessons learnt is available in the eRecords system and can be accessed here. Step 2 - Get support and set a date You will probably need assistance from your colleagues to carry out the weeding. Although it is good for everyone to lend a hand, weeding files does require a specific mind set and some staff may find the disposal of any documentation a difficult and stressful task. Be mindful of thi

title: Corporate Governance
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Corporate Governance > Records Management Corporate Governance ‌Hints and tips for managing email using Google Google no longer provides limitless storage for emails. Staff still have a legal responsibility to manage their emails as efficiently and effectively as any other University records. Some emails will need to be retained for a specific length of time, whilst others containing personal data must be kept only so long as they are necessary. However, we all receive vast numbers of email messages every day. Finding time to sort through and manage these emails is often difficult and, left untackled, the problem only grows. This page aims to give staff hints and tips on how they might harness the functionality of Google Apps Mail to help manage their mailboxes and save themselves time in doing so. Ultimately the management of a staff member’s mailbox is the responsibility of the individual. This page is designed to be read in conjunction with the Staff Email Policy (available in the Document Warehouse), Records Management Factsheet 4: Managing Email and Google’s own user guides (http://learn.googleapps.com/). Training in the use of Google Apps Mail is stongly recommended and available from the University’s IT Training team. The Records Management team also offer webinars to help you sort your email and keep UoP within the Google storage cap. If you have a personal Gmail account, all these hints and tips can be used to manage your personal email as well. 1 Start Again… If your email has been unmanaged for some time, the task of sorting all your existing mail may seem too much to contemplate. Sometimes it is simply easier to start again. Create a Label c

body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Corporate Governance > Records Management Corporate Governance Frequently Asked Questions ‌‌Everything you ever wanted to know about records management...! General FAQs Record Storage Weeding Filing & Retrieval Personal Info Disclosure FAQs Record Creation Retention/Disposal Scanning Email FAQs General FAQs Q: Why are records important? A: Without records the University would not be able to conduct business, to account for what has happened in the past, and to make decisions about the future. The University's records are a vital, corporate asset, and are required: to provide evidence of actions and decisions; to support accountability and transparency; to comply with legal and regulatory obligations, including employment, contract and financial law, as well as Data Protection legislation and the Freedom of Information Act; to support decision making; to protect the interests of staff, students and other stakeholders. Q: What does records management involve? A: Records management involves controlling all records (e.g. paper files, electronic records, emails) throughout their lifecycle from their creation or receipt until the time of their disposal. The controls should cover every aspect of record-keeping, including classification, access, storage, retention and disposal. For a summary of the main elements of records management, see Records Management Factsheet 1: Introduction Q: How will records management help me? A: Records management will make it easier for you to carry out your work because you will be able to find the information you need whenever you need it. It will also: Save time by ensuring your records can be found easily and quickly; Save spa

body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Estates and Campus Services > General information Estates and Campus Services Rechargeable Works Historically, when rechargeable works have been required direct contact has been made with any one of a number of the Estates Department staff. However, in an effort to provide a more efficient service please lodge initial requests with the helpdesk on ext: 6677 or any of the other methods outlined on the Repairs and Maintenance pages. This will then enable the appropriate member of staff to be allocated the job. On most occasions the relevant member of staff will contact you to obtain further and more detailed information. For minor jobs such as putting up shelves or notice boards etc, an estimate is not normally provided unless specifically requested. A cost code will be required against which the works can be charged. For more major works such as redecoration or revised office layouts etc, an estimate will be provided and you will be asked to provide a written (or e-mail) instruction together with an appropriate cost code. It should be borne in mind that some works may require planning permission or building control/fire officer approval and therefore sufficient time should be allowed for this in your planning process. Advice regarding this can be obtained from Carolina Spoor. It is important to remember that for health and safety and consistency reasons no works can be undertaken direct by departments. All works must be carried out via the Estates Department. title: Estates and Campus Services
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Estates and Campus Services > General information Estates and Campus Services Capital Works Capital works are major new build or refurbishment projects generally driven by a strategic need of the University. In most circumstances an external design team will be appointed by the Estates Department to undertake the design and contract administration of the project. A member of the Estates Department team will be appointed as Client Representative who will act as the liaison between the design team and the University. An internal Client Representative will need to be appointed by the Faculty or Support Service for whom the works are being undertaken. Responsibilities of the Internal Client Representative are: This representative will need to be given the authority to take decisions on behalf of the Internal Client and act as a single point of contact for the Client Representative and design team. He/She will also participate in the preparation of the Design Brief. This is a key document that states all the requirements of the end users and is therefore a key document to the design team and the ongoing success of the project. To attend design team meetings throughout the design phase and liaise with colleagues to ensure that the ongoing design fully meets your brief. Take an active part in the financial management of the project including any value engineering exercises that may be undertaken. Sign off the drawings and specification upon completion of the design phase. User changes cannot be considered after this point except in exceptional circumstances. Atend site progress meetings during the construction phase of the project. Prepare for the task of occu

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Recruitment, Screening and Hiring Human Resources Right to Work Document Checks As an employer, we have a duty to prevent illegal working. The Immigration, Asylum and Nationality Act 2006 says that employers should conduct document checks which show that your potential employee has the right to work in the UK and is permitted to do the type of work you are offering. You are required to undertake the right to work check within a reasonable time period before the employment commences. You should not make assumptions about a person’s right to work in the UK or their immigration status on the basis of their colour, nationality, ethnic or national origins. The best way to ensure that you do not discriminate is to treat all job applicants fairly and make the same document checks. Please note the temporary adjusted right to work checks that were introduced during the pandemic were withdrawn on 30 September 2022. Adjusted checking of UK and Irish passports is no longer a suitable UKVI right to work check. On 1 October 2022 UKVI introduced changes to the way we check right to work for UK/Irish nationals to that of international workers. Right to Work checks for British/Irish nationals For all British/Irish nationals you must obtain original documents, check validity and make clear copies to send to the People Hub for retention BEFORE someone starts working for the University. We are required by the Home Office to have seen and copied the original documents as follows: For British applicants: A British passport card (current or expired) Please copy the personal details (biodata) page of the passport. For Irish applicants: An Irish passport or pa

title: Recruitment, Marketing and Communications
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Recruitment, Marketing and Communications > Digital content Recruitment, Marketing and Communications Most factual information on the website, such as details about University services, is updated by the teams of those departments. This means that teams can update relevant information efficiently, without relying on the capacity of central website editors. To make these kinds of updates, you need to become a website content editor. Responsibilities Information accuracy Department information owners are responsible for ensuring that information is correct. For example, details about University services are provided by the teams of those departments. Website editing The content editor role is specifically to enable editors to update factual information on existing webpages across http://port.ac.uk and MyPort. This role is ideal for editors who need to regularly update information on the http://port.ac.uk website or MyPort for accuracy — for example a Student Service. This role is not to enable editors from departments outside of RMC to produce or publish new webpages, move pages, change URLs, or make significant webpage changes. Staff may request to become a website editor by requesting the relevant training. If you don’t wish to edit your own pages, you can suggest content updates through a Service Desk ticket for the Digital Content team to review. This option is best of you only need small updates once or twice a year. Making updates You'll send us your changes to review by saving your page as ‘Ready for Approval’ in Drupal. The Digital Content team will review your page against our content requirements and publish the page if everything is okay. As th

body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Catering Services > Events Catering Services Food on Campus ‌Food on Campus plays an important part in the success of your event, and the enjoyment of your guests. Our reputation for great food at competitive prices is outstanding, and you'll be delighted with the presentation. For our full food and drink range, please refer to our 'Food on Campus' brochure. For bookings please complete an online booking form. Bookings can only be made via the online booking system with a valid cost code. Please contact the Events Office on extension 3691 or email catering.events@port.ac.uk to discuss any dietary requirements and to make bookings for over 50 Guests. External bookings Organisations outside the University wishing to use our services should contact the Conference and Lettings office via email at Conferences@port.ac.uk or by telephone 02392 844 884. Please note that the prices quoted on these menus apply to internal events only, and that external events will be subject to V.A.T at the current rate. title: Catering Services
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Catering Services > Events Catering Services Food on Campus Terms and Conditions for the provision of food and beverages from the University of Portsmouth Events Department. Confirmation of booking Bookings are either secured via Online Catering Booking or by emailing catering.events@port.ac.uk. On written confirmation that your booking is secured and numbers/prices/dates agreed, a contract between us will be formed and you agree to be bound by these terms and conditions. Catering services We will supply the catering service for the event on the date set out in the confirmation of booking. We shall provide the catering services: In accordance with food catering industry standards for general hygiene and general cleanliness concerning: The preparation or cooking of food The handling and service of food, beverages, utensils and tableware and In accordance with statutory and regulatory requirements affecting the preparation, cooking, handling and service of food. Allergens and dietary requirements Dishes may contain allergens, if you or any of the customers, who you are booking food for, have any dietary requirements it is your responsibility that we (UoP Events) are made aware of this. We will endeavour to produce dishes for the dietary requirements and label them accordingly. If you have any questions regarding allergens or dietary foodstuffs please contact a member of the Events team: Email – catering.events@port.ac.uk Events Manager – Kevin Goldstone – kevin.goldstone@port.ac.uk / +44(0)23 9284 3733 Events Chef – Mike Williams – michael.williams2@port.ac.uk / +44(0)23 9284 3820 Event Administrator (Allergen Champion) – Tracy de Buriatte – tracy.deburia

title: Estates and Campus Services
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Estates and Campus Services > Soft Services (Campus Environment) Estates and Campus Services Electric Vehicle Charging The University provides electric vehicle (EV) charging stations for staff members with University car parking permits. Staff and students will be able to use the chargers at anytime during the day and the general public will have access to them Monday - Friday between 4.00pm - 7.00am and all day at weekends. Below, you’ll find all the details on how to use the chargers, pricing information, and frequently asked questions. How to Use the EV Chargers 1. Download the SEE-V App To access and pay for charging, download the SEE-V app: Android Users: Google Play Store iOS Users: Apple App Store 2. Register and Set Up Payment Open the SEE-V app and sign up with your university email. Agree to the University’s terms and conditions of use. Add a payment method in the app settings. 3. Locate a Charging Station Open the SEE-V app and use the map feature to find available charging stations on campus. Check real-time availability and pricing before arriving. The University has charging points located within the car parks listed below: Burnaby Car Park, Burnaby Road, Portsmouth, PO1 3AE (4 spaces) Eldon Car Park, Middle Street, Portsmouth, PO5 4BT (4 spaces) Milldam Car Park, Burnaby Road, Portsmouth, PO1 3RF (4 spaces) Portland Car Park, St James Street, Portsmouth, PO1 3AE (4 spaces) Ravelin Sports Centre, Cambridge Rd, Portsmouth, PO1 2SE (4 spaces) William Beatty Car Park, Hampshire Terrace, Portsmouth, PO1 2UP (4 spaces) 4. title: Equity, Diversity & Inclusion
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Equity, Diversity & Inclusion > EDI in Action Equity, Diversity & Inclusion Race Equality Charter and the University of Portsmouth In July 2018 the University signed up to a national scheme which aims to tackle race inequality across the HE Sector. Run by Advance HE, and launched nationally in 2016, the Race Equality Charter aims to improve the representation, progression and success of minority ethnic staff and students within higher education. Although similar to Athena SWAN, the national HE gender equality scheme, it is more comprehensive covering professional and support staff and academic staff; student progress and attainment and the diversity of the curriculum. Signing up to the Charter is a significant long-term commitment which will involve long-term institutional culture change across the University. We aim to build a culture of openness and trust that allows for honest reflection and difficult conversations about race and acknowledges that racial inequalities exist. Our membership of the Charter demonstrates our commitment to tackling race inequality for staff and students across the University. The Race Equality Charter principles are: Racial inequalities are a significant issue within higher education. Racial inequalities are not necessarily overt, isolated incidents. Racism is an everyday facet of UK society and racial inequalities manifest themselves in everyday situations, processes and behaviours. UK higher education cannot reach its full potential unless it can benefit from the talents of the whole population and until individuals from all ethnic backgrounds can benefit equally from the opportunities it affords. In developing solutions

title: Department for Curriculum and Quality Enhancement
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Department for Curriculum and Quality Enhancement > DCQE Director Department for Curriculum and Quality Enhancement Lucy Sharp - Interim Director of Curriculum and Quality Enhancement Email: lucy.sharp@port.ac.uk Ext: 3568 Profile: Lucy leads DCQE, which underpins the strategic aims of the University through the provision of psychological support and academic mentorship to students, strengthening academic skills, innovative pedagogy and the enhancement of learning and teaching, improving digital capabilities and expanding technology enhanced learning, and increasing data analytics through the student voice with internal and external surveys. Before joining the University in 2014, Lucy held senior leadership roles in Third Sector Organisations, Local Authorities and NHS Trusts where she led and transformed Integrated Community Mental Health Services. Lucy has also worked as an Approved Mental Health Professional, with interests in Forensic Mental Health and Veteran’s Mental Health. Lucy studied Sports Science and Theology at St Mary’s University College, and completed her postgraduate and professional studies at the University of Portsmouth. Aaron Sayers - Departmental Manager Email: aaron.sayers@port.ac.uk Ext: 3232 Profile: Aaron has worked at the University since 2008 when he joined the HR department as a temporary Admin Assistant. Since then he has worked in administrative and management positions in faculty and professional services and has been in his current role as Department Manager for DCQE since 2011. Aaron has a BA in Film Production, MA in Business Management and is a professionally certified PRINCE2 Practitioner. Aaron's experience and qualifications in HE, administration and management offers creativity, innovation and a safe pair of hands for the operational requirement

title: Who is who on the board of governors | University of Portsmouth
body: Skip to main content <iframe src="https://www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-WHX6C84" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Current membership The current membership of the Board consists of the following: 11 external governors 1 staff governor elected by and from professional services staff 1 staff governor elected by and from academic staff 1 Academic Council Staff governor 1 Student governor UPSU Elected Officer Vice-Chancellor Further information about each member of the Board can be seen below. External Governors Professor Sandy Pepper (Chair of the Board of Governors) Sandy joined the Board in October 2022 and is a member of the Senior Postholders' and Vice-Chancellor's Remuneration Committees. He is currently Emeritus Professor of Management Practice at the London School of Economics, where he worked full-time from 2008 until September 2022. Prior to this he had a long career at PricewaterhouseCoopers, where he held various senior management roles, including as Global Leader of PwC's Human Resource Services consulting practice from 2002 until 2006. He trained as a chartered accountant with Coopers & Lybrand (now PwC) and spent most of his career in practice working in tax and human resource services. His academic background is in organisational behaviour, management theory and new institutional economics. His research interests include executive pay, corporate governance and business ethics. Helen Astle Helen joined the Board in August 2025. She is an experienced pension trustee and non-executive director, currently serving as a trustee director of a large multi-employer pension fund/master trust and as a board member of a legal regulator. She is a seasoned executive-level lawyer and corporate governance leader, with 16 years’ experience advising boards within regulated organisations across financial services (including pensions), housing, and healthcare. She has successfully led and overseen legal services, corporate gove

body: Skip to main content <iframe src="https://www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-WHX6C84" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Make a payment online Pay your tuition, accommodation or language course fees If you're paying your student tuition or accommodation fees in full or part payments, or have sponsorship support, read the information here to make sure your online payments are made correctly. You can also find details on fees for our English language courses, international admission fees, how to stay on track with your payments and who to ask when you need help. We accept payments from: University of Portsmouth now welcomes UnionPay Cards for safe and convenient payment online. Debit or credit card International and UK bank transfer Make a payment Tuition fees Close all Full payment Once you register or re-register online, a tuition fee invoice will be generated and an email will be sent advising you of a new transaction on your account. You can view your account and pay your invoice by logging in to your Student View and accessing payment links via the My Finance tab. Pre-payments such as deposits aren't shown on your invoice; remember to deduct these before making the payment. You can check your balance in the My Finance tab. You can pay via American Express, Mastercard, Visa, Visa Debit or Paypal. You can pay by Flywire international bank transfer. Sponsorship If you are a sponsor and have been issued with an invoice you will be asked to log-in to the Webpay portal using your account number (found at the top left of your invoice). Payment can be made online 24 hours after the invoice has been issued using American Express, Mastercard, Visa, Visa Debit and Paypal. title: Security and Surveillance
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Security and Surveillance > crime/incident reporting Security and Surveillance Crime/Incident Reporting It is important that reports on all incidents of crime or suspect persons in buildings are made to the Security Control Room as soon as possible. It should be understood that although security will take a report of crime from an aggrieved person, it is important that the person also reports the matter to the police. This is necessary for insurance purposes. It is essential that enough information on the crime and suspect is passed to the Control Room Operator when reporting. Location of incident Brief description of incident Description of offender Time of incident Names of persons involved This will ensure that the most effective use can be made of the University security resources in manpower and CCTV. title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > My Rewards and Benefits Human Resources Pay / Salary Information Salary Scales Your salary will be paid monthly in arrears on the last working day of each month by credit transfer to your bank account. The University issues electronic payslips to staff which can be viewed on Employee Self Service (ESS). If you need to change the details of the bank account your salary is paid into, you are able to do this within Employee Self Service. For support on how to complete a bank account change visit the HR System - Employee Self Service site. You can view the University of Portsmouth current Salary Scales listing all spine points and grades. Progression to the top of your grade is by annual increment. Salary Changes for Staff on the Main Pay Scale There are two potential increases made to your salary in each calendar year: Your salary increases by one spine point until it reaches the maximum spine point for your grade. After this point, no spine point increments will be made. The nationally negotiated increase to your salary is implemented where the whole salary structure is uplifted. This is usually implemented in August. Cost of Employment Follow this link to the table showing the actual cost to the University of employment by spine point. The Real Living Wage The University has made a commitment to the Real Living Wage set by the Living Wage Foundation. Posts paid at Spinal Points 6 to 14 (Grades 1 and 2) will receive a discretionary supplement to their salary to increase them to the living wage, if appropriate. This supplement is included within the spine point pay scales. The Living Wage increase will be reviewed when the annual National

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > My Rewards and Benefits Human Resources Season Ticket Advances The University of Portsmouth offers employees an interest-free season ticket loan to cover the cost of public travel between home and the workplace. The scheme allows employees to make financial savings whilst encouraging the use of public transport wherever practicable. Buying a season ticket is generally cheaper than purchasing weekly or monthly tickets with some operators offering an annual ticket for the equivalent price of 40 weekly tickets. University Staff may also benefit from an additional discount via the easitPORTSMOUTH website. Please find detailed below a list of general conditions applicable to all season ticket loans. How it works The University will fund the purchase of a season ticket (up to a maximum value of £6,000) by way of an interest free loan. You then pay back this loan within the lifetime of the season ticket by equal monthly instalments from your net salary (that is, after tax and National Insurance have been deducted). The season ticket must be valid for travel between your two chosen stations and can be used at any time, including weekends, although the loan is approved on the understanding that it is mainly intended for travel between home and the workplace. Loans cannot be granted for monthly tickets or for tickets which cost less than £100 per annum. Loans are made in the form of a salary advance, which will be paid at the same time as your usual monthly salary. In the following month, Payroll will commence the regular deductions from your pay. In no circumstances will the repayment period be allowed to extend beyond or exceed the life of the

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > My Rewards and Benefits Human Resources Sickness Absence Managing sickness absence The University is committed to the fair and consistent treatment of staff in relation to sickness absence and to supporting individuals during periods of ill health. Our Sickness Absence and Ill Health Policy page provides information you may find useful. Managers are required to conduct a return to work form after every period of sickness absence, in an appropriate confidential setting, to support the employee’s return to work. Following each discussion, managers must complete and submit the return to work form via iTrent to ensure accurate documentation and appropriate follow up. Guidance on how to complete this form can be found on Moodle. Your manager can refer you to Occupational Health (OH) for advice if your health is affecting your fitness to carry out your role, or if your role is adversely affecting your health. For more information, visit the Referral to Occupational Health webpage. You can also download the Staff Guide to Occupational Health Referral and Advice. Entitlement to Sick Pay for Variable Hours Staff It is important that policies for Variable Hours staff reflect fair and consistent Terms and Conditions. Specifically, Variable Hours staff are entitled to claim Occupational Sick Pay in line with their contract of employment. Full details are available at our page Entitlement to Sick pay for Variable Hours Staff. title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > My Rewards and Benefits Human Resources Other Leave and Absence Family-friendly entitlements The University provides a wide range of Family Friendly entitlements including information and guidance regarding Adoption/Maternity/ Paternity Leave and Pay, Parental Leave, the Right to Request Flexible Working and Time-off for Dependants. Leave for religious festivals Short term and extended leave for religious or cultural holidays/festivals will need to be booked as annual leave. No reasonable request for annual leave should be refused by a manager. However, in deciding what is reasonable, a manager will need to look at all the implications on the team/department or service they deliver when authorising leave. See the University's Religion and Belief Policy for further details. Compassionate leave Line managers are authorised to grant up to five days' leave on compassionate grounds in any rolling 12 month period. Requests for leave of more than five days should be made to the Head of School/Professional Service, who is authorised to grant up to 10 days leave in any one year. In exceptional circumstances, leave in excess of 10 days may be granted. Line managers must notify HR of any compassionate leave taken by their staff via the People Hub. Full details can be found in the Guidance on taking Compassionate Leave. Sabbaticals Any academic member of staff, on an open-ended contract to undertake research, innovation and teaching, and who has three years’ continuous service with the University of Portsmouth at the time of commencing the sabbatical, is normally eligible to apply for a sabbatical. Periods of maternity, paternity, adoption leave o

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > My Rewards and Benefits Human Resources Flexible Working Flexible working is a way of working that suits an employee's needs. All employees have the legal right to request a change to their contractual terms and conditions of employment to work more flexibly, not just parents and carers. This could be a change in working pattern, the number of hours worked or a request to work from home. It is important to note that this is the right to request a change only, and not the automatic right to actually change your pattern. The government provide guidance on requesting flexible working. Please follow the links below on the process for requesting flexible working at the University of Portsmouth: Right to Request Flexible Working Guidance Guidance on Flexible Working Requests for restrictions of timetabled hours (for Academic Staff) Flexible Working Application form Photo by Luca Bravo on Unsplash Explore HR HomepageMy RoleLeaving My RoleManaging My TeamHR Business Partnering TeamLearning and DevelopmentHR SystemsMy Rewards and Benefits Health and Wellbeing Retail Discounts Events and New or Limited Time Offers Financial Wellbeing Travel Discounts Leave and Absence Annual Leave Sickness Absence Other Leave and Absence Flexible Working Leisure, Food and Drink Discounts Pay and Recognition Personal Development My Reward and Benefits menu Life Insurance Hardware and Software Recognition PensionsPayrollOccupational Health ServiceEquality and DiversityContact usStaff Surveys HR A-Z UniversityofPortsmouth Home Students Staff Contact and departments Home Staff services Departments Resources © University of Portsmouth Cookie policy Modern Slavery Sta

body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > My Rewards and Benefits Human Resources Annual Leave The information below provides information on leave options, with links to relevant policies and guidance. Some may be paid/unpaid/annual leave or combination thereof; and some also may be subject to eligibility/qualifying periods, therefore the Guidance on Annual Leave should be referred to for actual details. The annual leave year for all staff runs from 1 October to 30 September in any year and staff are normally expected to take their entitlement within this timeframe. Leave entitlement Portsmouth Campus London Campus Academic and Research staff receive an annual leave entitlement of 259 hours per year (equivalent to 35 standard full-time days), pro rata for part-time or part-year service. This includes: Deans, Heads, Professors, Readers, Researchers, University Tutors and Graduate Teaching Assistants Professional Services staff receive an annual leave entitlement of 236.8 hours per year (32 days equivalent), pro rata for part-time or part-year service Staff on casual contracts and variable hours contracts have an entitlement calculated in proportion to the number of hours worked All University of Portsmouth London staff have an annual leave entitlement of 222 hours (equivalent to 30 standard full-time days) Staff on casual contracts and variable hours contracts have an entitlement calculated in proportion to the number of hours worked You can check your leave entitlement, balance and leave taken by viewing your Self Service record, which you can access directly via Employee Self-service (ESS). You can also access Self Service from the Staff Essentials page. Joining or leaving th

title: Health, Safety and Compliance
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Health, Safety and Compliance > A to Z Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety Biological Safety Blood Please follow this link for more information on the Use, Handling and Taking of Blood within the University. University Biological Safety Officer The University has an appointed Biological Safety Officer. Containment Levels Biological hazards must be handled in the minimum containment level to their risk, for example hazard group 1= containment level 1. Hazard group 1 can be handled in containment level 2, but hazard group 2 can not be handled at containment level 1. The University has both containment level 1 and 2 facilities and the minimum control measures are listed below; Containment Level 1 (CL1) Containment Level 2 (CL2) Door should be closed while work in progress Eating, drinking, smoking, storing of food and applying of cosmetics prohibited Mouth pipetting is prohibited Hands must be washed after contamination suspected, after handing viable materials and when leaving lab PPE stored correctly, checked and maintained regularly Waste must be rendered harmless before disposal All accidents, incidents and near misses reported immediately Laboratory coats must be worn Howie Laboratory coats must be worn (and not worn elsewhere) Separate hand wash basin Taps should be the type not operated by hand Procedures should reduce the production of aerosols Procedures with infectious aerosols must be conducted in a safety cabinet Effective disinfectant Specified disinfection procedures Authorised access only Safe storage of biological agents Written training records (as determined by Risk Assessment) For more information please see Microbiological safety or contact the Health and Safety Office. Explore TrainingHealth and Safety TeamA to Z UniversityofPortsmouth Home Students Staff

title: Health, Safety and Compliance
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Health, Safety and Compliance > A to Z Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety Control of Substances Hazardous to Health Regulations (COSHH) 2002 Using chemicals or other hazardous substances at work can put people's health at risk, so the law requires employers to control exposure to hazardous substances to prevent ill health. Employers have to protect both employees and others who may be exposed by complying with the Control of Substances Hazardous to Health Regulations (COSHH) 2002 (as amended). Substances hazardous to health are: Generally those which are classified as being: very toxic toxic harmful irritant corrosive sensitising carcinogenic mutagenic toxic to reproduction (teratogenic) 2. Gases which act as simple asphyxiants 3. Biological agents and micro-organisms 4. Preparations and mixtures 5. Dust 6. Substances which have been assigned a Workplace Exposure Limit (WEL) 7. Any substance not mentioned above which creates a hazard to health COSHH does not apply to... COSHH applies to virtually all substances hazardous to health except: Flammable and explosive substances Lead and lead salts (Contact Health & Safety Office) Asbestos Radioactive substances which are only harmful by nature of their radioactivity. These substances are subject to separate pieces of legislation and University policies and procedures. Information on controlling these substances can be found by following the above links. 2020 COSHH Policy and Chemical Inventory Procedure The COSHH Policy has been reviewed and updated and a new Chemical Inventory Procedure has been introduced. The expectation for the Chemical Inventory Procedure is that a chemical inventory should exist for each Laboratory or area that stores chemicals and that a member of staff is identified to be responsible for its upkeep. title: Health, Safety and Compliance
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Health, Safety and Compliance > A to Z Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety Dangerous Substances and Explosive Atmospheres Regulations 2002 (DSEAR) The Dangerous Substances and Explosive Atmospheres Regulations 2002 (DSEAR) requires the University to make a suitable and sufficient assessment of the risks to his employees, where a dangerous substance is or is liable to be present in the workplace. Any requirement for a DSEAR assessment is identified through the fire risk assessment process and a specific assessment is completed by the Health & Safety Office. The estate is, broadly speaking, based on two main campuses, these being Guildhall and Langstone Student Village and extends to approximately 1.5 million sq ft of buildings together with associated playing fields and open parkland. The building stock extends to some 70 main buildings together with sundry additional minor structures.
