title: Health, Safety and Compliance
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Health, Safety and Compliance > A to Z Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety Working at Height The Work at Height Regulations 2005 came into force on 6 April 2005. Working at height is defined as "work in any place, including a place at or below ground level, or obtaining access to or egress from such a place, while at work, except by a staircase where, if suitable measures were not taken, a person could fall a distance likely to cause personal injury". Where work at height is required a risk assessment must be completed in order to identify the risk control measures required. Our risk assessment process takes into consideration the hierarchy of control for work at height as set out within the Work at Height Regulations and those in control of any work at height activity must make sure work is properly planned, supervised and carried out by competent people in a manner which is so far as is reasonably practicable safe. Only use ladders and step ladders for work at height where the low risk and short duration makes this acceptable and where the nature of the work enables three points of contact to be maintained throughout. The University has produced a Working at Height Procedure which provides more information. Working at Height Equipment All working at height equipment must be suitable for the purpose for which it is being used and comply with the requirements of the Regulations: Schedule 2 Requirements for guard-rails, toe-boards, barriers and similar collective means of protection Schedule 3Requirements for working platforms Schedule 4 Requirements for collective safeguards for arresting falls Schedule 5Requirements for personal fall protection systems Schedule 6Requirements for ladders Maintenance of Working at Height Equipment All working at height equipment must be ins

title: Health, Safety and Compliance
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Health, Safety and Compliance > A to Z Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety Lifting equipment The deployment of suitable lifting equipment/transport aids and their use in accordance with safe systems of work will reduce the need for manual handling and can help eliminate poor manual handling practices. Any equipment used at work that lifts or lowers a load is regulated by the Lifting Operations and Lifting Equipment Regulations (LOLER) 1998. These regulations deal with both the lifting equipment and the way it is used. As well as regular passenger lifts, LOLER also applies to equipment such as forklift trucks and reach trucks, tail-lifts on vans and trucks, scissor lifts, cherry-pickers, certain parts of conveyors, and dumb waiters. Currently, the statutory requirements for lifting equipment and accessories are that they must be: Marked with the safe working load, this may take the form of a maximum load on hoists and jacks Checked regularly (see requirement for thorough examination and inspection of lifting equipment). Statutory inspections must be performed by a competent person (usually an external inspection body). Inspections should also be performed by the user prior to use (a simple visual inspection is usually sufficient). Before lifting equipment is used for the first time, it must be thoroughly examined Only used by persons that have had suitable and sufficient information and training on their safe operation and use and any precautions or safeguards required Used under adequate supervision, the extent of the supervision being dependent on the competence level of the operator Immediately removed from service if found to be defective (disposed of or quarantined until repaired, inspected and proved to be safe for further use) Explore TrainingHealth and Safety TeamA

title: Health, Safety and Compliance
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Health, Safety and Compliance > A to Z Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety Microbiological Safety Legislation The main areas of Health and Safety law relevant to micro-organisms used in the University of Portsmouth are: The Health and Safety at Work etc Act 1974 Management of Health and Safety at Work Regulations 1999 Control of Substances Hazardous to Health Regulations (COSHH) 2002 (as amended) The Genetically Modified Organisms (Contained Use) (Amendment) Regulations 2005 (.pdf) This site deals with the health hazards presented by bacteria, fungi and viruses and the prevention of laboratory and non-laboratory acquired infections. A specific area of responsibility for the University of Portsmouth is the control of legionella bacteria in water systems. Risks from Micro-organisms Assessment of the relative risk of handling infectious agents has led to the adoption of a scale consisting of four Risk (or Hazard) Groups, ranging from the least (Group 1) to the most (Group 4) hazardous to laboratory workers and the community. The classification of micro-organisms on the basis of risk is: Risk Group 1 (no or very low individual and community risk): A micro-organism that is unlikely to cause human or animal disease. Risk Group 2 (moderate individual risk, low community risk): A pathogen that can cause human or animal disease but is unlikely to be a serious hazard to laboratory workers, the community, livestock or the environment. Laboratory exposures may cause serious infection, but effective treatment and preventive measures are available and the risk of spread of infection is limited. Risk Group 3 (high individual and community risk): A pathogen that usually causes serious human or animal disease but does not ordinarily spread from one infected individual to another. Effective

title: Health, Safety and Compliance
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Health, Safety and Compliance > A to Z Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety Personal Protective Equipment Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) is all equipment (including clothing affording protection against the weather), which is intended to be worn to protect the user against risks to their health or safety. Examples include: Hearing protection Gloves Eye protection and face-shields Respiratory protective equipment Safety boots or other footwear Warm and weatherproof clothing Safety helmets High-visibility clothing Laboratory Coats Safety harnesses or fall arrest systems Legal Duties Under the Personal Protective Equipment at Work Regulations 1992 (as amended): The University must ensure that suitable personal protective equipment is provided to university staff and students; who may be exposed to a risk to their health or safety, except where and to the extent that such risk has been adequately controlled by other means which are equally or more effective. The Health and Safety at Work, etc Act 1974 (HSWA) section 9, places a general duty of care on the University for ensuring the safety of their workers and requires that no charge is made for providing PPE. Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) plus PPE Issue Form Your risk assessment needs to show that you have considered PPE as a last resort. This form has been designed to show that due consideration has been given to the Principles of Prevent/Hierarchy of Controls and PPE is a last resort and the selection of the PPE is appropriate. The PPE Assessment Checklist is for all PPE, including disposable PPE. It can be used to cover several risk assessments e.g. If the School/Faculty uses one type of disposable gloves for a wide range of tasks. For PPE that has been issued to staff the PPE Issue Record Form will need to be co

title: Health, Safety and Compliance
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Health, Safety and Compliance > A to Z Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety Placements Placement Offices carry out checks and collate information for placements such as a Health and Safety Checklist (see below) and a Student Overseas Travel Risk Assessment. As per the normal procedure for placements, Placement Offices can escalate issues to Corporate Health and Safety or Insurance for guidance. UK Placements Placement providers will need to complete the Health and Safety Checklist for UK Placements - Word Version or Google Form. Clinical Placements Clinical placements are separated into two categories: placements with the NHS and placements with private healthcare providers. For placements provided by the NHS, it will not be necessary to send a H&S Checklist. For placements provided by private healthcare providers - placement providers will need to complete the Health and Safety Checklist for Clinical Placements - Word version or Google Form. Crown Placements Placement providers will need to complete the Health and Safety Checklist for Crown Placements - Word Version or Google Form. Self-Employed Placements All self-employed placements should have a Risk Assessment in place for the activities involved in their placement. Self-employed risk assessments are no longer required to be sent to Corporate Health and Safety for approval. If any assistance is required to ensure an appropriate risk assessment is in place for higher risk activities please contact Health and Safety at hsservicedesk@port.ac.uk. Self-employed placements should have arrangements in place for their own public liability insurance. International Placements Students on Placement in Home Country The following paperwork must be completed by the student and placement provider and sent to their Faculty Liaison for

title: Health, Safety and Compliance
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Health, Safety and Compliance > A to Z Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety Workplace inspections Workplace inspections are one of the primary methods of identifying and eliminating actual and potential hazards. These hazards can include problems with equipment, the workplace environment, the building, as well as with work practices. There are many different kinds of workplace inspections including daily "walkabout" inspections when you enter your laboratory or office, "pre-use" inspections of equipment you are going to run, and "spot-checks" by your work supervisor or advisors. Other inspections are more formal, and involve documenting the fact that inspections have been done on a regular basis as part of "due diligence". The Workplace must be inspected reguarly using the University's Workplace Inspection procedure. Please email hsservicedesk@port.ac.uk if you have any queries or require assistance to complete inspections in your area. 'The role of the Unison Health & Safety team is to work with the University in the interest of reducing injuries and ill health in the work place. This is achieved in consultation with management on new H&S policies and procedures, by ensuring accidents and incidents have been appropriately investigated and by overseeing workplace inspections and audits. Our aim is to promote and encourage involvement of all staff in Health & Safety matters and therefore reduce the likelihood of injuries or accidents at work occurring'. 'Workplace inspections help to identify hazards and potential risks and therefore play an important role in prevention. Unison Health and Safety Rep’s are therefore pleased to have had the opportunity to be involved in the design and launch of the new workplace Inspection forms and to encourage and support staff in carrying o

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Pensions Human Resources LGPS TIMELINE: HOW TO JOIN If you are an eligible employee in a Support, Administrative, Professional Services or Research role with a contract due to last at least 3 months, we’ll contractually enrol you in the Local Government Pension Scheme (LGPS) from your first day of employment. You need to remain a member for 2 years to qualify to keep your pension – otherwise your contributions may have to be refunded or transferred to another scheme, or you may be able to 'freeze' them on the expectation that you could return to LGPS later. If you have an employment lasting less than 3 months you won’t be contractually enrolled but can still choose to opt in by emailing us at pensionenquiries@port.ac.uk. If you are aged 75 or over, you are not able to join the scheme. You may also be automatically enrolled, where you were not in any pension scheme but meet the government’s criteria to be compulsorily enrolled. This happens where you: Were not, at time of assessment, in a pension scheme for this employment Have not previously opted-out of a pension in this employment, or opted-out more than 12 months before the University’s auto enrolment ‘staging date’, which happens every 3 years Earned over a minimum amount in a single month (£833 at time of writing) Are aged between 22 and your State Pension Age We’ll let you know if you are ever automatically enrolled. Opted out previously but now want to re-join? Just let us know by emailing pensionenquiries@port.ac.uk. DISCLAIMERThe University of Portsmouth is not your pension provider and many queries you have may be best directed to the schemes themselves, where they have dedic

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Pensions Human Resources LGPS TIMELINE: ESSENTIAL ACTIONS WHEN YOU JOIN Visit the Hants LGPS website as soon as you can to learn about all the benefits of being a member and read the Starter Information Booklet. There are video guides covering the basics, and the employee guide covering most of the detail and technical information you may need. You’ll be sent a welcome pack by email (normally a month or so after paying your first contributions) to invite you to register for the LGPS Member Portal. This is the best way to view and manage your pension and personal details, talk with the scheme administrators, and access your Annual Benefit Statement updating you on the value of your pension each summer. Follow the Just Joined pages to complete your details. You’ll want to check your name, address and marital/cohabiting status is up to date. IMMEDIATE ACTION: Update your ‘Expression of Wish’ on the portal as soon as possible to nominate a beneficiary for your pension and death in service payments. If you are nominating someone you live with who is not a civil partner or married to you, you may need to submit a Cohabiting Partner form. Returning to LGPS? See Re-joining for how this works. Got another pension? You may be able to transfer this to LGPS but have to complete within your first 12 months of membership. Want to add even more value to your pension? It’s never too early to do this. DISCLAIMERThe University of Portsmouth is not your pension provider and many queries you have may be best directed to the schemes themselves, where they have dedicated member helplines to assist you. These pages are for general informational purposes only

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Pensions Human Resources LGPS – WHAT DO YOU GET & WHAT DOES IT COST? The LGPS Benefits page details the many benefits of being a member: WHAT DO YOU GET? WHAT DOES IT COST? A pension set out in law making it very safe and secure An option for a tax-free lump sum when you retire Tax relief on all your pension contributions Option to pay 50% of contributions for 50% of benefits in the 50-50 scheme Increase pension value by paying extra contributions Massive employer contributions towards your pension of 20.6% A monthly pension payment from your retirement for the rest of your life Flexible (or 'partial') and early retirement options Annual cost of living adjustments to your pension Pension protections during long-term absence Ill health pensions (qualifying after 2 years) Death in Service lump sum of 3 times your pensionable pay Partner’s pension for life, in event of your death, generally based on 30.6% of your benefits (or higher for service prior to 2014) Children’s pension for eligible dependants in event of your death You’ll pay a percentage contribution on all your pensionable earnings (normally including salary, bonuses and overtime), at a rate depending on how much you earn which is often 3 or more times less than we pay as your employer - you’ll see all these contributions on your monthly payslips The good news is that you don't pay tax on contributions you make. For example, £100 of contributions for a 20% tax payer would only cost £80 from your take-home pay. This works for higher rate tax payers too, so you'd get the full 40% saving 'up front' without having to claim it from HMRC at the end of the year DISCLAIMERThe Universit

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Pensions Human Resources LGPS TIMELINE: HOW DOES IT WORK? The ‘How is my pension calculated’ section of LGPS Benefits provides details and examples of how your pension builds up value. Remember, LGPS is a ‘defined benefit’ scheme, meaning that unlike most workplace and personal pensions available, it increases in value according to knowable and defined rules, rather than being dependent on investment and market performance. The current scheme for all members is a CARE (or ‘Career Average Revalued Earnings’) pension: Each year you build up an Annual Pension (paid every year of your life after retirement) equivalent to 1/49th of your pensionable earnings. This is adjusted with cost of living changes so what you contribute today shouldn’t be left behind by inflation. This isn’t an easy thing to understand upon first reading – we recommend the ‘How is my pension worked out – an example’ section of the employee scheme guide for a better illustration. If you were a member prior to 1 April 2014 you may also have a Final Salary pension based on ‘60ths’, and if before 1 April 2008 may have one based on ‘80ths’: You build up a pension based on the total number of years’ membership you have (pro-rated for part time), divided by 60 or 80 (see dates above). This is multiplied by your best salary in the last 3 years at the point you retire. If you have the ‘80ths’ scheme you’ll get an automatic tax free lump sum of 3 times your annual pension too. All Final Salary pensions are now closed - you can’t earn any more years but its value will still increase as your salary goes up. Why Do I pay a higher percentage than I earn? It may look like you’re payi

body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Pensions Human Resources LGPS TIMELINE: TRANSITIONAL PROTECTIONS & 'McCLOUD' What does ‘McCloud’ mean for me?The age discrimination case (known as ‘McCloud’) against the government for moving from Final Salary to CARE (Career Average) schemes based on age means that members who joined before 31 March 2012 with both Final Salary and CARE pensions can qualify for protection to stay in their Final Salary schemes between April 2015 to March 2022. Hants LGPS will provide details on this when you decide to retire. Find out more on the Hants LGPS website (we recommend reading through the government's LGPS McCloud Factsheet first to see if you need to find out more). DISCLAIMERThe University of Portsmouth is not your pension provider and many queries you have may be best directed to the schemes themselves, where they have dedicated member helplines to assist you. These pages are for general informational purposes only and nothing contained within may be seen as financial advice. Where you have a decision to make regarding your pension, we would always recommend you pursue Independent Financial Advice, with a qualified advisor who is familiar with pensions and your particular scheme. body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Pensions Human Resources LGPS TIMELINE: OPTING OUT You may feel you need to opt out of your pension, however we would always recommend you remain in scheme whenever you can. Please consider this carefully and make sure you choose the right option for your circumstances. First consider the LGPS 50/50 Scheme: This lets you to stay in your pension, paying only 50% of contributions in return for accruing 50% of the benefits. You can later re-join the ‘main’ scheme at any time. If you still wish to opt out you can complete an Opt Out form and attach this to pensionenquiries@port.ac.uk. You can only request this on or after the first day you join and forms sent before this must be rejected. Opting out within 3 months of joining the scheme: UoP will refund all contributions through your pay, less tax and National Insurance deductions. This will be the case even if you have another deferred LGPS pension or if an LGPS pension is being paid to you. Opting out after 3 months but less than 2 years in the scheme: You will stop paying contributions from the month after submitting the form and may either claim a refund of contributions you have made, transfer your contributions to another scheme, or defer your choice and keep your service with LGPS in the expectation that you may return to membership later. You will be contacted by the scheme within several weeks of opting out with the options available to you, or you may contact them in advance at pensions@hants.gov.uk or 01962 845588 (please have your National Insurance number to hand to help identify yourself). Opting out after 2 or more years’ membership: You cease paying contributions in the fol

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Pensions Human Resources LGPS TIMELINE: DURING YOUR MEMBERSHIP With your LGPS pension, you don’t need to worry about investment options or market performance - you’ll get 1/49th of your pensionable earnings added to your annual pension while you work. However, you must still keep track of your pension value and keep personal details (including your beneficiaries) up to date using your Online Member Portal. Each year (sometime before 31st August) you will receive an ‘Annual Benefit Statement’ via your Member Portal. This is the best way for you to track the value of your pension. It will show the benefits you’ve earned so far and also give a forecast of what it would be worth if you continued at your current pay up until your normal retirement date, and indicate the tax-free lump sum you can choose to take. Consider options to add to your pension when you are able to do so. Having a Baby or Adopting? During paid occupational and statutory parental leave your pension will continue to build up as if you were working normally. However, you won’t build up pension at all during unpaid leave. This includes parental leave during the unpaid stages of Maternity, Adoption or Shared Parental (normally months 9 to 12). To top this up again, take a look at the 'Unpaid Leave' section below. Unpaid Leave Your won’t build up pension during unpaid leave but may choose to top this up again with a Shared Cost Additional Pension Contribution or 'standard' Additional Pension Contribution. If you submit a request within 30 days of your unpaid leave ending, you are entitled to a 'Shared Cost Additional Pension' where you pay 1/3 of the cost, and the Universit

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Pensions Human Resources LGPS TIMELINE: ADDING TO YOUR PENSION You have the option to further increase the value of your pension by making additional payments when you feel able to do so. You can do this at any stage of your membership and it will often benefit you to do this as early as possible. Are you approaching retirement and want to boost your pension while you are still earning? Are you thinking about retiring early and want to offset some of the reductions you take when doing so? Are you looking to benefit even more from the tax savings granted on pension contributions? Do you have a projected pension you're aiming for and want to ensure you stay on track? Pensions are incredibly tax efficient, meaning that standard rate tax payers may save 20% on tax the contributions they make, and higher-rate payers can save 40%. When selecting added pension through our 'Salary Sacrifice' AVCs you can also add up to 8% in National Insurance savings on top of that. Of course, as with any investment you have to wait to see the return on investments, and with pensions the downside is having to wait until you retire (which for some will be a long time away!). However, for those that are in a position to do so, paying even more towards your pension is a great way to invest in your future. There are a few different options you have to consider for doing this. Additional Pension Contributions (APCs): Make extra contributions to your LGPS pension to increase your annual payments when you retire. Arrange them as regular or one-off payments, via monthly payroll or with direct payments to the scheme Additional Voluntary Contributions (AVCs): Invest in

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Pensions Human Resources LGPS TIMELINE: LEAVING THE UNIVERSITY If you are looking at employment options outside of the University, make sure you add Reward and Pensions to the equation. LGPS is one of the best schemes available, with the University paying over 20% of your salary in employer contributions towards your pension and all the other LGPS benefits you automatically receive as a member. If you do leave us before taking your pension, what happens next will depend on how long you were a member. If you have less than 2 years membership, this may mean your contributions could be refunded, transferred to another scheme or if you have over 2 years in the scheme your pension will become ‘deferred’ until you decide to retire and take your pension later. Find out what will happen in your circumstances on the Hants LGPS website. If you have been a member for long enough to stay in the scheme, after leaving you must still keep your personal details up to date (including your beneficiaries), and you’ll continue receiving an Annual Benefit Statement for your ‘deferred’ pension each year via the membership portal. Thinking of Transferring Out? after you leave?LGPS is one of the best (and safest) pension schemes available. Do some research, get some advice, know the risks, and make sure you understand what option is best for you. DISCLAIMERThe University of Portsmouth is not your pension provider and many queries you have may be best directed to the schemes themselves, where they have dedicated member helplines to assist you. These pages are for general informational purposes only and nothing contained within may be seen as financial advice.

body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Pensions Human Resources LGPS TIMELINE: RETIREMENT Deciding to retire is one of the biggest decisions you’ll make. Thankfully, LGPS offers different options to suit your circumstances. Age Retirement – your full pension paid when you retire and leave work on or after your normal pension age. If you choose to keep working, your pension will continue to increase but you must start taking it before turning 75. If you retire and decide you want to, you can still apply to return to UoP later under a new contract. Early Retirement – leave work and take the pension you’ve earned so far before your normal pension age. Because your pension is being paid to you earlier than expected, the annual amount will be reduced as it’s being paid earlier and for a longer period. You may retire as early as age 55, increasing to age 57 in April 2028. If you have membership in an LGPS Final Salary scheme, this part of your pension has a retirement age of 65, which may be earlier than your Career Average section 'normal pension age'. Where this is the case, reductions would be worked out separately based on each scheme age, so it's possible to take your Final Salary at 65 or later without reduction, with only the Career Average part reduced if you haven't reached the Career Average date at that time. If you have been in LGPS a long time, part of your pension may be protected from these early retirement reductions under what is called the '85-year rule'. If you were a member before 1 October 2006, have reached age 60, and your combined age and length of membership (in whole years) add up to 85 or more you should speak with LGPS to confirm what this means for yo

This is the best way for you to track the value of your pension. It will show the benefits you’ve earned so far and also give a forecast of what it would be worth if you continued your membership until your normal retirement date, and indicates the maximum tax-free lump sum you could choose to take. These are published online through your online Member Portal by 31 August each year, covering the previous financial year (April through March). If you didn't join the scheme until after 31 March, you won't have a statement until next year. Our LGPS Administrator Hampshire Pension Services offer instructions and support for accessing and understanding your statement so be sure to use the below links if there's anything you aren't familiar with: Sign in or Register for your Member Portal (queries can be sent using the “My Message” system once logged in) Help logging in or to request your statement in an alternative format - pensions@hants.gov.uk Understanding the numbers and FAQ - https://www.hants.gov.uk/hampshire-services/pensions/local-government/about-the-scheme/annual-benefit-statements PowerPoint guide and walkthrough - https://documents.hants.gov.uk/pensions/Yourpension-yourfuture-ABS-presentation2025.pptx We'd encourage you to look through and compare with your statement for the previous year, if you have one. Remember that any additional earnings or changes to pay you've had since 31 March won't be accounted for, nor any pensio

body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Pensions Human Resources LGPS & Absences Family Leave (Maternity, Paternity, Shared Parental, Adoption, Parental Bereavement) During paid or statutory paid family leave, you will continue to earn pension at your usual (unreduced) rate. You will still pay contributions on whatever pay you do receive. Periods of unpaid Family does not count as pensionable service and pension will not accrue during while you are unpaid. However, you may elect to buy back your ‘Lost Pension’ during this period (see below). If you are in the 50/50 arrangement (where you pay half contributions for half the pension accrual) and move to nil pay for any reason while still in ‘Ordinary’ family leave periods (normally the first 26 weeks), you’ll automatically be moved back to the main (full) arrangement even though you won’t be making contributions. You may, however, choose to elect back into the 50/50 arrangement again following your return to work. Other Family Leave (Antenatal, Premature Birth, Neonatal) Paid period for time off as a result of Antenatal, Premature Birth, or Neonatal care will be pensionable, while unpaid periods will not be pensionable. You may elect to buy back any ‘Lost Pension’ during the unpaid period (see below). Keeping in Touch (KIT Days / SPLIT Days) 'KIT Days' and 'Shared Parental Leave In Touch Days' are credited as pensionable service. You will pay pension contributions on whatever you earn. Sickness Absence While you are on authorised sick leave, your pension will keep accruing at normal rate and will count as pensionable service – even while you are not being paid. You will still pay contributions on whatever pay you do receive. I

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Pensions Human Resources LGPS Ill Health Retirement Employees with at least 2 years of membership in the Local Government Pension Scheme (LGPS) who become unable to continue working due to illness may qualify for ‘Ill Health Retirement’ (IHR). Assessment for IHR is done by an independent occupational health physician appointed by our occupational health provider, who considers medical evidence and assesses your immediate and future prospects to undertake work. The independent assessor will issue a report to UoP, who make a final decision on IHR in accordance with pension scheme rules. If approved, the next step will be agreeing a final day of service and your ill-health pension will start the day after you leave. LGPS Official Guidance and Policy You should read through the Hants LGPS and LGPS Member pages, which contain information on eligibility and terms, appeals and the official scheme guidance. Ill Health Retirement Application Process It is likely that those considering IHR will already be in discussion with management, HR, and our Occupational Health provider about their health and attendance. If you think this might apply to you, you should raise this with them in the first instance, and you will be guided through the process by a member of HR. Ill Health Retirement - Tiers of Payment To be approved for IHR, you have to be assessed as: Being permanently incapable of carrying out your current job Having a reduced likelihood of being able to take gainful employment (working 30 hours a week in any job for at least a year) before your normal pension age How much ‘reduced likelihood’ there is will inform a ‘Tier’ of IHR which determ

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Pensions Human Resources Buying Extra Pension Teachers' Pension offer a few different ways to add extra value to your pension benefits. Read through the options below to see if any suit your circumstances. Faster Accrual Faster Accrual allows you to accrue pension at a higher proportion of your salary by increasing the contributions you pay each month. This is done over a whole year between April and March, and you typically need to apply no later than January to guarantee it will be processed in time for the coming year. Additional Pension Buying Additional Pension allows you to boost your Annual Pension value. You can buy Additional Pension by either paying a lump sum or regular contributions deducted from your earnings. You have to purchase a minimum of a £250 increase to Annual Pension (paid every year of retirement), so bear in mind that this will cost multiple times that amount to purchase, but thankfully you can split the cost over multiple years if you need to. The cost will also vary depending on how old you are at point of purchase. Teachers’ provide a calculator online to help work this out. Additional Voluntary Contributions (AVC) The Teachers’ Pension AVC scheme allows you to build up a separate flexible pension pot with Prudential. You’ll make separate contributions from your monthly earnings, the amount you contribute is invested according to your choices and its value can go up and down over time depending on how well those investments preform. These AVCs are linked to TP, but you don’t have to take them at the same time as your normal benefits, and can even take them flexibly while continuing to work. You may also vary

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Pensions Human Resources Teachers’ Pension Ill Health Retirement Members with at least 2 years of qualifying service in the scheme who may be permanently unable to continuing teaching due to illness may be eligible for Ill Health Retirement (IHR). Members with between one and two years of service are not eligible for IHR but may apply for the benefits earned so far as a lump sum payment if they are in ‘serious ill health’ (see below). It is important you check you have access to your Teachers’ Pension ‘MyPensionOnline’ account and that all your personal details and beneficiaries are up-to-date as you begin this process. Scheme Guidance and Policy You should read through the Teachers’ Pension IHR pages, which contain the official information on eligibility and terms, forms to complete, appeals and the scheme guide. Considering IHR It is likely that those considering IHR will already be in discussion with management, HR, or our Occupational Health provider about their health and attendance. If you think IHR might apply to you, you should raise this with them in the first instance, so you can be supported through the process. Applications must be based on medical evidence. This may be from assessment with the University’s occupational health provider, or from your own personal doctors or qualified assessors. Application Process If you have decided to apply for IHR, you are required to fill in two PDF forms to send to us and your medical assessor(s). We recommend reading the forms through entirely as they contain helpful instructions and guidance that isn’t easily found in the other policy documents. If necessary, an appropriate representa

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Pensions Human Resources TPS Transitional Protection (also known as the 'McCloud' remedy) This section is only relevant to Teachers' Pensions members who were in the scheme or another public pension scheme (such as LGPS, NHS or Civil Service pension but not USS) on or before 31 March 2012. Between 2015 and 2022, public sector pension members (including Teachers' Pension) in 'Final Salary' pensions were moved (transitioned) to a new 'Career Average' pension with a different set of term and conditions and calculations for how pension value was determined. The date people moved to the new scheme (or if they moved at all) was determined by the age of the member. These terms could be more favourable or less favourable depending on the circumstances of the individual, which led to the process being appealed. As age alone was used to determine these transition dates, this process was found to be discriminatory by the Court of Appeal. As part of the remedy to rectify this, affected Teachers' Pension members can now choose which scheme (Final Salary or Career Average) they wish to be a member of between 1 April 2015 and 31 March 2022. Initially, all members who previously had moved to Career Average have been put back to Final Salary as if they had never left for this period, and all members then move to Career Average from 1 April 2022. However, affected members still have the choice to switch back again (which they must decide at point of applying for retirement). Teachers' Pension have written or are writing to all affected members to confirm how they are impacted, what options they have and when they will need to make decisions. While the o

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Pensions Human Resources Teachers' Pension & Absences Family Leave (Maternity, Paternity, Shared Parental and Adoption) The period when you are on family leave remains pensionable as long as you receive at least half pay or statutory pay. Pension contributions will be deducted using the same contribution rate as your usual salary, but the percentage only applies to your actual pensionable earnings in the period. Should your pay drop below half pay and you aren't receiving statutory pay, it is deemed you are no longer be in pensionable service. You won’t accrue further pension during the ‘not in pensionable service’ periods but you should still count as ‘in service’ for applications of Ill Health or Retirement and Death in Service calculations providing you have been ‘not in pensionable service’ for less than 12 months. Other Family Leave (Antenatal, Premature Birth, Neonatal, Parental Bereavement) Paid period for time off as a result of Antenatal, Premature Birth, Neonatal, or Parental Bereavement will be pensionable, while unpaid periods will not be pensionable. Sickness Absence Sick leave is deemed pensionable service as long as you are paid at least half of your salary relating to that employment. Your usual contribution rate is used but this applies to actual pensionable earnings in the period only. Sick leave will no longer be pensionable service once your pay falls below half of your usual salary (including statutory-only periods). You won’t pay contributions on less-than-half pay either. You won’t accrue further pension during the ‘not in service’ periods but you should still count as ‘in service’ for applications of Ill Health

body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Pensions Human Resources MyPensionPlus - Flexible Benefit Option Modules This page is for current members of the MyPensionPlus (MPP) pension and benefits scheme. If you are not sure whether this applies to you, check your contract and payslip to see what scheme you are paying into - if you see contributions for 'MPP' or 'MyPensionPlus' on your latest payslip, this page is for you. Where you are a member of another scheme, please instead return to our Reward front page to see the many other benefit options available to you. Eligibility to select benefits If you are an active member of MyPensionPlus, you must select one of a range of additional benefit options each year, at no cost to yourself, to personalise your scheme as suits you best. These benefits take effect from 1 April and apply for a full year while you remain a member. You will be asked to make your selection in an annual Options Window, opening each January for all members who joined the scheme on or before 31 December, and who are expected to remain in post beyond 1 April. If you don't make a choice, or if you joined the scheme outside the eligibility for the Options Window, then you will remain in our default benefit option (see latest guidance below) until the following year's Options Window opens. These options may change each year as new benefits may become available, so be sure to read the latest guide as it becomes available. During this window, you also have an opportunity to make changes to how, and how much, you pay into another big part of MyPensionPlus - the pension itself. We'll confirm your selection after the Options Window closes, and be in touch later to pro

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Pensions Human Resources MyPensionPlus Timeline: Becoming a Member New Employment Contracts: MyPensionPlus is our benefits and pension scheme for University of Portsmouth Academic Services Limited (UASL) employees who are salaried (not paid via fees or timesheets) on a permanent/indefinite or fixed-term basis. If you are not sure if you are eligible, check your contract and look at the section for ‘Pension’. The terms of enrolment will depend on the duration of your contract. If your employment contract is for more than six months durationYou will be contractually enrolled in the scheme from your contract start date If your employment contract is for a duration of less than six monthsYou will not be contractually enrolled when you start, but may voluntarily opt into the pension scheme at any time If you start on a contract for less than six months but are later extended beyond thisIf you are not already in the scheme at this point, you will be contractually enrolled from the 1st day of the month following extension You are not eligible to join if you are aged 75 or over, or if you are not normally resident in the UK. Auto Enrolment: You may alternatively be automatically enrolled, where you were not in a pension but met the government’s assessment to be auto-enrolled. This happens where you: were not, at time of assessment, in a pension scheme for this employment have not previously opted-out of a pension in this employment, or opted-out more than 12 months before an auto-enrolment ‘staging date’, which happens every 3 years earned over a minimum amount (currently £833) in a single month are aged between 22 and your State Pension Age W

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Pensions Human Resources MyPensionPlus Timeline: Pension Contributions We have partnered with Aviva to provide their ‘Workplace Pension’ to all ‘MyPensionPlus’ members. You will contribute a percentage of your salary towards your pension, and we do the same. These funds are then invested according to your choices. From the first day you join, we will pay employer contributions of 12% of your pensionable salary to your pension. You'll pay a contribution on your salary too, which will default to 6%. You'll benefit from an immediate tax saving on whatever you contribute, and your contributions have the chance to grow over time with investments. Changing how much you contribute and 'Employer Matching' You may choose to contribute as little as 3% of your pensionable salary each month. Where you can contribute more, we will match your extra contributions to a maximum employer amount of 15%. Your Contribution Our Contribution 3% (Minimum) 9% (Minimum) 4% 10% 5% 11% 6% (Default) 12% (Default) 7% 13% 8% 14% 9% 15% (Maximum) Above 9% 15% (Maximum) What counts as pensionable pay? Pensionable pay includes salary and London Allowance only. All other allowances, performance payments (including Living Our Values Awards), supplements and claimable elements are not pensionable. Paying your Contributions When you join the scheme, you will make contributions to your pension via one of two options - 'Salary Sacrifice' or 'Basic Deductions'. The default (and a beneficial option for most) is to pay via Salary Sacrifice, but you can choose to switch to the Basic model if you wish. Many members would benefit from the 'Salary Sacrifice' method by saving both t

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Pensions Human Resources MyPensionPlus Timeline: Building your Pension The 'MyPensionPlus' Aviva Company Pension is a ‘defined contributions’ or 'money purchase' scheme. That means your own pot of money is invested according to choices you make, and has a chance to grow (and keep growing) until you choose to retire. The earlier you invest, the longer it has with a chance to grow – that means decisions you make near the start of your career will normally have even more impact than those nearer the end… Investing You choose, from a range of options, how your funds are invested. This is an important decision, although you can change your options later. The fund or funds you invest in may have different aims and levels of ‘risk verses reward’, or may align with different ethics or beliefs. Aviva explains some background on how investment works in their scheme guide and their investment guide gives information on the different types and aims of investment funds and the levels of risk involved. A little confused? Don’t worry, you are in good company! Aviva aims to make these decisions as simple and transparent as possible. If you aren't ready to make a choice you can begin in a ‘default’ investment plan, which can be a good place to start until you want to consider further. The next most straightforward choice is to select one of Aviva’s ‘Investment Approaches’. These are a blend of investments which intend to align with broad intentions of a pension, such as short or long term focus, high or low risk, lump sums or regular monthly payments, and can automatically adapt over time depending on how close you are to retirement. Finally, more expe

Read our Contributions page to understand what this means and to consider whether you need to switch. Next, make sure you find some time to read through the Aviva Workplace Pension guide to understand the benefits of your Aviva scheme and how it works, and the Investment Guide to see what options you have to invest your pension. Registering Online We will set you up as a new member towards the end of the first month you'll be paying contributions in Aviva will normally send you two ‘welcome packs’ by post to your home address with your pension plan information, including instructions on how to opt out, if this is something you feel you need to consider (note that if you wish to opt out and want a refund, you’ll need to do this within 30 days of Aviva posting you the ‘welcome pack’ so will need to act quickly) Once you have your welcome packs, go to Aviva’s MyAccount to register for online access to your pension and follow the instructions on screen. Enter the Policy Number you have been provided, so it matches you up with our workplace scheme. It is recommended to use a personal email so access is readily available at home, and after you leave or retire Some of your personal details should be pre-filled, such as name, NI number and address. Aviva’s terms and conditions are that you must check these are correct when you first sign in You will be required to verify your account by provid

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Pensions Human Resources MyPensionPlus Timeline: During your Membership You should keep logging in regularly to see your contributions and investments, track how these perform and grow over time, make fund changes if you choose to, and always keep personal details up to date whenever they change. You’ll also get an Annual Statement to review each year summarising your contributions and investments. Flexible Benefit Options Your membership of MyPensionPlus affords you an additional Flexible Benefit Option at no cost to yourself. In January / February each year, you'll get to review the options available and pick the one that interests you most, to take effect from April to March. If you don't pick an option, or if you join outside the Options window, then you'll be enrolled in whatever our 'default' option is until the next year. Find out more here. Consider additional contributions If you are in a position to contribute more to your pension, it is one of the best ways to invest in your future. You can make one-off or regular payments directly to Aviva and immediately benefit from 20% tax relief added on top. You’ll pay these directly to Aviva from your personal savings or bank account. As you pay them separately, they won’t show up on your University payslip. Alternatively, you can increase the percentage of your earnings you contribute each month (saving NI costs as well as tax if you use Salary Sacrifice), directly from your salary. Depending on the rate you choose, we may pay extra to your account when you do too (see Contributions and Employer Matching). To do so, email pensionenquiries@port.ac.uk with your request and let us know

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Pensions Human Resources MyPensionPlus Timeline: Opting Out We recommend staying in a workplace pension wherever your circumstances allow it, as it’s one of the best ways to invest in your future. However, should you need to, you may choose to opt out of the scheme. If you do opt out, you will stop paying into your pension and we won’t pay in either, and you will lose access to all other benefits and selectable benefit options in the scheme, including ‘Death in Service’ Life Insurance. Please consider this carefully and make sure you choose the right option for your circumstances. Opting out within 30 days of registration: If you inform Aviva you wish to opt out within 30 days of registration, they’ll refund us any contributions you paid, and we’ll refund you in your next payslip. Note that the 30-day period starts on the date your second Aviva 'Welcome Pack' was sent (check the date on the letter), not when you receive it, so you should do this as quickly as possible to receive a refund (see After you Join for more details on this welcome pack, registering online, and finding any other details you need to opt out). This letter should normally be posted to the home address you have given us within a week or so of the first payroll you make contributions in (for example, if you are enrolled on the first of the month but paid on the 28th, you should normally get the letter shortly after the 28th, and it may even be dated on the 26th or 27th). If you are urgently seeking to opt out with a refund and haven't had this letter within the expected time, let us know at pensionenquiries@port.ac.uk. Requests must be submitted via Aviva's opt out

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Pensions Human Resources MyPensionPlus Timeline: Resignation If you are looking at employment options outside of the University, make sure you add Reward and Pensions to the equation. We are proud of our MyPensionPlus scheme, with us paying a starting rate of 12% employer contributions towards your pension with a Salary Sacrifice option, 3 times salary Death Benefit, a choice of flexible rewards at no additional cost, on top of all the other benefits you receive as an employee. If you do leave us before taking your pension, your employee and employer contributions will remain in your Aviva pension pot, and will continue to be invested with the same opportunity to keep growing until your retirement. You will not be able to pay new contributions via salary, and of course we will not contribute further either. However, you may convert our workplace pension to a ‘personal one’ and continue to make contributions directly to Aviva. There are some restrictions and minimum limits on this so check your options directly with Aviva. You may also wish to consider changing your investment options now your circumstances have changed – particularly if you had selected an Investment Plan focused on longer-term membership. You should still log in regularly after you leave to keep track of your pension value, update your personal details and beneficiaries, and to review your Annual Statements. Thinking of transferring from Aviva to another scheme after you leave?Do some research, know the risks, and we strongly advise you seek financial advice to make sure you understand what option is best for you. There are some restrictions on what schemes you may tr

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Pensions Human Resources MyPensionPlus Timeline: Retirement With your MyPensionPlus Aviva pension, you may choose to take retirement benefits whether you are intending to leave work or not, and even while you continue working full time (although this may mean a higher proportion of pension income could be taxable). The ‘Minimum Pension Age’ to be able to take your pension is currently 55 years old but due to increase to 57 on 5 April 2028. You must take your Aviva pension no later than your 75th birthday. There is no default retirement age under this scheme; however, pension scheme investment modelling will, by default, assume you intend to draw your pension at your state pension age. You will have several options when taking your pension, and may wish to seek free impartial advice from Pension Wise (part of the government-funded Money Helper service), from Aviva, or from an alternative advice service. You may draw all or only some of your pension, and you can decide how to do that by choosing a lump sum, taking individual payments as you need them, or by exchanging for regular income for life (known as an annuity). 25% of the total value of your pension(s) can be accessed ‘tax free’ when drawing lump sums or individual payments. If you purchase an annuity, then normal income tax applies. Let us know your intended retirement date (whether your are leaving employment or not) via a People Hub ‘Pay Enquiry’, and we’ll make sure everything gets done on our side. More information on your options and how this works is contained in the scheme guide or via the Aviva Help Line. DISCLAIMERThe University of Portsmouth and its subsidiaries are not

body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Pensions Human Resources MyPensionPlus: Aviva Pension & Absences Family Leave (Maternity, Paternity, Shared Parental and Adoption Leave) During paid family leave you will pay contributions on the actual earnings you receive. While you are eligible for Statutory Family Leave pay, the University will contribute based on the ‘normal’ pay you had prior to going on reduced pay. If you move to unpaid leave, neither you nor the University will make further payments until you return to work. However, if you move to unpaid leave for any reason while still in ‘Ordinary’ family leave periods (normally the first 26 weeks), such as by not being entitled to statutory pay, the University will continue making its contributions while you don’t have to. Your funds will continue to be invested (including during unpaid periods) so may increase or fall in value depending on how the investment choices you have made perform. If you move to statutory pay only while using a 'Salary Sacrifice' contributions arrangement, this will cease and you'll move to 'Basic Deductions' instead. See below for further details. Other Family Leave (Antenatal, Premature Birth, Neonatal, Parental Bereavement) You will continue to make pension contributions on any earnings or statutory pay you receive. Keeping in Touch (KIT Days / SPLIT Days) You will pay contributions on payment for any KIT days during your Family Leave. Sickness Absence You will continue to pay pension contributions during sickness absence on whatever earnings you are paid, and we will do same too. This includes any statutory sick pay you receive. If you move to unpaid sickness, you and we will not make contribu

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Pensions Human Resources Your UASL Nest TIMELINE - what you need to know about your pension How you Join - Starting your Contract You will not join Nest automatically from your start date; however, you can request to join at any time. Additionally, as you enter the third calendar month of a contract, we'll start assessing you under the government’s pension ‘auto-enrolment’ criteria and you will join Nest automatically if/when you qualify for it (see ‘Auto-Enrolment’ below). How you Join - By Request If you are eligible to join but are not in the scheme yet, you can ask us to enrol you. Please email your request to pensionenquiries@port.ac.uk, and you may join the scheme as soon as the first day of the following month. How you Join - Auto-Enrolment We will assess you for Automatic Enrolment in the third calendar month of your contract and will automatically enrol you if you qualify. Find out more at UASL Auto-Enrolment. Once you Join You will receive a welcome pack in the post inviting you to sign up for NEST’s online member account. This pack will explain how the pension works and the options you have with your membership. It will normally be sent to your home address by post within five working days of them setting up your account. It will also provide details on how you can opt-out or leave the scheme, if you choose to. Leaving the Scheme We would always recommend staying in a workplace pension if your circumstances allow it - it’s one of the best ways to invest in your future. However, if you can't or don’t want to be in a pension, what happens when you request to leave depends on how long you have been a member. Leaving the Scheme

body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Pensions Human Resources Nest (except UASL Nest) Auto Enrolment You have been enrolled in the Nest Pension Scheme, as you have been assessed as meeting the government’s criteria for ‘automatic enrolment’. UASL Nest is a ‘Defined Contribution’ or ‘Money Purchase’ workplace pension. The contributions you and the University make are paid to your personal ‘pension pot’, and are invested according to your wishes. You may find out more information about what options you have and the benefits of being in this scheme on our scheme guide, and on the Nest website. Nest will send you a new joiner welcome pack by post, normally within a few days after you first get paid. This will invite you to register online and also provide details on how to opt out, if you choose to (see below for more information on opting out). Contributions The default rate of contributions for members is 3%, with a 2.4% deduction from your monthly basic salary and an extra 0.6% added to your pension pot as tax relief. If you wish, you may increase the amount you pay. The employer contribution is 6%. To find out more about contributions and tax relief, please refer to the scheme guide. Opting Out We would encourage you to think carefully before deciding to opt out of a pension and ensure that it is the right decision for you. There is an initial Opt-Out period, which lasts one month starting three working days after your enrolment, during which time you may elect to opt-out. You must apply to NEST directly. Your Welcome Pack from Nest will confirm the window you have to opt-out of the scheme and provide instructions on how to do so. Nest will then close your account and ins

Your funds will continue to be invested (including during unpaid periods) so may increase or fall in value depending on how the investment choices you have made perform. title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Pensions Human Resources NHS & Absence Sickness Absence You will continue to earn NHS pension at your normal rate while on paid, reduced pay, or statutory pay sickness absence. Contributions will be deducted at the normal rate on whatever pay you earn. If you move to nil-pay absence, you won’t earn further pension but will still be considered ‘in service’ for any ‘Death in Service’ benefits. Family Leave (Maternity, Paternity, Shared Parental and Adoption Leave) When you are on Family leave, you continue to be in pensionable service and will accrue pension at your normal rates. You will continue to pay contributions on whatever you earn. If you move to nil-pay Family Leave, you will continue to earn pension providing you continue to pay contributions based on the rate of pay immediately before the period of unpaid leave begins. That means you may need to pay these backdated contributions when you return to paid work. You may instead request not to pay these, but would be considered out of service for the unpaid period. Other Family Leave (Antenatal, Premature Birth, Neonatal, Parental Bereavement) Paid period for time off as a result of Antenatal care, Premature Birth, Neonatal, or Parental Bereavement will be pensionable while any unpaid period will not be pensionable. Keeping in Touch (KIT Days / SPLIT Days) You will pay pension contributions for any KIT days during your Family Leave. This will be credited as pensionable earnings. Other Authorised Unpaid Leave For other types of authorised unpaid leave, you can choose whether to continue pension payments and have the period count as pensionable service. If you do, you need to notify

So long as you are eligible, you will have been contractually enrolled into Aviva and don’t need to do anything else to join. If you are not sure if you are eligible, check your contract and look at the section for ‘Pension’. You are not eligible to join if you are aged 75 or over. Auto Enrolment: You may alternatively have been automatically enrolled, where you were not in a pension but meet the government’s assessment to be auto-enrolled. This happens where you: were not, at time of assessment, in a pension scheme for this employment have not previously opted-out of a pension in this employment, or opted-out more than 12 months before an auto-enrolment ‘staging date’, which happens every 3 years earned over a minimum amount (currently £833) in a single month are aged between 22 and your State Pension Age We'll always let you know if you are ever auto enrolled. Opted out previously but now want to re-join? Just let us know by submitting a People Hub 'Pay Enquiry'. You can start from as soon as the first of the next month. DISCLAIMERThe University of Portsmouth is not your pension provider and many queries you have may be best directed to the schemes themselves, where they have dedicated member helplines to assist you. These pages are for general informational purposes only and nothing contained within may be seen as financial advice. body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Pensions Human Resources Aviva for London Campus Timeline: Contributions From the first day you join, we will pay contributions of 10% of your salary towards your Aviva Company Pension account. This is calculated on your gross monthly salary (meaning before tax and National Insurance deductions). You'll pay a contribution on your salary too and Aviva then automatically refund 20% of the tax you paid straight to your pension account. When you join, you’ll be set up to contribute 4.8% of your pay to your pension, so another 1.2% will be added as the refund of tax to give a total of 6%, or 16% when combined with the employer payment. You’ll be able to check these contributions each month on your payslip. …so what counts as salary? We apply your percentage contributions to the whole of your salary each month, before deductions of tax and National Insurance. If you have a London Allowance, that is pensionable too. Contributions from backdated pay following promotions or pay rises will be made in the month they are paid. All other payments are non-pensionable, and will not result in any additional contributions being made. DISCLAIMERThe University of Portsmouth is not your pension provider and many queries you have may be best directed to the schemes themselves, where they have dedicated member helplines to assist you. These pages are for general informational purposes only and nothing contained within may be seen as financial advice.
