body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Pensions Human Resources Aviva for London Campus Timeline: During your Membership You should keep logging in regularly to see your contributions and investments, track how these perform and grow over time, make fund changes if you choose to, and always keep personal details up to date whenever they change. You’ll also get an Annual Statement to review each year summarising your contributions and investments. Consider additional contributions If you are in a position to contribute more to your pension, it is one of the best options to invest in your future. You may make a one-off payment to your pension at any time, and immediately benefit from 20% tax relief when while your overall pension contributions remain below 100% of your relevant UK earnings. There are some additional limits on tax - find out more on our Tax & Pensions page. You can make one-off or regular payments to your pension. You’ll pay these directly to Aviva from your personal savings or bank account. As you pay them separately, they won’t show up on your University payslip. Alternatively, you can request to increase the percentage you pay each month on your Portsmouth earnings. You must email pensionenquiries@port.ac.uk with your request and let us know the percentage you wish to pay. You can do this from as soon as start of the following month. Higher Rate Taxpayer? Do you pay income tax on your UASL London earnings at a Higher Rate? If so, you should complete an annual self-assessment tax return with HMRC each year to claim back any additional tax you paid on your contributions. For contributions paid at 40% tax, 20% will have been added to your pension pot already,

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Pensions Human Resources Aviva for London Campus Timeline: Opting Out You may feel you need to opt out of your pension, however we would always recommend you remain in scheme whenever you can. Please consider this carefully and make sure you choose the right option for your circumstances. Opting out within 30 days of registration: If you inform Aviva you wish to opt out within 30 days of registration, they’ll refund us any contributions you paid, and we’ll refund you in your next payslip. Note that the 30-day period starts on the date your second Aviva 'Welcome Pack' was sent (check the date on the letter), not when you receive it, so you should do this as quickly as possible to receive a refund (see After you Join for more details on this welcome pack, registering online, and finding any other details you need to opt out). This letter should normally be posted to the home address you have given within a week or so of the first payroll you make contributions in (for example, if you are enrolled on the first of the month but paid on the 28th, you should normally get the letter shortly after the 28th, and it may even be dated on the 26th or 27th). If you are urgently seeking to opt out with a refund and haven't had this letter within the expected time, let us know at pensionenquiries@port.ac.uk. Requests must be submitted via Aviva's opt out page or by calling 0800 145 5744 once you have your confirmed plan number (found on your 'Welcome Pack'). Please be aware we cannot do this on your behalf so you must go directly to Aviva. Exiting the scheme after 30 days: After 30 days, you aren’t technically ‘opting out’ but are leaving the scheme

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Pensions Human Resources Aviva for London Campus Timeline: Resignation If you are looking at employment options outside of the University, make sure you add Reward and Pensions to the equation. We are proud of our London Aviva scheme, with us paying 10% employer contributions towards your pension on top of all the other benefits you receive as an employee. If you do leave us before taking your pension, your employee and employer contributions will remain in your Aviva pension pot, and will continue to be invested with the same opportunity to keep growing until your retirement. You will not be able to pay new contributions via salary, and of course we will not contribute further either. However, you may convert our workplace pension to a ‘personal one’ and continue to make contributions directly to Aviva. There are some restrictions and minimum limits on this so check your options directly with Aviva. You may also wish to consider changing your investment options now your circumstances have changed – particularly if you had selected an Investment Plan focused on longer-term membership. You should still log in regularly after you leave to keep track of your pension value, update your personal details and beneficiaries, and to review your Annual Statements. Thinking of transferring from Aviva to another scheme after you leave?Do some research, know the risks, and we strongly advise you seek financial advice to make sure you understand what option is best for you. There are some restrictions on what schemes you may transfer to so check with Aviva first to see what is possible. DISCLAIMERThe University of Portsmouth is not your pension prov

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Pensions Human Resources Aviva for London Campus Timeline: Retirement With Aviva, you may choose to take retirement benefits whether you are intending to leave work or not, and even while you continue working full time (although this may mean a higher proportion of pension income could be taxable). The ‘Minimum Pension Age’ to take your pension is currently 55 years old but it is due to increase to 57 on 5 April 2028. You must take your pension no later than your 75th birthday. You may draw all or only some of your pension, and you can decide how to do that by choosing a lump sum, taking individual payments as you need them, or by exchanging for regular income for life (known as an annuity). 25% of the total value of your pension(s) can be accessed ‘tax free’ when drawing lump sums or individual payments. If you purchase an annuity, then normal income tax applies. Let us know your intended retirement date (whether your are leaving employment or not) via a People Hub ‘Pay Enquiry’, and we’ll make sure everything gets done on our side. More information on your options and how this works is contained in the scheme guide or via the Aviva Help Line. DISCLAIMERThe University of Portsmouth is not your pension provider and many queries you have may be best directed to the schemes themselves, where they have dedicated member helplines to assist you. These pages are for general informational purposes only and nothing contained within may be seen as financial advice. Where you have a decision to make regarding your pension, we would always recommend you pursue Independent Financial Advice, with a qualified advisor who is familiar with pensions an

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Pensions Human Resources Aviva for London Campus Pension & Absences Family Leave (Maternity, Paternity, Shared Parental and Adoption Leave) During paid family leave you will pay contributions on the actual earnings you receive. If you move to reduced pay or statutory-only pay, the University will contribute based on the ‘normal’ pay you had prior to going on reduced pay. If you move to unpaid leave, neither you nor the University will make further payments until you return to work. However, if you move to unpaid leave for any reason while still in ‘Ordinary’ family leave periods (normally the first 26 weeks), such as by not being entitled to statutory pay, the University will continue making contributions while you don’t have to. Your funds will continue to be invested (including during unpaid periods) so may increase or fall in value depending on how the investment choices you have made perform. Other Family Leave (Antenatal, Premature Birth, Neonatal, Parental Bereavement) You will continue to make pension contributions on any earnings or statutory pay you receive. Keeping in Touch (KIT Days / SPLIT Days) You will pay contributions on payment for any KIT days during your Family Leave. Sickness Absence You will continue to pay pension contributions during sickness absence on whatever earnings you are paid, and UoP will do same too. This includes any statutory sick pay you receive. If you move to unpaid sickness, you and UoP will not make contributions. Your funds will continue to be invested (including during unpaid periods) so may increase or fall in value depending on how the investment choices you have made perform. DISCLAIMERThe U

body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Pensions Human Resources Pensions Awareness Week Pensions Awareness Week occurs each year, normally in the second or third week of September. Schemes, advocates and advisors across the industry come together to provide free events and learning opportunities so there’s never a better time to learn more about your pension and how it works, check up on the value you've earned, and give some thought to your future. Whatever scheme you are in at Portsmouth – Teachers’ Pension, LGPS, MyPensionPlus, NHS, Aviva London or NEST – your pension is one of the biggest and best benefits we are proud to offer. Each scheme works differently, so be sure to find some time to learn more by looking through the latest information and resources on offer. Are you not in a pension scheme yet, or have you opted out? We would always recommend joining up as soon as your circumstances allow it. Just email pensionenquiries@port.ac.uk and we’ll let you know what you need to do. Pensions Awareness Week 2025 - starting Monday 15 September All Members Pensions Awareness – book free-to-all pension talks running Monday through Wednesday, although these sessions won’t cover specific information for any schemes you are a member of. If you can’t attend them live, they are available to catch-up later. They also offer some articles, FAQs and links, covering the basics and beyond Don’t forget your State Pension, which most will be earning alongside the workplace schemes. Register for access via HMRC online services, and view your forecast. Learn how it works and if there are any gaps in national insurance contributions, you may be able to make a voluntary top up for a big boos

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Pensions Human Resources Tax & Pensions Annual Allowance What is it? The ‘Annual Allowance’ is the maximum that can be saved across all your pensions in a tax year (6th April to 5th April) without a tax change being applied. This combines your UoP pension(s), any added pension and AVCs, along with any other pensions you contribute to personally or through other employments or contracts. HMRC establish the value of the Annual Allowance, and it can change each year. Where this limit is exceeded, unused allowances from the previous 3 years can be applied first, but the remaining amount will be taxed at your income tax rate. Generally, this will be very unlikely to affect you unless you are a very high earner (more than £100,000) or have very high (more than £1,000 monthly) in extra contributions throughout the year but it is always your own responsibility to check and report this (or to do so through taking Financial Advice). What do you need to do? Your pension provider will normally tell you if you exceed the annual allowance with their pensions alone, but they won’t know if you will exceed it only after combining your AVCs and/or payments in other schemes. If you think there is any possibility this could affect you, you must find out more via the links below, ask your pension(s) for this value, consider Financial Advice, and if necessary report and pay the tax charges to HMRC. If you haven’t had any contact about Annual Allowance from your schemes or AVCs you paid into, you can request a pensions saving statement from them to get the amount of Annual Allowance used. How the value is determined is different depending on what type of pen

body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Pensions Human Resources Workplace Pension Auto Enrolment We will contact you if you are ever enrolled in a pension scheme under the government’s criteria for ‘automatic enrolment’ in your role. This means that you: Earned over a minimum amount (currently £833) in a single month Are aged between 22 and your State Pension Age Were not, at time of assessment, in a pension scheme for this contract Have not previously opted-out of a pension in this contract, or opted-out before the University’s latest organisational re-enrolment date, which happens approximately every 3 years The correspondence will make clear what workplace pension scheme you have joined, and date you have been enrolled from (normally the start of the month you qualified in), and you will see employee and employer contributions on your payslips from this date. You may be enrolled even if you already have a deferred pension, or if a pension is already being paid to you. If you want to know more about automatic enrolment, there is some further information on moneyhelper.org.uk. Depending on the pension you have been enrolled in, contributions rates (how much you and we will pay), benefits, effect on tax, and what you need to know will vary. Please follow the relevant link below for the pension scheme you have joined. MyPensionPlus UASL Nest Aviva for London Campus Local Government Pension Scheme (LGPS) Teachers' Pension Scheme (TPS) Nest (Other Subsidiaries except UASL Nest) Opting Out (leaving the scheme) We would encourage you to think carefully before deciding to opt out of a pension and ensure that it is the right decision for you. If you do still wish to opt out the ab

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Pensions Human Resources Purchasing Additional Holiday & Pensions If you are considering applying for additional time off under the Holiday Purchase scheme, you’ll have a choice to pay for it using a ‘salary sacrifice’ arrangement. Under salary sacrifice, your salary is temporarily reduced by the cost of the Holiday, allowing you to save money by not paying Income Tax or National Insurance on the amount sacrificed. There is more detail on this arrangement in the Holiday Purchase Scheme guide, which you should ensure you have read and understood first. One other impact of using salary sacrifice to purchase Holiday is that you won’t pay pension contributions on the amount reduced either. That does mean you’ll pay less, but that may not be what you are seeking if you wish to keep your pension accruing at the same rate. Thankfully, no matter what scheme you are in, there are voluntary options to pay extra so you can ‘top up’ your pension back to where it was, or by as much as you can afford. These aren’t limited to Additional Holiday Purchase either – you can look into them whether or not you buy time off with a salary sacrifice arrangement. Each scheme has different options and processes, so take a read through the relevant section for you below. MyPensionPlus Aviva and London Aviva pensions You can make one-off or regular payments to your pension directly to Aviva. Alternatively, you can request to increase the percentage you pay each month on your earnings by letting us know at pensionenquiries@port.ac.uk. See the section on ‘Consider additional contributions’ on our Aviva ‘During your Membership’ page for more details. Local Government

Three things tie together our plan for the future: Our ambitious mission Our vision for bringing it to life The values that define the way we work, collaborate and behave We've already made giant leaps towards our goals – we're developing our campus and becoming a more sustainable university, and the strategies and plans we have in place are ready to be explored below. body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Estates and Campus Services > Soft Services (Campus Environment) Estates and Campus Services Minibuses Driving Minibuses on University Business (UK) Only Every driver of a minibus used on University business must have completed the Driver Declaration to be lodged with the Transport Section. Only those members of staff over 25 years of age, 2 years driving experience and with a category D1 licence entitlement can drive minibuses on University business. All new minibus drivers are required to undergo a medical examination to be repeated at five-year intervals (unless otherwise stated). Upon successful completion of the medical, it is a requirement that minibus drivers are assessed with the Assessor of the Campus Environment Office. Paperwork for the assessment test is available from the Transport Section. The total cost of each assessment is £50.00 and this takes place subsequently at five yearly intervals (unless otherwise stated). People Carriers All staff are required to take a medical if wishing to drive a 7/8/9 seater people carrier. Please contact the Transport Section. Foreign licences Employees who hold a valid ?full? driving licence issued in the European Community or Iceland, Liechtenstein and Norway can be included under the Universities insurance policy. Licences issued by any other country need to be referred to the Transport Section prior to approval being given. Small Bus Permits (Section 19) The Section 19 permit scheme enables the University to make a nominal charge to passengers for providing transport without the need to obtain a PSV Operators License. Contributions must not exceed a level to recover the costs of running the minibus, in

body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Estates and Campus Services > Soft Services (Campus Environment) Estates and Campus Services Vehicles owned by Employees It is the responsibility of every driver to make sure they have adequate insurance for their own private cars used for University business. This includes travelling between different sites. Most private motor policies restrict use to social, domestic and pleasure purposes only. Employees using their own vehicles on University business will need to extend their personal insurance to include business use (including passenger cover). When completing your expenses claim (FIN 1) you are required to sign a declaration confirming appropriate cover is in place. There is often no charge for this extension. However, please be warned: Some insurers provide quotations for social, domestic and pleasure purposes only, plus commuting to and from the employee’s normal place of work. This is not the same as “business use” and will not cover the occasional trip to a meeting elsewhere. If you are named as a driver on someone else’s motor insurance (e.g. spouse), please check that business use is available to the appropriate person. The University is unable to accept any liability in the event of an insurance claim being made. Further advice can be obtained from the Insurance Officer: Anita Randell, Insurance Officer University of Portsmouth Finance Department, University House, Winston Churchill Avenue, Portsmouth, PO1 2UP. title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Conditions of employment Human Resources Research Passports Pre-Engagement Checklist These notes should be read in conjunction with the “Research in the NHS: HR Good Practice Resource Pack”. 1. Employment screening checks If the HR department is able to confirm that these pre-employment checks have already been completed at an earlier date, the pre-engagement process does not normally need to be repeated. 1a. ID with a photograph In order to complete Section 5 of the Research Passport (RP) application, the HR department will need to confirm that an identity check has been completed. Acceptable documentation to support this are: photographic evidence supported by originals of either a passport or UK driving licence, and proof of the researcher’s current address, such as a utility bill, a bank statement or the most recent council tax bill. Utility bills and/or bank statements must be within the past three months. In addition, where possible, electronic databases such as electoral roll information may be searched to verify identity. Once HR confirms that these checks have been conducted, this stage does not need to be repeated. 1b. Two references & 1c Verification of permission to work/study in the UK As the University routinely obtains two references and verification of permission to work/study in the UK as part of the recruitment process, these checks may be confirmed as having been conducted and do not need to be repeated. 1d. Exploration of gaps in employment The NHS’ expectation is, as part of the RP application, that the University will check for gaps in employment/ study of more than six months in the past three years. body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Conditions of employment Human Resources Research Passport FAQs Eligibility How does the Research Passport apply to visiting international researchers that are not formally employed by the University of Portsmouth? In this situation, the University would need to complete the Research Passport form. Where there is no formal employment contract in place between the researcher and the University, a suitable status, (e.g., guest/visiting/honorary) should be put in place to ensure that there is clear accountability between the University and the researcher. We would then undertake the necessary pre-engagement checks to support the completion of the Research Passport. Any associated cost would be allocated to the relevant Institute/School/department. How to complete the form If I am unsure which section(s) on the pre-engagement checklist/Role Assessment form my work falls under, who should I ask? In the first instance ask your line manager/supervisor/PI. If they do not know then contact the R&D office in the relevant Trust who will be able to look up the project details on their database. Does the person named under ‘Manager in NHS organisation‘(Section 2.5) have to be an NHS employee? Yes. They should either be an NHS employee in the Trust in which you applying to conduct research or they should be a University of Portsmouth employee with an Honorary Clinical or Research Contract with the Trust in which you wish to conduct your research. Your line manager/supervisor/PI should know who this is. What do I write under ‘NHS organisation(s) (Section 2.5) if my research will involve work with various departments? Write down the main department(s)

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > My Role Human Resources Staff FAQs – EU Settlement Scheme Page last updated - 12 August 2021 I am a non-UK EU national. Do I need a visa to continue working at the University? The rights and status of EU citizens living in the UK who have obtained EUSS remain the same. If you were living in the UK on or before 31 December 2020 you should have applied for the EU Settlement Scheme. If you have not applied through the Settlement Scheme during this time you will need to apply for immigration status under the new Immigration system, or apply for EUSS with a justification for the reason for a late application. If UKVI does not grant you EUSS you will have to obtain an alternative visa or leave the UK, and this may lead to dismissal from your role at the University. I am a Norwegian, Icelandic, Liechtenstein or Swiss national, how am I affected? EEA and Swiss nationals should also have applied for EUSS. If you have not applied through the Settlement Scheme you will need to apply for immigration status under the new Immigration system, or apply for EUSS with a justification for the reason for a late application. If UKVI does not grant you EUSS you will have to obtain an alternative visa or leave the UK, and this may lead to dismissal from your role at the University. Will I have to pay for medical care? For those who have Settled Status through the scheme there will be no change in relation to your ability to receive NHS medical care in the UK. What support services are available for employees? If you have personal concerns related to the changes employees can access personal support from the University’s Employee Assistance Programme (EAP) on

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Learning and Development Human Resources UoP Learning and Development Toolkit What is the Learning and Development Toolkit? Whatever your role or grade, this innovative online toolkit is packed with professional development materials designed to assist you in dealing with the day-to-day challenges of your job. Whether you are looking to develop your skills and performance, prepare for a PDR or source development opportunities which support personal reflection and Continuing Professional Development then the Learning and Development Toolkit is the place to look.The resources are sub-divided into key themes including UoP specific initiatives to help inspire you. Alternatively if you’re looking for something in particular such as contributing effectively in meetings, problem solving or tips on how to motivate others you can use the search facility which will source all associated resources.These resources are fully referenced, kept up to date and can be viewed on-line or printed off for your ease.How it works The wide range of resources is accessible directly from this welcome page, making the Learning and Development Toolkit both quick and easy to use.Using the intuitive navigation, staff can quickly and simply find the tools and resources they require. The Toolkit features relevant, streamlined content, written by a team of experts and editors, making them easy for staff to both read and understand.Each resource has a short description to ensure the user selects the right resource. Once selected, the complete document can be viewed online, or downloaded in Microsoft Word or PDF format.All resources can be edited and adapted by the user.

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Learning and Development Human Resources Progressing Your Career - Taking Ownership Careers rarely move forward by themselves. While your employer may take an active interest in developing your skills and knowledge, it’s ultimately your responsibility to achieve your long-term career goals. Everyone has different career ambitions. However, whether you want to become the next Chief Executive or just fancy a change of scenery, the following tips should help you make progress. Progression top tips 1. Exploit opportunities If you feel that your talents are not being fully utilised or developed, look for opportunities where you can apply your skills or gain experience. For example, volunteer to organise an event or be part of a working party, join a project team, offer to help induct new team members or overhaul ineffective systems or processes. Keep your eye on internal bulletins for vacancies or secondment opportunities. Ask colleagues in other departments about any future opportunities and ask them how best to make your interest known. Ask your manager about job rotation or shadowing programmes. 2. Increase your visibility Not all career development opportunities are easy to spot or create. So, in order to make sure you are considered for any that arise, you will need to increase your visibility within the organisation through self-promotion. Self-promotion is about making others aware of your abilities and ambitions. In order to build this awareness, you will need to market yourself and demonstrate your talents wherever possible. Exploit the opportunities that come your way, as mentioned above, and try to create your own. Think especial

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Learning and Development Human Resources Career Development - Who can help? Things you can do to help yourself Build a portfolio of evidence to demonstrate your skills and professional experience Identify your skills and strengths Be aware of available resources to support you eg Learning & Development Toolkit, AUA etc Be involved in work opportunities -work shadowing, working groups, events etc Look for opportunities - Volunteer to do things – people like someone who is helpful – if you do basic things well you are more likely to be offered work with more responsibility Take time to reflect on events or work experiences – what you have gained / learnt/ where does it add value and impact Write up summaries, reports, papers, publications- discuss and share with others Discuss your personal development as part of PDR or interim discussion Things your Manager can do to help Developing individuals is part of a line managers role. Discuss the following type of opportunities with your line manager: On-the job training. Where learning is achieved through performing new tasks and activities, normally under the supervision of an experienced colleague. Job-shadowing. This involves spending time with another member of staff to learn about their role and what it involves. Delegation. Where individuals are given a development opportunity to deliver a short term task. Depending on the activity that is delegated, this can be an excellent way to develop planning, decision-making, problem-solving and leadership skills. Reading materials (e.g. books, journals). Reading about a subject matter is often the best way of finding out about it, but this is pro

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Learning and Development Human Resources What does Mentoring at UoP mean? Mentoring is a supportive learning partnership where a more experienced individual (the mentor) is assigned to act as an adviser or guide to a colleague (the mentee). A mentoring partnership is a confidential safe place to explore ideas and facilitate learning and professional development. A formal programme using mentoring software enables us to identify needs across the University and offers a fair and consistent approach ensuring that all staff have equal access to be matched to a mentor who can offer appropriate support. The programme gives staff the opportunity to engage in mentoring activity and contribute to the organisation’s capability whilst actively promoting a ‘sharing mind-set’. Mentoring can help staff continuously develop their skills, increase their knowledge and understanding of the University and HE sector and aid them in building resilience to better respond to transition and change. There is also a positive personal impact for mentors, who report that the process is not only a rewarding and developmental experience but also an opportunity to reflect on their own experience and practice. Mentoring partnerships are generally finite and should come to a natural end when the mentee's objectives have been achieved. Being a Mentor Anyone can be a mentor if they have skills to share. Mentors are passionate about supporting others by sharing their knowledge, expertise and experience and offer valuable ideas and advice when appropriate. Becoming a mentor is a great way to stay connected with the wider community and an opportunity to make a positive imp

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Learning and Development Human Resources How do I join the Staff Mentoring Programme? Joining the programme is quick and easy. From the landing page just sign in from your PC and a guidance document is accessible from the Guidance and Support Tab. There are different invite codes for Mentees and Mentors so please use the relevant code below: invite code (774ebd) to join as a mentee invite code (071a9e) to join as a mentor 1) Create your profile to demonstrate skills, knowledge and experience and to identify areas in which you would like to develop.2) Review matches and begin your Mentoring Journey to enable mentoring partnerships that suit your individual circumstances and support you to achieve career and personal development.3) Communicate with your mentoring partner and decide together the parameters of the relationship, such as how often you will meet and set your goals for the partnership. There is an expectation that the mentee will make initial contact with the mentor by using the chat or meeting options available on the platform.4) Meet as often as possible either in person or via email, phone or video chat. You are encouraged to meet approximately once a month in a neutral, private space, so that you have an opportunity to think, talk and reflect.5) Actively engage with the platform and resources available to cultivate your mentoring partnership6) Agree goals and milestones and record these on the system to evidence the progress of your mentoring partnership. body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Learning and Development Human Resources FAQs and Case Studies Mentee Case Studies‌ "Meeting with a mentor offers the space and freedom to focus on your own development and priorities, anything from building on ideas following training or Aurora, to overcoming difficult situations and making progress towards new approaches to work and projects, or learning about new areas. Being a mentee has enabled conversations about CPD and interests outside of the general job role/line management structure. This has been beneficial when adapting to different roles within the University, as it is easier to be honest about challenges and opportunities, learn from the Mentor's perspective and experience, and figure out ways forward." Emma Scott-Ward (Mentee), Global Admissions Team Leader "When I embarked upon the mentoring programme I thought very carefully about who I wanted to be my mentor. I am currently a middle manager for the university in a very operational role managing 9 staff, but I wanted to be mentored by someone who, although would have previously been in an operational role, is in a far more senior and strategic position now. I wanted to learn how they approached building their career, what skills they had focused on to take them from one level to the next and whether that was a level I aspired to. It was also important to me that I was mentored by someone whom I admired and respected and felt I could learn from. One of the reasons I would really recommend having a mentor is not only the knowledge and coaching you receive, but also the reassurance you get from it. None of us are the finished article and it is encouraging to hear what yo

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Leaving My Role Human Resources Notice Periods Details of the standard notice periods on completion of the probationary period are as follows: Heads of Departments, Deans and Senior Managers Please refer to your individual Contract of Employment. Professors If you are a Professor, the notice period you must give is three months. The University, as your employer must also give you three months’ notice in the case of termination of your employment. Lecturers, Senior Lecturers, Principal Lecturers and Readers If you are a Lecturer, Senior/Principal Lecturer or a Reader, the notice period you must give is two months. The University, as your employer must give you three months’ notice in the case of termination of your employment. Research Staff If you are a Research staff member, the notice period you must give is one month. The University, as your employer must also give you one months’ notice in the case of termination of your employment. Support Staff If you are on scale point 31 and below, the notice period you must give is one month. If you are on scale point 32 and above, the notice period you must give is two months. If you are unsure what scale point you are currently on, you can find out by checking your salary (as stated on your payslip, available via ESS) against the current University pay-scales which will give your scale point and grade. The University, as your employer must give you the following notice period in the case of termination of your employment, dependent on your length of continuous service: Less than 8 years continuous service: two months Between 8-11 years continuous service: one week for each year of continuous

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Leaving My Role Human Resources Leaving the University - issues to consider Once your notification of leaving has been actioned by the People Operations Team you will be able to view your confirmed adjusted leave entitlement via ESS. If you need to know your leave entitlement before this, an estimate can be provided. Submit a query to PeopleHub to request this information. Please note the following points: When you leave the University, any outstanding annual leave (pro-rata to the leaving date) should normally be taken before the termination date. However, where this is not possible, payment may be made in lieu of leave accrued but not taken. If your final leave balance shows that you have taken more than the accrued leave entitlement for the current leave period, we will make a deduction in respect of the excess leave taken from your final payment. Annual leave entitlements shown in ESS reflect the entitlement for the full year: if you are checking your balance, please remember that this entitlement will only be reduced proportionately when a leaving date has been input into the system. If you are leaving within 3 months of returning from maternity, adoption or shared parental leave (or an equivalent period if you have reduced your hours) when you have received University Maternity Pay, the Payroll department will need to reclaim the University Maternity Pay/University Adoption Pay from you. Please check the Maternity Leave and Entitlement Policy for further details. If you work Less than 52 Weeks per Year, for example if you are employed as a term time member of staff, and paid “average pay” you will need to make a comparison betwee

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Leaving My Role Human Resources Staff Leavers Procedure & Checklist Your line manager should provide a copy of the resignation letter to Human Resources (HR) when you are leaving the University, in order that the resignation may be acknowledged and a Leaver’s Pack sent out to you and copied to your line manager. The Staff Access to University Facilities and Leavers Procedures document provides more information. Once notification of your resignation or retirement has been received in the People Operations Team , we will contact you within 3 working days to acknowledge receipt. Please note that we will send an email to your University email account. If you have not heard from the HR Service Centre after this period, please confirm with your line manager that it has been sent to us for action. To prepare for your departure from the University it is important that you consider the following: Home address- It is your responsibility to check that your correct home address is recorded on the HR System and updated when necessary using Employee Self Service. University Equipment/Property- You must return any University equipment/property (keys, mobile phone, laptop, uniform etc) before leaving. University Staff Card- You must return your Staff Card to your line manager by your leaving date. University Car Park Permit- You must return your car parking permit to Transport Services, Mercantile House, by your leaving date. For any queries relating to the parking permit, please contact the Car Parking Office on 023 9284 2805 or email car.parking@port.ac.uk IT access- Your Line Manager will request closure of your email and other accounts, as describ

title: Our Strategy | University of Portsmouth
body: Skip to main content <iframe src="https://www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-WHX6C84" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> University strategy 2030 Our path to distinctiveness Our mission To create, share and apply knowledge, to make a difference, to individuals and society. Our values Ambitious We pursue bold ideas in an environment where creativity, innovation and success is encouraged and celebrated We nurture and value relationships and partnerships that foster a global outlook We inspire and support staff and students to achieve their potential and meet the challenges of society We never settle for second best Responsible We act with integrity for the greater good We insist on upholding the highest academic and professional standards We respect and celebrate diversity and equal opportunity through an inclusive culture We aim for sustainability across all our activities Open We work consultatively and collaboratively to benefit from new perspectives We share ideas and maintain transparency We are trustworthy and do what we say we will do We listen and respond to the needs of our students and staff Our vision Image Top-25 UK University A top-25 UK University, internationally recognised for excellence through our connection with our students, communities and society Image Outstanding and transformational learning Forging connections with and for our students to ensure outstanding and transformational learning for all Image Leading-edge research Driving change through our leading-edge research connected to society’s global challenges Image Strong partnerships Connecting education to workforce and skills needs through strong partnerships at regional, national and global levels Image Connected, inclusive community Nurturing a connected, inclusive community where our students and people thrive together Image Agile and responsive Connected across our University community to ensure we are agile and responsive Go to slide 1Go to slid

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Learning and Development Human Resources Recruitment and Selection Training As you’ll be aware, we’re not currently running the Recruitment & Selection (R&S) Training as a new programme based around the new recruitment system is being developed as part of the HRF transformation project. R&S Training is the collection of courses required to be able to act as a Trained Interviewer (TI) on an interview panel. As all interview panels must have at least one TI and it has been some time since the training has been run, we recognise there is a need for more people to act as Trained Interviewers. As an interim solution, we’ve created a document summarising key things the people responsible for the recruitment and selection process must be aware of, and processes we must follow to remain compliant. It’s also full of information to help you run the best possible recruitment and selection activities, maximising your chances of getting the right person into your vacancies. This document summarises information from a suite of training modules that make up R&S Training. Anyone acting as an interim Trained Interviewer will first need to read the document. We also strongly recommend you refer to it throughout recruitment for guidance at each stage. At the end of the document you’ll find a link to a Google Form for you to complete to confirm you’ve read it. We’ll use the responses to this form to keep a list of those people who can act as an interim Trained Interviewer on a recruitment panel, should no one who has completed the full training programme be available. We’ll also add you to the mailing list for the Recruitment Bulletin, which will keep you

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Learning and Development Human Resources ‌‌‌‌Delivering Successful Change Change is an ever-present and essential part of daily life, and the University is a constantly evolving organisation. While this can make it a dynamic and vibrant place to work, change can present challenges for individuals and groups, and needs to be effectively managed if we are to realise the benefits of making changes within the University. Where change is likely to impact on the employment, contracts and/or general terms and conditions of staff, there is a legal requirement to consult formally with the recognised Trade Unions and the staff involved. This is detailed in a separate policy, the University’s Organisational Change Policy: Redeployment and Redundancy however, following the recommended approaches in this guidance for communication and engagement may also assist with this process. If there is any question of a possible change affecting the employment, contracts and/or terms and conditions of staff, you must consult immediately with your HR Business Partner, who will advise you on the process to be followed. Involving your team in the need for change, and the plan to implement change, is essential in making sure your team is supportive. Change is usually required to solve a problem, to do something more effectively or to adapt to a changing environment. Discussing these challenges with your team, and jointly creating a plan to solve the problem, will ensure your team owns the change and is keen to make it happen together. Implementing a change without consulting your team will make understanding and accepting the need for change much more difficult.

title: Staff Essentials
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Staff Essentials Welcome to Staff Essentials, providing news, events and useful information for University of Portsmouth staff. Home Staff Services Departments Resources How to be a Better Communicator These pages are all about communication and employee engagement and can be used by any member of staff, but particularly managers, as a guide should you need to refer to sections to deal with any situation. The pages are largely based on information from an inspirational book, How to be a Better Communicator, by David Oliver and Chris Carey. The pages contain almost 150 easy to read nuggets of advice covering key aspects of leadership communication, including communicating during change, team meetings, developing a personal style and understanding the audience. Sections also cover practical skills such as better presentations, effective email and writing. The main sections taken from the book are supplemented with practical information about communicating at the University of Portsmouth plus some further studies, examples and theories. As a manager, you may already be doing many of the things listed, but there should be some ideas and suggestions you haven’t tried before. Communicating and engaging with staff is one of the toughest things for any leader to get right, and while these pages don’t pretend to have all the answers, they may help make things a little easier. Although targeted at managers, the information contained here will be of use to all staff, particularly some of the sections on dealing with change, effective email and writing skills. Use the navigation on the left to view the various topics covered. The Staff Communications Policy details how we communicate at the University of Portsmouth

body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > My Rewards and Benefits Human Resources Car and Bike Discounts Car Benefit Scheme Are you looking for a brand new electric or plug-in hybrid car with an inclusive motoring package and one convenient monthly amount taken from your gross salary? Our car benefit scheme can do all that and more! Your complete driving package includes insurance, servicing, replacement tyres, MOT maintenance, Road Tax, Breakdown Cover and Lifestyle Protections. To find out more information, see what cars are available and obtain personal quotes, please access the scheme via the Vivup benefits platform. Not registered for Vivup? It's easy to do by following this link and entering some basic information and it will also give you access to a huge range of discounts across the UK's major retailers including supermarkets, technology, fashion travel and much more! Note: the scheme is open to employees with a minimum of 6 months service. For full eligibility criteria please see our car benefit scheme page. University Car Parks - Discounted Charges Staff are entitled to park in university car parks during their working day only at a discounted rate, subject to certain exceptions. In order to take advantage of this, you will need to register to obtain a parking permit and set up an account to allow parking fees to be debited. For further information and details of how to apply please click on Staff Parking. If eligible to use the University car parks, you will need to register for both a parking permit and Evology to pay for your parking. Please ensure your details are kept up to date and match in both systems. Full details on the car parking policy and regulations c

The easit card costs £5.90 (September 2024) and is available to University staff as part of the easitPORTSMOUTH network. Register for a card or login to your account on the easitPORTSMOUTH website. University Bus There are two university bus services which run during term time only. For more information on costs, routes and how to pay for the bus, please visit the relevant Estates and Campus Services page. Discounted fares on First Bus - Commuter Travel Club Get discounted fares on First Bus by joining the Commuter Travel Club and following the steps below: Log in or register Select University of Portsmouth from the drop down menu. Enter your work email address to validate that you work for the university. Download the First Bus App to your smart phone and set up an mTicket account. Purchase your discounted Commuter Travel Club ticket on the website and it will be sent straight to your phone. title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > My Rewards and Benefits Human Resources Ferry and Hovercraft Discounts As an island city, we are well connected across the Solent to the Isle of Wight, Gosport and Hayling Island. There are a number of choices for foot and bike passengers and cars to the Isle of Wight. Gosport Ferry discount Travelling between Gosport and Portsmouth is faster by water, with the Gosport Ferry taking only 4 minutes to cross the harbour. Ferries run from 5.30am to midnight, up to 7.5 minutes at peak times. Gosport Ferry offers all staff a 10% discount on quarterly season tickets, with and without a bicycle. To purchase your ticket you need to take the following to the Gosport Ferry ticket office: Original Staff ID card as proof of ID A photocopy of Staff ID card 2 passport style photos of yourself Download and complete the Gosport Ferry University of Portsmouth discount form. Wightlink Ferry Staff Discount Wightlink is offering staff members 15% off car travel and 20% off foot travel when travelling for personal use to and from the Isle of Wight. To take advantage of this discount, please book online. Tickets must be booked online via the link provided and are not available via phone or in the terminal. Please note this discount cannot be applied to season tickets, Wightlink offers a variety of season and multi trip tickets which may help you to save if you frequently use the ferries. Full details can be found on their website. Hovercraft to the Isle of Wight - 25% staff discount for personal travel You can travel between Ryde on the Isle of Wight and Southsea in just 10 minutes with Hovertravel. The frequent service runs 7 days a week for foot passengers

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > My Rewards and Benefits Human Resources Learn a Language The School of Languages and Applied Linguistics is responsible for the delivery of the Institution Wide Language Programme (IWLP). IWLP provides opportunities for all campus-based taught students from across the University to learn a foreign language alongside their studies. Furthermore, each year there are a limited number of places reserved for staff to also learn a language. At present, there are seven languages that can be studied, Arabic, British Sign Language, French, German, Italian, Mandarin and Spanish. IWLP is an important part of the University’s commitment to global engagement. Furthermore, it can encourage the development of intercultural awareness in a mobile working environment and is recognised as a ‘career-enhancing activity’. Each year there is a number of course places available to University staff where a module has already recruited to the minimum class size requirement. Staff places will be allocated on a first come, first served basis. However, those who have not previously undertaken an IWLP module will be prioritised over those who have. There will be no charge to staff, nor their department. However, their participation in an IWLP class will need to be supported by their line manager and would be expected to feature in their PDR. Staff can submit an application request via email to iwlp@port.ac.uk. Staff are reminded that places are subject to availability. For further information on the how the programme works; the routes available and application dates please see the Student Learn a Foreign Language page. body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > My Rewards and Benefits Human Resources Living our Values Awards Scheme The Living Our Values Awards scheme is a tiered, nomination-based individual recognition scheme that aims to highlight and reward the exceptional demonstration of our organisational values. This scheme provides meaningful recognition through monetary awards, celebrating individuals who go above and beyond in exemplifying the behaviours and principles that define our culture. Living Our Values Awards can be nominated at 5 levels of reward, with more impactful behaviours resulting in greater monetary recognition. Who can make a Living Our Values Award nomination? Anyone can nominate anyone for a Living Our Values Award using Hornbill, giving a statement describing the positive impacts made and justifying the reason for the level of nomination. Nominations will then be evaluated by an executive panel and a final decision and level of award will be determined. Follow our Brief Guide below for more information about the scheme and instructions on how to use it: Brief Guide Explore HR HomepageMy RoleLeaving My RoleManaging My TeamHR Business Partnering TeamLearning and DevelopmentHR SystemsMy Rewards and Benefits Health and Wellbeing Retail Discounts Events and New or Limited Time Offers Financial Wellbeing Travel Discounts Leave and Absence Leisure, Food and Drink Discounts Pay and Recognition Personal Development My Reward and Benefits menu Life Insurance Hardware and Software Recognition Appreciative Management Practice Peer Recognition: Highfive Rapid Reward: ThankUoP Living our Values Awards PensionsPayrollOccupational Health ServiceEquality and DiversityContact usS

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > My Rewards and Benefits Human Resources Rapid Reward - Thank UoP Thank UoP is a manager-led, rapid, retail voucher-based recognition scheme that enables timely and meaningful recognition of individual contributions. It supports senior managers in embedding a culture of appreciation within their teams and aligns daily leadership with our institutional values. Thank UoP is intended to bridge the gap between messages of appreciation and a formal nomination for a Living Our Values Award to reward colleagues quickly when they ‘go the extra mile’ in smaller yet important ways. Thank UoP vouchers may be awarded in multiples of £10, to a maximum of £50 for a single award. Sending and Receiving a Thank UoP Voucher Thank UoP uses the Highfive platform and app, where designated Issuers can award vouchers to accompany a Highfive. The Issuers are nominated managers or administrators in each faculty, department. If you aren't one of these nominated users, then you should contact the right person (see list below) in your area by email, including the information of your nominee and the rationale justifying an award. Follow our Brief Guide for more information about the scheme and instructions on how to use it: Brief Guide Thank UoP Nominated Issuers Role / Areas of Responsibility Thank UoP Issuer Deputy Vice-Chancellor (Education) Anne.Murphy@port.ac.uk Deputy Vice-Chancellor (Global Engagement & Student Life) Chris.Chang@port.ac.uk - Recruitment, Marketing and Communications Mark.Barlow@port.ac.uk - Department of Academic Services Peta.Tattersall@port.ac.uk - Student Support Services Kevin.Child@port.ac.uk - UoP Sport Paul.Tilley@port.ac.uk - UoP Glo

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > My Rewards and Benefits Human Resources Peer Recognition - Highfive Highfive is our peer-to-peer recognition solution that makes it easy for colleagues to celebrate and appreciate one another in a timely and meaningful way. Accessible by all colleagues, Highfive allows anyone to send a personalised message of thanks or praise to a colleague who has gone above and beyond, demonstrated our values, or made a positive impact. Highfives that are not sent as ‘private’ will be visible to all on the University platform so it’s also a great way of enabling a colleague’s manager to see the positive impact that they are having. When to use Highfive Everyone can send a Highfive, regardless of their role, team, or level of seniority, to say “I see and value what you’ve done.” You might want to send a Highfive if a colleague: Went out of their way to help you or someone else Demonstrated one of our core values—Ambitious, Responsible, or Open Delivered excellent work or exceeded expectations Made your day easier with a kind gesture or positive attitude Contributed to team success or supported cross-department collaboration How to send a Highfive Highfives are sent through our Employee Benefits Platform (Vivup). See our Brief Guide below for more information about the scheme and instructions on how to use it: Brief Guide Explore HR HomepageMy RoleLeaving My RoleManaging My TeamHR Business Partnering TeamLearning and DevelopmentHR SystemsMy Rewards and Benefits Health and Wellbeing Retail Discounts Events and New or Limited Time Offers Financial Wellbeing Travel Discounts Leave and Absence Leisure, Food and Drink Discounts Pay and Recognition Personal

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > My Rewards and Benefits Human Resources Appreciative Management Practice - the Importance of Appreciation The foundation of our approach to recognition is Appreciative Management Practice: a leadership mindset that values, acknowledges, and builds on the strengths and contributions of individuals and teams and builds a connected community. When managers regularly show appreciation it creates an environment where our entire community will be more engaged, motivated, and resilient. Appreciative Management Practice fosters trust, strengthens relationships, and encourages a positive working environment where colleagues feel seen and valued. It ensures that acknowledgment is not just reserved for major achievements but woven into day-to-day leadership. This helps reinforce our values in real time, supports wellbeing, and contributes to a culture where all colleagues are inspired to do their best work and grow within the University. The principles of Appreciative Management Practice are applicable whether a senior leader, manager, colleague or team member. Each of us should adopt an appreciative approach and we are all encouraged to do so. Does Appreciative Management Practice mean we have to change how we manage people? The practices and behaviours we have highlighted here are already a part of good management skills embodied daily across our University. However, we can can use this as a prompt to refresh and remind ourselves to find the time to make sure we do this at every opportunity, showing thanks and demonstrating kindness to our colleagues. title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Learning and Development Human Resources Apprenticeships – What are they and how do they work? What are Apprenticeships? “An apprenticeship is a job with training to industry standards. It should be about entry to a recognised occupation, involve a substantial programme of on and off-the-job training and the apprentice’s occupational competence should be tested by an independent, end point assessment. Apprenticeships are employer-led: employers set the standards, create the demand for apprentices to meet their skills needs, fund the apprenticeship and are responsible for employing and training the apprentice. But the needs of the apprentice are equally important: to achieve competence in a skilled occupation, which is transferable and secures long term earnings potential, greater security and the capability to progress in the workplace. Institute for Apprenticeships An apprenticeship is a programme of training to gain the required knowledge, skills and behaviours for a specific job role to enable career progression. Apprenticeships last for a minimum of 12 months and cover a range of industries and occupations. Apprenticeships are open to all age groups from school leavers aged 16 to those who have been working for years or are seeking to start a new career. What types of apprenticeships is the University offering? Apprenticeships are available to both existing staff and new recruits to the University of Portsmouth, under the following models: New roles as apprenticeships Entry level roles: these are likely to be Grade 2 or Grade 3 roles in Professional Services. They include Intermediate or Advanced Level Apprenticeships provided by a

Chartered Manager Degree Apprenticeship Senior Leaders Masters Degree Apprenticeship - three options of Master's Degree are available: MBA Strategic Leadership MSc Educational Leadership MPA Public Administration Risk and Safety Management Professional Degree Apprenticeship Project Management Degree Apprenticeship If you would like to apply for a place on a Degree Apprenticeship, please discuss this with your manager to gain approval prior to you applying. Once you have approval to apply, please contact staff-apprenticeships@port.ac.uk Find out more about the application process. Please note, all applications from staff members must follow this HR application process in order for funding to be allocated from the University's Apprenticeship Levy Account. Staff applications sent directly to Admissions will be delayed and may jeopardise individuals gaining a funded place. body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Learning and Development Human Resources Non-Degree Apprenticeships Non-Degree Apprenticeships could provide the training needed for staff who require learning and skills in their current role. Apprenticeships are available at a range of levels: Intermediate (level 2), Advanced (level 3) or Higher (levels 4-7). The HR Apprenticeship Team have contracts set up with local training providers to deliver the training needed at this level. We currently have provision for the following apprenticeship courses: Business Administration - level 2, 3 and 4 Customer Service – level 2 and level 3 Data Analyst – level 4 Team Leading and Management – level 2, 3, and 5 Associate Project Management level 4 Digital Marketer level 3 Accountancy level 3 and 4 Laboratory Technician level 3 There are many more Apprenticeship subjects available, to find a specific apprenticeship standard click here. There are set processes in place for undertaking an external apprenticeship. Whether you are a staff member or a manager, the first step is to contact the HR Apprenticeship Team: staff-apprenticeships@port.ac.uk or to complete this enquiry form Explore HR HomepageMy RoleLeaving My RoleManaging My TeamHR Business Partnering TeamLearning and DevelopmentHR SystemsMy Rewards and BenefitsPensionsPayrollOccupational Health ServiceEquality and DiversityContact usStaff Surveys HR A-Z UniversityofPortsmouth Home Students Staff Contact and departments Home Staff services Departments Resources © University of Portsmouth Cookie policy Modern Slavery Statement Privacy policy Website terms and conditions
