body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Learning and Development Human Resources Academic Professional Apprenticeship (APA) The application window for January 2022 intake is now OPEN The deadline for applications has been extended to 10th January 2022 The Academic Professional Apprenticeship (APA) provides a framework for early career teachers, aiming to support you in the development of your academic knowledge, skills, values and behaviours, enabling you to successfully deliver contemporary pedagogic practice in higher education and further enhance your role within the University. Completion of this course will provide you with three formally recognised qualifications: Postgraduate Certificate in Higher Education Academic Professional Apprenticeship Certificate Higher Education Academy Fellowship The course delivery occurs via an active blended learning approach, which is underpinned by 4 key workshop days in year 1. The online activities are designed to be flexible, allowing you to tailor your studies around the requirements of your workplace role. To support you in your studies, protected time is allocated in your workload plan whilst you undertake this course.Additionally, you will have the support of a highly experienced course team and a subject specialist within your department, who will act as your mentor. Funding The Academic Professional Apprenticeship is fully funded for teaching staff through the Apprenticeship Levy, subject to meeting an eligibility criteria (for those staff who are not eligible a comparable pathway will be offered). Please complete an APA Application form to register on the course. Four easy steps to developing your career Step 1 - Complete the

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Learning and Development Human Resources Application process for the University’s Degree Apprenticeship Scheme This opportunity is open to staff who are on an open-ended contract or a fixed-term contract which does not expire before the end of the Degree Apprenticeship programme. Applicants must have their line manager’s approval and be able to spend 20% of their paid working hours on their study. Apply for a University Degree Apprenticeship. Application requirements Before making an application, an initial conversation must take place with your line manager. If your manager agrees this would be an appropriate development opportunity and that core activities can be managed, you will then be required to write a proposal to be submitted to your manager outlining the following: The reasons for applying for a Degree Apprenticeship How the content of the course profile will enhance your capabilities within your current role How the learning from the Degree course will enhance or add skills that will enable you to successfully complete PDR objectives, and ensure the successful delivery of the ambitions, priorities or enablers of the new University Strategy How the work that will be missed due to the 20% off the job training will be managed, ensuring service delivery levels will not be negatively impacted. Please note, undertaking and completing a Degree Apprenticeship will not automatically lead to promotion or an increase in Grade or level of responsibility. 2 Step Degree Apprenticeship Application Process Skills Matrix - this questionnaire checks your current skills, knowledge and behaviours in relation to your chosen apprenticeship to che

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Learning and Development Human Resources Course requirements Off - the - job training To be eligible for an apprenticeship, the individual must require significant new learning to take place. An apprenticeship requires staff to spend 20% of their paid working time on “off-the-job training”. This is learning that takes place outside of their normal day-to-day work and leads towards achievement of the apprenticeship. Some courses require weekly attendance and others are block release, but across the entire programme staff must have spent 20% of their paid hours on off-the-job training. Off-the-job training must take place within the person’s paid working hours, and therefore applicants would need to have their manager’s endorsement and agreement that their workload will be adjusted. Manager’s support Apprenticeships are based on the work that you do in your employed hours, and therefore it is critical that your manager supports your application. This is not only important for the off-the-job training time and the implications on the individual’s workload, but also to ensure the staff are able to apply new learning in the workplace and facilitate opportunities for taking on new tasks, as appropriate. Course delivery may require line managers to have some involvement in certain assessments (for example being interviewed or attending presentations delivered by their member of staff). Before making an application, a written proposal should be submitted to your line manager. title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Recruitment, Screening and Hiring Human Resources Recruitment guidance and the contract process for employing temporary staff If you haven't recruited a temporary member of staff before and require some advice on the process, please raise a ticket via People Hub. If you have recruited before and understand the process, you just need to complete a Temporary Employee Requisition Form, add a job description/person specification and send to jobsboard@port.ac.uk. The advert will be used to promote your temporary role through the Careers and Employability Service (previously known as Purple Door) The Careers and Employability Service offers 2 options: Online Jobs Board- an advert will be placed online for students and / or graduates depending on the role and as requested by managers. Applicants will apply directly to managers as detailed in the advert. Graduate Recruitment Consultancy (GRC)- for graduate-level roles, an email will be sent out to final-year students & graduates who are registered with the GRC. Applicants will apply directly to managers as detailed in the advert. GRC do not select or match candidates for these roles. Suitable candidate already identified If you have already identified a suitable candidate for this role, who has the essential skills (e.g. requirement for research, specialist secretarial skills etc), you may offer the role to them without advertising the role with the Careers and Employability Service. body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Recruitment, Screening and Hiring Human Resources Honorary and Visiting Appointments Procedure Introduction An honorary or visiting appointment is a voluntary arrangement which allows esteemed individuals from other institutions and businesses to cooperate with academic and/or professional services colleagues at the University. It is awarded as a mark of individual standing and in expectation of a contribution that may benefit the University. It also allows them access to certain University facilities to further collaborative research to the mutual benefit of both themselves and the University. Members of UEB and Directors/Heads of Service have a responsibility for proactively seeking useful honorary and visiting relationships. N.B. There is a separate process for conferring the title of Emeritus Professor. This document defines the criteria and procedure to be followed in offering visiting or honorary appointments. The University Research and Innovation Committee (URIC) or Executive Deans or Directors/Heads (as appropriate) have responsibility for making appointments in line with this procedure and for notifying the People Operations team A letter of appointment for the Honorary/Visiting Professor or Visiting Academic/Professional must be issued by the Vice Chancellor’s Office, Dean’s or Directors /Heads office (as appropriate) Subsequently, the appropriate terms and conditions for the appointment must be issued by the People Operations team for agreement with the appointee All honorary/visiting appointments must be recorded on the University’s Human Resources management information system Appointments may be terminated at the discret

title: Health, Safety and Compliance
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Health, Safety and Compliance > First Aid Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety First Aider Skills Workshops A refresher workshop is available in-house to which all First Aiders are strongly recommended to attend. The aims of the workshops are to provide an opportunity for the University's First Aiders to refresh their training, practice unused skills and discuss complex cases. There is a requirement to pre-book the workshop to ensure that there is enough resource available. Why In-House? The advantages of in-house refresher training are: Training would be on site and therefore candidates would be easily contactable by their departments if they were needed urgently. The training would be bespoke to the University situation and could include real scenarios as case studies. The first aiders would have the opportunity to develop networks and initiate future interactions between themselves. Is this instead of the Re-Certification Course? No - The skills workshops are an addition to the current First Aider Training Programme. To book onto the next available workshop please email: hsservicedesk@port.ac.uk Explore TrainingHealth and Safety TeamA to Z UniversityofPortsmouth Home Students Staff Contact and departments Home Staff services Departments Resources © University of Portsmouth Cookie policy Modern Slavery Statement Privacy policy Website terms and conditions

Aims of the workshop For candidates to gain knowledge and skills in various subjects relating to First Aid. Aims of the workshop To re-gain the required qualification to continue as a fully qualified First Aider at work as set by the Health and Safety Executive Learning outcomes How to act in the event of an emergency CPR ( cardio-pulmonary resuscitation ) Unconscious casualty Wounds, bleeding and shock Burns & scalds Injuries to bones, muscles and joints Eye injuries, poisoning Dealing with a casualty overcome by gas and fumes Dealing with major and minor illnesses Students will also gain the ability to maintain simple factual records and provide written information to a doctor or hospital if required and they will also recognise the importance of: Personal hygiene in first aid procedures The legal framework of first aid provision at work The use of first aid equipment provided in the workplace The role of first aiders in emergency procedures Other staff development opportunities relevant to this topic Further information about all Health and Safety courses can be found here: https://staff.port.ac.uk/departments/services/corporategovernance/healthandsafety/training/ Explore Training Biological Safety Biosafety Awareness COSHH - Chemical Safety and COSHH Dealing with Chemical Spills and Emergency Preparedness Drone Training DSE Online training Fire Awareness First Aid Emergency First Aid at Work (1 day) First Aid at Work (3 day) First Aid at Work Re-certification (2 Day) FAQs Genetic Modification Safety Manual Handling Mental Health First Aid Pressure Systems Safety Risk Assessment Safe Use of Compressed Gas Working at Height Health and Safety TeamA

title: Health, Safety and Compliance
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Health, Safety and Compliance > Training Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety If you have a first aid related question you would like answered please email: firstaid@port.ac.uk. Please put 'First aid FAQ' in the subject line. Who is responsible for restocking our First Aid Box? It is the department's responsiblity to restock the First Aid box. For suggested contents of a First Aid Box please refer to the HSE. Alternatively, first aid kits that are in line with BS 8599-1 can now be purchased as well. This standard sets out the minimum level that workplace first aid kits should conform to. Please ensure all First aid supplies purchased are latex free especially plasters. How many First Aider's should there be in my department? Guidance on numbers of First Aider's can be found on the HSE document First Aid at work. The Health & Safety office maintain a First Aid Needs Assessment document - please contact the hsservicedesk@port.ac.uk for further information. What do I do if I require a paramedic/Ambulance? If you require a paramedic/Ambulance, please call 3333. See click here for more information Our first aid boxes are blue not green. Is this ok? Depending on where you have bought your first aid boxes from will affect what colour they are. The colour does not matter, what is important is that the boxes are easily identifiable as a first aid box and that the contents meet requirements of the HSE. Do halls of residences need to provide first aid supplies for the students as well as staff? As this is a First Aid at Work policy the boxes only need to be stocked sufficiently for staff. This is different from the academic buildings as this is also the students place of work. How often should the contents of first-aid boxes be replaced? Although there is no specified review timetable

title: Get your prospectus | University of Portsmouth
body: Skip to main content <iframe src="https://www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-WHX6C84" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Discover our courses View our digital undergraduate or postgraduate prospectus Get your copy of our prospectus Our horizons are broad. Our instinct is to explore. Our promise is to inspire. View our undergraduate or postgraduate prospectus and step into our world. Whatever stage you're at in choosing a degree and university, you'll find everything you need to know about studying in Portsmouth in our prospectus. Order a digital undergraduate prospectus Inside our undergraduate prospectus you'll find many reasons to choose Portsmouth: from our Gold-rated teaching, to our student-friendly city, 4 miles of beaches and great nightlife. Read more Order your digital postgraduate degree guide Explore our Master's courses and research degrees in our postgrad course guides. Order a prospectus | We use cookiesWe may use cookies to analyse visitor data, improve our website, show personalised content and give you a great website experience. title: Music
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Support Departments > Music > What we offer Music What we offer Here at the University of Portsmouth, music plays an increasingly important part in our learning environment. Although it is a part of a few degree programmes, music is mainly recreational. That means that every student is given the same opportunity to join one of our ensembles, attend performances and perform in concerts. We aim to provide a positive musical experience to all who are interested - students, staff or members of the community, regardless of ability and experience. It's a wonderful way to meet people, make friends and engage in a collective and collaborative activity over and above studies. Listed below are the ensembles that we offer and a brief description of what they entail. If you would like to join one of these groups after reading this section, please go to our register here page. Full details of rehearsal times and venues are available on our news and events page. Choir We offer two choirs: our Student Chorus, which performs lighter repertoire, and the main University of Portsmouth Choir, which tackles major choral works in addition to lighter repertoire. We encourage students to be in both and we run rehearsals back-to-back to facilitate that engagement. In recent years, our concerts have included songs from the shows and charts plus works such as Haydn’s Nelson Mass, Faure's Requiem and Handel’s Messiah. We have also collaborated with other choirs, including the Portsmouth Festival Choir and commissioned new works from internationally renowned composers. The choirs perform at least three times a year in a variety of local venues and it takes part in Campus Christmas, festive singi

title: Music
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Support Departments > Music > FAQs Music FAQs How can I find out more about music activities at the University? The best way is to email music@port.ac.uk. Where are the rehearsal and practice rooms? All the main music facilities are located in the White Swan Building, located on White Swan Road, just behind the New Theatre Royal. Our choirs rehearse in the first-floor lecture theatre (EW1.11) of Eldon Building, which is accessed from Eldon Street. Are instrument or voice lessons available? The University offers lessons in voice, piano, music theory and some other instruments, at a cost of about £22 per half hour (£44 per hour). For more information, visit our What We Offer page or else contact music@port.ac.uk. How can I start a group or find other musicians with similar interests? If you are interested in musical activities, please register online. For other enquiries please email music@port.ac.uk. title: Music
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Support Departments > Music > Contact us Music Contact us Email: music@port.ac.ukPhone: 023 9284 3023Address: White Swan Building, White Swan Road, Portsmouth PO1 2GA, United Kingdom Who are we? Cerys Coppins Cerys Coppins looks after the day-to-day running of our musical activity at the University of Portsmouth. When she is not administering musical activities, Cerys is a PhD student and a graduate of Portsmouth's BA Musical Theatre degree programme. George Burrows George Burrows is Professor of Music and Theatre at the University of Portsmouth and interim Director of Music for the University. He has conducted the University's choirs for 20 years and advises Cerys on the running and development of musical activities at the University. Ken Wharton-Emms Ken Wharton-Emms is director of the Big Band and Concert Band. He is a professional saxophonist and clarinetist, who trained with the Royal Marines and holds a degree in Music and Sound Technology from the University of Portsmouth. Further information If you are interested in musical activities, please register online. If you have any comments about these pages or wish to provide feedback about any aspect relating to the musical life of the University, please email music@port.ac.uk. title: Freedom of Information Act 2000
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: Access to Information > Freedom of Information Act 2000 > Information for staff Freedom of Information Act 2000 Guidance for staff on dealing with individual requests under the Freedom of Information Act 2000 1. Background 1.1 As from the 1st January 2005, the Freedom of Information Act 2000 (FOIA) has given any person the right of access to all information held by Public Authorities. A public authority is defined as a body that receives public funding to carry out some or all of its work. All education establishments (except for nursery schools) are deemed to be public authorities. 1.2 People have the right to request any information from the University. The request must be in writing and can be addressed to any member of University staff but it does not have to state that it is being made under the FOIA. The University has 20 working days from the day after the request has been received (not opened) in which to respond to the request. 2. How is a request defined? 2.1 The way the legislation has been written, that is, that a person is not required to state that their request is made under the FOIA, means that any request for information should be treated as a request made under the FOIA. Whilst in practical terms many of the requests the University receives will be the same as they were before the FOIA came into force, all requests must now be answered within 20 working days. 2.2 For a request to be considered under the FOIA it should:- be made in writing – emails, text messages and social media are all acceptable methods of making a request; state the name of the requester and an address for correspondence (which can be an email or other online account), and; describe the information reque

title: Corporate Governance
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Corporate Governance > Information Governance Corporate Governance IG Resources for All Staff The Basics IG Core Training - This eLearning course is available on Docebo (Core Training Learning Plan) for all staff. It covers the basics of Data Protection, Records Management, Freedom of Information and Information Security. You should initially take and pass the course as part of your staff induction. You should retake the course to refresh and update your knowledge every 2 years. Policies - You should familiarise yourself with all IG policies. These are available on our website: Information Governance Policy Data Protection Policy Data Breach Notification Policy Email Policies - Staff and Students FoI Fee Charging Policy Records Management Policy Retention Policy Additional policies relevant to IG: ICT Acceptable Use Policy Information Security Policy Staff Access to University Facilities and Leavers Procedures The University Retention Schedule - This is the document which dictates how long we retain our records. Your area may have a local retention schedule, but it must be drawn from the University Retention Schedule. If you have a need to deviate from the University Retention Schedule, this must be formally raised to the University Records Manager (email recordsmanagement@port.ac.uk). Data Privacy Statements- These set out how the University will manage personal data. We have a number of over-arching statements to cover core University processing, but if your team is collecting data outside of core purposes (e.g. an event or research etc), your area must have its own Privacy Notice(s). Statement for Students Statement for Staff Statement for Applicants

title: Corporate Governance
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Corporate Governance > Information Governance Corporate Governance IG Resources for Line Managers & Supervisors As a member of staff, you should also refer to the resources for All Staff. When a new member of the team starts HR Staff Induction Checklist - Containing details of all the IG policies a new starter should read can be found linked from the HR Staff Induction page. IG Core Training - Make sure your new starter takes the IG core training session. If they have transferred from another department, you should make sure they have taken the course within the last two years, otherwise they should take it again. Staff Induction Conference - Make sure your member of staff knows about the staff induction conference. Sam and Sarah have a stall in the Market Place... come and see us for information (and sweeties!). We also have a set of virtual resources. System Access- Make sure your member of staff has the right access to the right University systems. This may include: Access to group mailboxes Access to shared drives (K:drive, Google) Access to corporate systems If your member of staff transferred from another department, make sure that any access they had in their previous role that is no longer requried is removed. Local Rules - Make sure your member of staff is aware of any local protocols for managing information. When a member of the team leaves Staff Leavers Policy- Make sure you follow the staff leavers policy. For IG pay particular attention to: Business Continuity - Make sure that any business critical files/emails are moved to shared storage, so that colleagues can continue working after the person leaves. If the staff member had particular I

title: Corporate Governance
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Corporate Governance > Records Management Corporate Governance Frequently Asked Questions... ‌‌Frequently Asked Questions about the use of Google Email. Email PolicyManagement of EmailGuidance & Advice Email Policy FAQs Q: Can I use UOP email for personal use? A: No. See Staff Email Policy Q: Can I keep my email account open after I leave UoP? A: See Staff Access to University Facilities and Leavers Procedure section 4.3. Prior to your departure, you should discuss with your line manager the need to transfer any correspondence and/or files held in your Google account (including email), which may be required by you colleagues. You are expected to set up Out Of Office messages to state that you have left UoP and provide appropriate forwarding addresses. If a valid business reason exists, accounts can be kept open after you leave, but for no more than 90 days from your leaving date. A “Staff Account Extension Request” can be made via the IS Service Desk and must be authorised by the Executive Director of Corporate Governance. Q: I don't want my UoP email address displayed on the website, what can I do? A: Although the General Data Protection Regulation makes email addresses personal data, there is an implicit expectation upon staff that a work email address will used for work purposes. This includes making your email address available to colleagues, students and third parties who may need to contact you for work purposes, including where appropriate the general public. However, if you do not wish your UoP email address to be displayed on the website, you could use a generic address for contacting your business function. This could be a UoP group/shared mai

title: Freedom of Information Act 2000
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: Access to Information > Freedom of Information Act 2000 > Publication schemes Freedom of Information Act 2000 Introduction The Freedom of Information Act 2000 ("the Act") was implemented to improve access to information held by public authorities in order to increase the transparency of how and why public authorities act and make decisions as they do. The University is a public authority under the Act. One of the requirements of the Act is to publish and maintain a publication scheme, which makes available a large amount of information to the public. The Publication List will give you access to documents which are available either through this website or in hard copy. The University also publishes information for the University of Portsmouth Enterprise Limited (UPEL) and University of Portsmouth Investments Limited (UPIL). These can be accessed from the menu on the left of the screen. Please click here for details of who to contact to obtain documents only available on paper or in other formats. What is a publication scheme? A publication scheme is a document setting out groups of information that must be made accessible by the public authority to improve access to the information. This document is then expanded - in a publications list - to identify the actual documents that hold the required information.The first publication schemes for Higher and Further Education were validated by the Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) until 31 December 2008. In renewing the schemes, the ICO has amended the format and content of the model scheme prepared for Higher Education institutions and therefore the following publication scheme (live from January 2009) is based on the new requirements. What is

title: University of Portsmouth | University of Portsmouth
body: Skip to main content <iframe src="https://www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-WHX6C84" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> This Freedom of Information (FOI) publication scheme highlights all information that is publicly available about the University. The information is arranged under 7 headings to help you find what you want quickly: Who we are and what we do What we spend and how we spend it What our priorities are and how we're doing How we make decisions Our policies and procedures Lists and registers The services we offer We have separate publication schemes for our subsidiary companies – University of Portsmouth Enterprise Limited (UPEL), University of Portsmouth Investments Limited (UPIL), University of Portsmouth Services Limited (UPSL), and ASTA Technology Limited (ASTA). If the information you want isn't available in our publication schemes, you can make a Freedom of Information request. 1. Who we are and what we do This class has information on how the University is set up, including its legal status, location and contact details. It also includes information about organisations we work with. Close all 1.1 Legal framework This includes information about the legal and corporate status of the University. The legal framework for the University of Portsmouth Higher Education Corporation The University of Portsmouth Higher Education Corporation is the legal name for the University, but we're more widely known as the University of Portsmouth. The Education Reform Act 1988 and the Further and Higher Education Act 1992 set out how universities must be constituted. The Instruments and Articles of Government of the University The Instruments of Government (PDF) sets out the constitution for the Board of Governors, its officers and their roles The Articles of Government (PDF) sets out the rules for the conduct of the University, the procedures for the Board of Governors and Academic Council, and the appointment of senior officer

title: Health, Safety and Compliance
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Health, Safety and Compliance > A to Z Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety Dangerous Substances and Explosive Atmospheres Regulations 2002 (DSEAR) The Dangerous Substances and Explosive Atmospheres Regulations 2002 (DSEAR) requires the University to make a suitable and sufficient assessment of the risks to his employees, where a dangerous substance is or is liable to be present in the workplace. Any requirement for a DSEAR assessment is identified through the fire risk assessment process and a specific assessment is completed by the Health & Safety Office. body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Health, Safety and Compliance > A to Z Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety Radiation Protection Unit The Radiation Protection Unit is responsible for ensuring that University staff, students, members of the public and the environment are protected from the low level radiation produced in certain research Equipment and experimental studies. Personnel For guidance and information in relation to ionising radiation, please speak to any of the following individuals: Dr Graham Malyon: University Radiation Protection Officer, IMS, 07756284614 Dr Darren Gowers: Deputy Radiation Protection Officer and Radiation Protection Supervisor, School of Biological Sciences, St Michael's Building, Ext. 2057 Mr Tom Campbell-Adams: Radiation Protection Supervisor for the Centre of Radiography Education, St Michael's Building, Ext. 4518 Mr Andrew Slack: Radiation Protection Supervisor for the Dental Academy, William Beatty Building, Ext. 5288 Dr Heather Massey: Radiation Protection Supervisor for the DEXA, Sports Science Centre, Extreme Environments Laboratory, Ext. 3545 Please be aware that no new work with ionising radiation may be performed without prior authorisation from the local Radiation Protection Supervisor and the University Radiation Protection Officer. Please see the 'Best Available Techniques' document below. This explains the management structure of radiation protection in the University and describes how we are working to minimise the environmental impact of our activities: Best Available Techniques This information can also be found via the following link: Radiation Protection Unit Please contact the Health and Safety Office if you require further information. Explore TrainingHealth and Safety TeamA to Z UniversityofPortsmouth Home Students Staff Contact and departments Home Sta

title: Health, Safety and Compliance
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Health, Safety and Compliance > Training Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety COSHH - Safe Use of Chemicals Target audience: Pre-requisite for Technicians who use chemicals in their work and aspirational for Technical Staff who wish to widen their knowledge as part of development. Aims This training will give participants an understanding of: - the legislative requirements for the use of chemicals and substances hazardous to health in the workplace. Learning Outcomes As a result of attending, participants will: - have awareness of the legislation that applies to the use of chemicals and substances hazardous to health - be able to outline the classifications of hazardous substances and associated ill-health risks - know what to include and where to find the relevant information for your COSHH risk assessments - be able to identify suitable and sufficient control measures and how to maintain them - understand when exposure monitoring and health surveillance is required - understand the requirements for emergency planning Explore Training Biological Safety Biosafety Awareness COSHH - Chemical Safety and COSHH Dealing with Chemical Spills and Emergency Preparedness Drone Training DSE Online training Fire Awareness First Aid Genetic Modification Safety Manual Handling Mental Health First Aid Pressure Systems Safety Risk Assessment Safe Use of Compressed Gas Working at Height Health and Safety TeamA to Z UniversityofPortsmouth Home Students Staff Contact and departments Home Staff services Departments Resources © University of Portsmouth Cookie policy Modern Slavery Statement Privacy policy Website terms and conditions

title: Health, Safety and Compliance
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Health, Safety and Compliance > Training Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety Dealing with Chemical Spills and Emergency Preparedness Training Target Audience: Pre-requisite for Technicians who work with chemicals. Aims This training will give participants an understanding of: - Impact of chemical spillages on the University - Overview of the relevant legislation - Risk assessment which includes identifying chemical hazards, routes of entry and people at risk - Procedure for dealing with chemical spillages - Control measures for chemical spillages - Spill prevention Learning Outcomes As a result of attending, participants will be able to: - Explain why dealing with spillages correctly is important and the consequences of not doing so - Identify the legislation relevant to spillages of hazardous materials. - Outline the hazards associated with chemicals that are used on campus. - Outline the procedures for dealing with spillages. Explore Training Biological Safety Biosafety Awareness COSHH - Chemical Safety and COSHH Dealing with Chemical Spills and Emergency Preparedness Drone Training DSE Online training Fire Awareness First Aid Genetic Modification Safety Manual Handling Mental Health First Aid Pressure Systems Safety Risk Assessment Safe Use of Compressed Gas Working at Height Health and Safety TeamA to Z UniversityofPortsmouth Home Students Staff Contact and departments Home Staff services Departments Resources © University of Portsmouth Cookie policy Modern Slavery Statement Privacy policy Website terms and conditions

title: Health, Safety and Compliance
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Health, Safety and Compliance > Training Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety Fire Safety in Laboratories Target Audience: Technical, Academic and Research staff who work in laboratories. Aims To give members of staff a greater understanding of fire hazards in laboratories; how to control them and how to comply with the Dangerous Substances and Explosive Atmospheres Regulations 2002 (DSEAR). To provide candidates with an understanding in the following subject areas: - The principles of fire and explosions - The properties of solids, liquids and gases - The mechanisms of fire and explosions (and provide an explanation of the fire triangle and fire quadrahedron) - Fire and explosion prevention - Requirements of DSEAR Learning Outcomes - Able to identify potential fuel and ignition sources. - Have an understanding of exothermic reactions, water reactive and pyrophoric substances - Have an understanding of control and mitigation measures - Have an understanding of the requirements of DSEAR Explore Training Biological Safety Biosafety Awareness COSHH - Chemical Safety and COSHH Dealing with Chemical Spills and Emergency Preparedness Drone Training DSE Online training Fire Awareness First Aid Genetic Modification Safety Manual Handling Mental Health First Aid Pressure Systems Safety Risk Assessment Safe Use of Compressed Gas Working at Height Health and Safety TeamA to Z UniversityofPortsmouth Home Students Staff Contact and departments Home Staff services Departments Resources © University of Portsmouth Cookie policy Modern Slavery Statement Privacy policy Website terms and conditions

title: Health, Safety and Compliance
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Health, Safety and Compliance > Training Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety Pressure Systems Safety Target Audience: Pre-requisite for Technicians who work with pressure systems Aspirational for Technical staff who wish to widen their knowledge as part of development Aims To provide candidates with an understanding in the following subject areas: - Overview of the Pressure Equipment Regulations 1999 & the Pressure Systems Safety Regulations 2000 - Safety valves - Types of failure in pressure systems - Inspection and maintenance of pressure systems Learning Outcomes - Outline the main requirements for relevant legislation - Outline the main features and safety accessories associated with pressure systems - Identify pressure systems that can be found within the University - Outline how the legislation applies to the purchasing of new and pre owned equipment - Outline procedures for operating, maintaining and testing of pressure systems and equipment - Outline the modes of failure for pressure systems and equipment - Outline methods to prevent failure Explore Training Biological Safety Biosafety Awareness COSHH - Chemical Safety and COSHH Dealing with Chemical Spills and Emergency Preparedness Drone Training DSE Online training Fire Awareness First Aid Genetic Modification Safety Manual Handling Mental Health First Aid Pressure Systems Safety Risk Assessment Safe Use of Compressed Gas Working at Height Health and Safety TeamA to Z UniversityofPortsmouth Home Students Staff Contact and departments Home Staff services Departments Resources © University of Portsmouth Cookie policy Modern Slavery Statement Privacy policy Website terms and conditions

title: Health, Safety and Compliance
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Health, Safety and Compliance > Training Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety Safe Use of Compressed Gas – Theory and Practical Target Audience: Technical, Academic and Research staff who use Compressed Gases. Aims To give members of staff a greater understanding of the hazards associated with compressed gases and how to comply with the Dangerous Substances and Explosive Atmospheres Regulations (DSEAR) 2002 and the Control of Substances Hazardous to Health 2002 (COSHH). The practical session will give candidates the opportunity to install gas handling equipment, such as 2 stage pressure reduction heads and flame arresters. This training will give participants an understanding of: - A legislative overview - The properties of gases - Types of cylinders and how to identify them - Hazards associated with compressed gases - Principles of combustion, including oxygen enrichment - Control measures - Requirements of relevant legislation The practical session will include manual handling of gas cylinders, connection and testing of gas handling equipment, maintenance and use of personal protective equipment. Learning Outcomes As a result of attending, participants will be able to: - Know the properties and classification of compressed gas - Identify the different types of compressed gas and cylinders - Identify the health hazards associated with compressed gases - Identify potential fuel and ignition sources - Select suitable control measures - Outline the requirements of legislation, including DSEAR and COSHH in relation to compressed gases - Safely install and test gas handling equipment Explore Training Biological Safety Biosafety Awareness COSHH - Chemical Safety and COSHH Dealing with Chemical Spills and Emergency Preparedness Drone Training DSE Online training Fire Awareness Fi

title: Health, Safety and Compliance
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Health, Safety and Compliance > Training Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety Biological Safety Training Leader Christina Scott, Biological Safety Officer Target audience: Pre-requisite for anyone with responsibilities in biological laboratories; particularly those working in CL2 laboratories. Aspirational for staff and students who wish to widen their knowledge as part of development. Aims This training will give participants an understanding of: - Why biological safety matters - How to contain biological agents - Risk assessment process relating to biological hazards Learning Outcomes As a result of attending, participants will be able to: - identify the legislation applicable to the safe use of biological agents - outline the requirements of containment level laboratories - outline the hazards associated with the biological agents they handle - outline the control measures that may be used. body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Health, Safety and Compliance > A to Z Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety Chemical Protection DIN EN 374 – Protection from Chemical Risks The European standard DIN EN 374 defines the performance requirements for gloves, which protect the wearer from chemicals and micro-organisms. Most chemical resistant gloves will be tested to EN 374 - 1. EN 374 - 1 will test your gloves to a total of 18 different chemicals before awarding them with a certification of either A, B or C depending on how resistant the glove is to chemicals. Finally, this standard also includes a logo that quickly informs the user of which chemicals the glove can be used with. The letters correlate to a chemical that can be identified in the table below the logo. Finally, the type corresponds to the information below the chemical table. Marking of Type A Gloves Protective gloves that comply with a permeation performance of at least level 2 (Table 1) against a minimum of six test chemicals listed in Table 2, the following pictograms shall be used with reference to this part of EN 374. The six tested chemicals shall be identified by their code letter which shall be marked under the pictogram as shown below. If other chemicals not present in the list have been tested, information about the performance levels shall be provided in the user instructions Marking of Type B Gloves Protective gloves that comply with a permeation performance of at least level 2 (Table 1) against a minimum of three test chemicals listed in Table 2, the following pictograms shall be used with reference to this part of EN 374. The three tested chemicals shall be identified by their code letter which shall be marked under the pictogram as shown below. title: Health, Safety and Compliance
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Health, Safety and Compliance > A to Z Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety Introduction Each type of eye protection is specifically designed to protect the eyes from various hazards. Some equipment such as safety masks can also be classed RPE (Respiratory Protective Equipment) due to its additional protective qualities. The selection of face/ eye protection will depend on the task. It should be noted that standard spectacles are not adequate PPE, and should be worn with safety specs on top to offer protection from splashes. Alternatively prescription safety glasses are available. Grade S Can be used in hazardous areas where there is risk of small flying particles from other workers or processes in the vicinity but where the wearer is not close to the source of the hazard. Grade F Is suited for tasks like drilling, sawing, grinding, hammering or risk from any process which could emit particles whose energy equates to the test specifications above. Comparison Safety Glasses (Spectacles) Safety Goggles Face Shield Safety glasses are a stylish choice of eye protection, although they may not offer the highest level of protection. Safety glasses, also known as safety spectacles, are sold in various PPE stores. Safety glasses are classed as PPE and therefore will need to be provided by the employer at no charge to the employee. Smoke safety spectacles are popular due to some of them having protection against UV. Safety goggles are common in various environments such as laboratories and workshops. Safety goggles tend to be made up of an acrylic lens, rubber frame and elastic strap. These can be purchased for low prices and are readily available. Protective goggles are used to protect against particles, shrapnel and in some circumstances vapours and gasses. Safety goggles, also kno

title: Health, Safety and Compliance
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Health, Safety and Compliance > A to Z Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety Protective footwear should be tried on if possible before purchase as ill fitting footwear can cause other health issues. You should also consider other comfort factors. Breathable safety boots prevent feet from becoming hot, sweaty and uncomfortable — they're an investment your workers will thank you for. There are also ways to increase comfort without changing the style of boot, including wearing insole inserts and good-quality socks. When your workers are comfortable, there'll be better equipped to do their job to their best ability, so investing in the right, comfortable footwear can really make a difference to your business's work output. Advise workers against the idea of "breaking in" new footwear because, contrary to popular belief, if the shoe fits well, it should feel comfortable straight away. If discomfort occurs early on and insoles and socks aren't making a difference, advise employees to try a different shoe, rather than grinning and bearing it. Uncomfortable safety footwear is not something that should be put up with and it's important to show workers that their comfort isn't a secondary consideration. The Health and Safety Executive (HSE) advises that PPE must be properly looked after and stored when not in use. Before wearing, safety footwear should always be checked for faults, damage, wear and tear, and dirt. Footwear should also be cleaned regularly. If boots are smooth leather, they should be polished and treated; if you provide suede footwear, it must be brushed and treated with a water-resistant spray. Soles should be brushed and washed to remove dirt and contaminants, and the shoes should be stored in a cool, dry place away from direct sunlight. For more detailed cleaning in

title: Health, Safety and Compliance
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Health, Safety and Compliance > A to Z Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety This document provides guidance for selection, use and care of lab coats. Failure/refusal to wear required PPE by employees is a basis for disciplinary action. A student not wearing required PPE in a laboratory/technical area may not participate in lab activities until such PPE is worn. Lab coats are an important part of personal protective equipment that serve to: Provide protection of skin and personal clothing from incidental contact Prevent the spread of contamination outside the lab (provided they are not worn outside the lab) Provide a removable barrier in the event of an incident involving a spill or splash of hazardous substances Individual departments, schools or faculties must determine what type of lab coat is necessary. Under no circumstances should the employee work without the necessary lab coat. Students in teaching lab settings are required to provide a lab coat at their own cost. Lab coats are required in all undergraduate labs where chemical, biological, or radiological materials are used. All laboratory workers are responsible for following the requirements for wearing and properly maintaining lab coats as outlined in this policy guideline and in laboratory-specific safety training. Selection of Lab coats: Lab coats are available in a variety of materials and provide varying degrees of protection. Examples include: splash resistant coats, static free coats, chemical resistant coats and flame resistant coats. When selecting a lab coat you must ensure that the coat you are selecting provides correct the type of protection that is appropriate for your needs as highlighted by a COSHH or risk assessment. After conducting the COSHH assessment you should select the appropriate lab coat.

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Pensions Human Resources LGPS: Additional Pension Contributions (APCs) Extra contributions to your LGPS pension as regular or one-off payments; arrange via payroll or by direct payments to the scheme How to do it: Read through the guidance on the Hants LGPS website. You may not do this while in the 50-50 scheme but can easily switch back. You can make regular monthly deductions (from 1 year to as many years as you wish up until your normal retirement age), or by a one-off payment. Contribute via monthly payroll, or direct to LGPS. You’ll get tax relief as normal through payroll, or contact HMRC for refund if paying directly. The purchase cost will depend on personal circumstances (age, when you start paying etc.) and the number of months you wish to pay the contributions over. The online calculator will work out the cost to you. When using the calculator, you will be asked for the ‘Percentage your employer has agreed to pay (%)’ – put ‘0%’ for this as while we pay a large contribution towards your normal pension we can't support voluntary additional payments. Be aware that payments can be increased by LGPS each year if they are revalued. There’s a maximum increase to your annual pension value you can have each year without paying an additional tax charge, but this is unlikely to affect most members who are not very high earners or those who already have very valuable pension(s), or those paying very high extra pension amounts over the year (typically £1,000 per month or more). You can find out more on our Tax & Pensions pages and if you think this may be of relevance to you we recommend you pursue Independent Financial Advice. Once you

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Pensions Human Resources LGPS: Additional Voluntary Contributions (AVCs) Invest in a separate Prudential pension associated with LGPS An AVC gives you a separate pension with increased flexibilities. It works differently to LGPS – you can contribute as much as you want and choose (either through payroll or how it is invested, and its value is dependent on how well those investments perform in the market. Be aware that because of this, its value could potentially go down as well as up. LGPS uses Prudential to manage these AVCs. You can agree a monthly amount, or select a percentage to contribute, and you may vary what you pay to suit your circumstances. You can access this pension when you retire, and can use the funds built up to do any combination of: topping up your LGPS, taking a lump sum (tax free to HMRC limits – normally 25% of total pensions value), and buying an annuity (regular pension payments). There’s a maximum amount you can save in your pensions each year without paying an additional tax charge, but this is unlikely to affect most members who are not very high earners or those who already have very valuable pension(s), or those paying very high extra pension amounts over the year (typically £1,000 per month or more). You can find out more on our Tax & Pensions pages and if you think this may be of relevance to you we recommend you pursue Independent Financial Advice. How to apply: First read through the AVC Guide, LGPS webpage and Prudential site for LGPS to ensure it’s the right choice for your circumstances. Make sure you have read and chosen an ‘investment fund’ at this point as you’ll be asked to select one during you

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Pensions Human Resources LGPS: Salary Sacrifice Shared Cost Additional Voluntary Contributions (AVCs) Invest in a separate Prudential pension associated with LGPS and benefit from both tax and National Insurance savings through 'My Money Matters' (previously known as 'AVC Wise'). Our ‘Salary Sacrifice’ AVC scheme works to give you the same Prudential pension as ‘normal’ AVCs, but with the extra benefit of saving the cost of National Insurance as well as tax on all contributions made. To do this, we have partnered with 'My Money Matters' who administer these contributions for you as a ‘salary sacrifice’ arrangement. Unlike most other salary sacrifice arrangements, our LGPS Salary Sacrifice AVC does this without reducing the pension you and we pay on your normal (unreduced) salary so you'll still get the full benefits of your full pensionable pay. AVCs give you a separate pension with increased flexibilities. They work differently to LGPS – you can contribute as much as you want and choose how it is invested, and its value is dependent on how well those investments perform in the market. Be aware that because of this, its value could potentially go down as well as up. LGPS uses Prudential to manage these AVCs. You can access this pension when you retire, and can use the funds built up to do any combination of: topping up your LGPS, taking a lump sum (tax free to HMRC limits – normally 25% of total pensions value), and buying an annuity (regular pension payments). If you already have a standard AVC you may wish to consider switching to Salary Sacrifice to benefit from extra NI savings. There’s a maximum amount you can save in your pension

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Pensions Human Resources MyPensionPlus Auto Enrolment You have been enrolled in the MyPensionPlus (MPP) scheme (using Aviva Workplace Pension), as you have been assessed as meeting the government’s criteria for ‘automatic enrolment’. Aviva will send you a welcome letter and then a new joiner 'welcome pack' by post to your registered address, normally within a few days after you are paid following your enrolment. This will invite you to register online and also provide details on how to opt out, if you choose to (see below). Benefits Aviva Workplace Pension is a ‘Defined Contribution’ or ‘Money Purchase’ employer pension. The contributions you and the University make are paid to a personal ‘pension pot’, and are invested according to your wishes. Members of MyPensionPlus are also provided ‘Death in Service’ Life Insurance and a £400 per year (pro-rated for part time employment) additional employer contribution which may be swapped with a range of other free Benefit Options each year. You may find out more information about what options you have and the benefits of being in this scheme on our intranet pages, and in the Aviva Workplace Pension Scheme Guide. Contributions The default rate of contributions for members is 6%, with 12% paid by the University. Depending on how much you earn, you may be enrolled to pay contributions by either Salary Sacrifice or Basic Deductions. You can confirm which method you were eligible for at enrolment by looking at your payslip. If you are eligible for Salary Sacrifice but would prefer to pay by Basic Deductions for any reason, you may elect to switch contribution methods. Further information on rates,

body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Pensions Human Resources LGPS Auto Enrolment You have been enrolled in the Local Government Pension Scheme (LGPS), administered by Hampshire Pension Services, as you have been assessed as meeting the government’s criteria for ‘automatic enrolment’. LGPS is a ‘Defined Benefit’ employer pension scheme and you may find out more information about what this means and the many benefits of being in this scheme on our scheme pages and the Hampshire Pension Services LGPS website. Details on contribution rates (how much you pay) and tax relief may be found here. As a new joiner, you will be sent a welcome pack by email (normally within a month or so of paying your first contributions) to invite you to register for the LGPS Member Portal where you can view and manage your pension online. Opting Out or Reducing your Contributions We would encourage you to think carefully before deciding to opt out of a pension and ensure that it is the right decision for you. You may alternatively wish to consider the 50/50 scheme, where you pay only 50% of contributions in return for accruing 50% of the benefits. If you do still wish to opt-out there is more information found here, where you can follow instructions to complete the Hampshire – LGPS opt out form available from the Hampshire Pension Services website then email this to pensionenquiries@port.ac.uk. Opting out within 3 months of joining the scheme You will be treated as never having been a member. University of Portsmouth payroll will refund any contributions that you have paid, less normal tax and National Insurance deductions. Opting out after 3 months of joining the scheme Your membership will end f

body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Pensions Human Resources Teachers' Pension Auto Enrolment You have been enrolled in the Teachers’ Pensions (TPS), as you have been assessed as meeting the government’s criteria for ‘automatic enrolment’. TPS is a ‘Defined Benefit’ employer pension scheme and you may find out more information about what this means and the many benefits of being in this scheme on the Teachers' Pension website. Details on contribution rates (how much you pay) and tax relief may be found here. As a new joiner, you should sign up for the ‘My Pension Online’ member portal, where you can update personal details, nominate beneficiaries and view and manage your pension online. Opting Out We would encourage you to think carefully before deciding to opt out of a pension and ensure that it is the right decision for you. If you do still wish to opt out there is more information found here. Opting out within 3 months of joining the scheme University of Portsmouth payroll will refund any contributions that you have paid, less normal tax and National Insurance deductions. Opting out after 3 months of joining the scheme Your membership will end from the 1st day of the month after you submit the request. After this date, if you have opted out with more than 3 months and less than 2 years’ membership, you may seek a refund or transfer (in most circumstances) by contacting Teachers’ Pension directly using the ‘My Pension Online’ portal or on 0345 6066166. You may need your Teachers’ Pension reference and National Insurance number to help identify yourself. What if I opt out but want to opt back in later? You can request re-enrolment at any time via ‘My Pension Online’, an

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Pensions Human Resources Nest (except UASL Nest) Auto Enrolment You have been enrolled in the Nest Pension Scheme, as you have been assessed as meeting the government’s criteria for ‘automatic enrolment’. Nest is a ‘Defined Contribution’ or ‘Money Purchase’ workplace pension. The contributions you and the University make are paid to your personal ‘pension pot’, and are invested according to your wishes. You may find out more information about what options you have and the benefits of being in this scheme on the scheme website. Nest will send you a new joiner welcome pack by post, normally within a few days after you first get paid. This will invite you to register online and also provide details on how to opt out, if you choose to (see below for more information on opting out). Contributions The default rate of contributions for members is 4%, with a 3.2% deduction from your monthly basic salary and an extra 0.8% added to your pension pot as tax relief. If you wish, you may increase the amount you pay. The employer contribution is 6%. To find out more about contributions and tax relief, please refer to the scheme guide. Opting Out We would encourage you to think carefully before deciding to opt out of a pension and ensure that it is the right decision for you. There is an initial Opt-Out period, which lasts one month starting three working days after you are enrolment, during which time you may elect to opt-out. You must apply to NEST directly. Your Welcome Pack from Nest will confirm the window you have to opt-out of the scheme and provide instructions on how. Nest will then close your account and instruct us to refund any contributio

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > My Role Human Resources Employee Assistance Programme (EAP) Supporting the mental health and wellbeing of all our colleagues is incredibly important to us, and our new Employee Assistance Programme will help us do just that. Whether you’re facing problems at work or at home, you can access impartial, independent and entirely confidential advice from qualified counsellors for any issues you may be facing, such as Anxiety; Bereavement; Stress; Depression; Workplace Issues; Trauma; Relationships; Finances and debt; and Family Difficulties. Accessing the Helpline Confidential 24/7 Helpline: The telephone helpline is available 24/7, 365 days a year and is completely confidential and to anyone needing mental health support. To access the helpline, simply call 0800 023 9324 from any standard UK landline or mobile phone Digital Resources and Self-Help:A wide offering of resources is also available and includes downloadable self-help workbooks, debt advice and more. Visit the EAP Platform to find out more: www.port.ac.uk/myeap Is it confidential? The University does not know who uses the service unless the individual personally chooses to tell someone about their contact with Vivup. Vivup do provide statistics to the University to show how many employees use the service and the broad types of issues raised, for example; ‘relationship breakdown at home’ or ‘bullying and harassment in the workplace. No information is ever passed on which could potentially identify an individual. title: Department for Curriculum and Quality Enhancement
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Department for Curriculum and Quality Enhancement > Academic Development Department Department for Curriculum and Quality Enhancement Academic Consultancy To support you in enhancing your practice and the student learning experience, we offer a free learning and teaching consultancy service for all staff in the University. We work with individuals, small and large groups or whole departments. We work with you as 'critical friends' providing in-depth development that meets both your teaching needs and the learning needs of your students. We work with you on your agenda, at a time, place, and in a manner that suits you. Consultancy can take many forms, from individual advice to larger 'events', all tailored to your requirements. Our consultancy service can help you in a number of ways, including: Making student feedback more effective Enhancing assessment practices Course design, curriculum planning, unit design / redesign Evaluation of courses / units / student focus groups Responding to NSS data and forward planning Effective learning and teaching using technology Working with external partners Working with international students Peer development and review We can help with many other topics: in short, if you have a learning and teaching issue, we can help. To discuss your requirements, please contact acdev@port.ac.uk. title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Learning and Development Human Resources Erasmus+ Staff Opportunities Academic and Professional Services staff can apply for funding to undertake overseas activities within EU/EEA and Candidate countries (Turkey and FYROM) via the Erasmus+ programme. The programme is centrally administered by the Exchanges and Study Abroad (ESA) team in UoP Global. The University of Portsmouth is an active participant in the programme and offers the chance to: Provide a personal and professional development opportunity, especially for colleagues new to the HE sector Develop your career and skills in your field Experience different learning practices and teaching methods Take part in an exchange of expertise and experience Develop your social and intercultural experience Help to reinforce relationships between institutions Improve your foreign language skills Participating in such activites may bring new opportunities for collaboration in teaching and research. Any knowledge gained by such activities can also be shared with colleagues and can assist towards encouraging students to undertake an overseas opportunity. What is Erasmus+ staff mobility? Erasmus+ is a European Commission funded programme that supports staff to undertake teaching and training activities. Eligibility & Criteria Academic (Teaching/Research staff) - opportunity offers Teaching mobility: Must be an academic (teaching) member of staff Must have an Erasmus+ agreement in place Participate in teaching activities at a partner institution in the same subject area Teach at any academic level for a minimum of 8 hours for a working week Minimum duration of two days Should assist in the mode

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Learning and Development Human Resources Leader and Manager as Coach The Leader and Manager as Coach programme provides leaders and managers with additional skills to conduct PDRs in an effective and engaging way, work towards a coaching style of management and be empowered to lead staff to achieve institutional goals and support staff in dealing with change. The programme consists of three half-day workshops over a period of 3 months, including pre, post and interim exercises between sessions to embed the learning and provide an opportunity to use the tools learnt and put the skills into practice. A variety of further support and resources will be available including online resources and support from colleagues on the programme using an action learning style format. Cohorts will be no more than 16 delegates per programme to encourage interaction, sharing and a valuable learning experience. In order to be eligible for the Leader & Manager as Coach programme, you must currently lead and/or manage people, conduct PDRs and have the full support of your Line Manager. The Benefits to Coaching There are multiple benefits to cultivating the appropriate coaching skills and leadership / management styles, including: building on PDRs and ensuring effective reviews which are fully aligned with departmental plans and lead staff to achieve institutional goals improving performance and commitment improving job satisfaction and morale at all levels creating a shared language of leadership encouraging idea generation working towards a coaching style of management better communications across departments To ensure accessibility, the programme will run
