title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Learning and Development Human Resources Recruitment and Selection Training The Recruitment and Selection training will now be delivered in a modular style, allowing staff involved in the interview process to access the required modules in a timely manner. 'Trained Interviewer’ status is achieved once the pre programme elearning packages and modules 1, 3, 5 and 6 have been completed. The full programme and module outlines, including the target audience and aims of the module can be found here. Module 5: Recruitment and Selection: Interview Practice Target Audience Trained Interviewers and all staff who are new to the interview process and will be on a panel Outline This workshop will focus on the preparation and conducting of interviews. The aims of the workshops are to: - Give staff the opportunity to prepare for interviews - Practice interviewing skills in a safe environment - Explore listening and questioning skills and question types - Ensure the interviewing process is fair and effective - Provide guidance on giving feedback to successful and unsuccessful candidates Learning Outcomes: By the end of this module participants will be confident in conducting fair and effective interviews and giving feedback in line with the University’s Recruitment and Selection process. Format 2.5 hour workshop Presenter(s) HR Officer (HR Service Centre) Further Resources www.port.ac.uk/landdtoolkit Please see the Learning and Development Events calendar for a full list of scheduled dates. To book a place on this event, please e-mail learninganddevelopment@port.ac.uk Explore HR HomepageMy RoleLeaving My RoleManaging My TeamHR Business Partnering Team

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Learning and Development Human Resources Recruitment and Selection Training The Recruitment and Selection training will now be delivered in a modular style, allowing staff involved in the interview process to access the required modules in a timely manner. 'Trained Interviewer’ status is achieved once the pre programme elearning packages and modules 1, 3, 5 and 6 have been completed. The full programme and module outlines, including the target audience and aims of the module can be found here. Module 6: Recruitment and Selection: On-line Recruitment Target Audience Any staff involved in the recruitment process, either as a Trained Interviewer, a Panel member or administrative support. Outline The aim of the module is to guide users through the on-line recruitment system, explaining how it works and their responsibilities. The aims of the module are: To show delegates how to log in to the recruitment system To explain the end-to-end recruitment process (advert to offer) To confirm responsibilities and access for each role (Trained Interviewer, panel member, Administrative support, Candidate, HR) To highlight any changes in process. Learning Outcomes: Confidence to log in and use the system Understanding of how the on-line recruitment system works. Pre-event Preparation In preparation for the attending the training you may wish to download the user guides from the HR website and bring along to the session to make notes against. Format 2 hour module where candidates will be provided with an overview of the system and be able to ask questions about how it works and processes they will need to use. Presenter(s) HR Officer (HR Service Centre

body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Learning and Development Human Resources Recruitment and Selection Training The Recruitment and Selection training will now be delivered in a modular style, allowing staff involved in the interview process to access the required modules in a timely manner. 'Trained Interviewer’ status is achieved once the pre programme elearning packages and modules 1, 3, 5 and 6 have been completed. The full programme and module outlines, including the target audience and aims of the module can be found here. Module 3: Recruitment and Selection: Shortlisting and Selection Process Target Audience All staff involved in the shortlisting process (Trained Interviewers, Convenors and Panel Members) Outline The shortlisting and selection module allows attendees to review a set of applications, work as a group to identify who they would/would not shortlist, understand how to update the online recruitment system and produce shortlisting feedback. They will also review information to be provided to the successful/unsuccessful candidates and the offer of employment stage. The aims of the workshops are to: Provide staff with an understanding of the shortlisting process including updates to the online recruitment system Give staff the opportunity to undertake a shortlisting exercise involving discussion with colleagues and making valid shortlisting decisions. Allow attendees the opportunity to consider and produce a set of shortlisting decisions for each of the candidates. Learning Outcomes By the end of this module participants will: Understand the criteria for shortlisting Be comfortable undertaking shortlisting Be aware of the information that needs to be recor

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Learning and Development Human Resources Recruitment and Selection Training The Recruitment and Selection training will now be delivered in a modular style, allowing staff involved in the interview process to access the required modules in a timely manner. 'Trained Interviewer’ status is achieved once the pre programme elearning packages and modules 1, 3, 5 and 6 have been completed. The full programme and module outlines, including the target audience and aims of the module can be found here. Module 1: Recruitment and Selection: United Kingdom Visas and Immigration Target Audience Trained Interviewers, Chairs and Convenors Outline This module is aimed mainly at Faculties that recruit applicants from non-UK/EEA countries and who require a visa to live and work in the UK. It includes the legislative and compliance requirements under the current Immigration Act (consolidated in 2015) enforced by UKVI. The aims of the module are to: To clarify compliance when recruiting non-UK/EEA nationals Identify the responsibilities of those involved in the recruitment process Identify and clarify the records to be kept to ensure compliance Learning Outcomes By the end of this module participants will: Have a clear understanding of their responsibilities when recruiting non-UK/EEA nationals Have an up to date knowledge of UKVI compliance during recruitment Will understand what records need to be kept and for how long Format Online training module - approx 40 minutes to complete including a quick quiz. title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Learning and Development Human Resources Advance HE: Aurora Programme Leadership Development opportunity for women (and those who identify as women) The University has committed to the Advance HE Aurora Leadership programme: 'Since its launch in 2013 more than 8,300 women from nearly 200 different institutions across the UK and Ireland have participated in Aurora. Reflecting the challenges, we all currently face, Aurora has been re-designed as a fully virtual experience. Therefore, all Aurora activity will now be delivered online. Led by a team of four leadership experts, participants will explore four key areas associated with leadership success: Identity, Impact and Voice; Power and Politics; Core Leadership Skills; Adaptive Leadership Skills. Each of the four delivery days are held online, with delegates usually being drawn from a wide range of institutions in the same region. These days combine prominent keynote speakers, open space technologies, outstanding networking opportunities, energy and dynamism with the development workshops which include interactivity, small group work, individual reflection, sharing of experiences and learning from role models. The development days are intended to be introductory events which will provide inspirational stories, guidance for growth of skills and knowledge, opportunities to share solutions to common problems and signposting for further learning' (Advance HE 2021). Aurora is a development initiative for those who identify as women who are at senior lecturer level and equivalent in Professional Services and below, ambitious for a career in the sector and interested in exploring leadership an

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Learning and Development Human Resources What is the Springboard Programme? The Springboard Programme is a three-month personal and work development programme for women (and those that identify as women) run across 35 countries, designed to help you recognise your own potential and reach your goals. Springboard – Springboard Consultancy Individuals speak of a new sense of purpose, an open approach to change and a more positive attitude. Employers report being delighted with the changes to their staff, who show a more focused and positive outlook, offering solutions rather than problems as well as increased motivation. Springboard helps organisations tackle the gender pay gap and work on the key issues of diversity and inclusion. Programmes 15 onwards will be facilitated by Chris Cross, one of Springboards Senior Consultants. Chris Cross has been a professional trainer, coach and facilitator for over 20 years. With extensive training and coaching experiences in universities across the UK. Working with teams, groups and individuals helping them make the most of their skills, their strengths and experiences. As one of Springboards senior trainers Chris has seen more than 2,000 women complete this award-winning programme. “I love creating a supportive and encouraging atmosphere. Springboard is designed to be interactive and I will help you feel comfortable and inspired to make the most of this opportunity. Whether you are ready for big changes, or time and space to reflect, I will be encouraging you and guiding you through the programme.” Who can participate in Springboard? Pre-event preparation Please click to access the Advanced Information Governance Training Handouts via EDM (Electronic Document Management) within Objective Navigator. Please print copies to bring along to the session if you would like to take notes against them. We advise that you do this the day before the event so that you are printing off the most current version. If you currently do not have access to Objective Navigator , click for details of how to add it to your PC/laptop. Session outline This session builds upon the foundation of the Information Governance eLearning package. The session will provide further detail on records management issues and details of the General Data Protection Regulations 2018. The aims of the session are: General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) – to explain the differences between the GDPR 2018 and the Data Protection Act 1998, and what we should be doing in order to be compliant with the 2018 legislation Records management in the 21st Century - what are the current challenges

Learning Outcomes By the end of this workshop participants will: Define autism in the context of the neurodiversity model Identify 4 broad areas in which people on the Autism Spectrum may have different perceptions Suggest examples of 'reasonable adjustments' that could accommodate such differences. Format: Presentation Q&A Presenter(s) Autism Hampshire Delegate Feedback Click for the Learning and Development calendar with all available dates. title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Learning and Development Human Resources Learning and Development Title Disability Awareness Training Target Audience All staff with an interest in increasing their awareness of disability and improving inclusivity in the workplace for staff with disabilities. Outline Presented by Toucan Diversity Training, a user led, award winning social enterprise based in Portsmouth. The training is designed and delivered by a group of accredited trainers who all have a lived experience of a disability. The workshop aims to raise general disability awareness and offer guidance, advice and support on how to improve inclusivity in the workplace for staff with a disability, as well as provide a unique opportunity to ask questions from someone with a lived experience of disability, in a safe learning environment. The aims of the workshop are to: Understand where attitudes come from and how they have changed over time Highlight the importance and value of the 'social model' of disability Gain a brief overview of the Equality Act, in relation to disability, and learn what is expected of service providers and employers in the UK Be aware of respectful language and etiquette Explore and understand the barriers people with disabilities face everyday Identify how to create a more inclusive environment. Pre-event Preparation Please read through the following before attending the session: Disability Related Absence Guidance and Disability Guidance Format A 3.5 our workshop, including powerpoint presentation, group activities, quizzes, video work and discussion. Presenter(s) Toucan Diversity Training Click for the Learning and Development calendar with all avai

Aims of the workshop As a result of attending participants will be competent to carry out the University's emergency evacuation procedure. Working at Height – for staff routinely required to work off ladders etc. First Aid at Work – initial and refresher training for those taking on the role of Workplace First Aider. title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Learning and Development Human Resources Learning and Development Title Mental De-Clutter Target Audience All Staff Outline This 2 hour workshop will provide a reflective space for delegates to identify what is causing them 'mental clutter' and then provide them with strategies to manage mental clutter and how to 'de-clutter'. The facilitator will draw on neuroscience, metaphor and mindfulness in delivering this workshop - which will be practical and experiential in its delivery style. Delegates will be signposted to further reading and you-tube clips to support this short workshop. The aims of the workshop are to: Provide delegates with approaches and strategies to 'de-clutter' their minds and thereby enhance focus Give an understanding of how very quickly and simply we can create 'mental clutter' from our throught processes alone Allow an opportunity to understand some of the more scientific aspects of what is going on for them during times of stress, overload and limited performance. Learning Outcomes By the end of this workshop participants will: Have learnt a variety of techniques/approaches to help de-clutter their minds and provide head-space to think and focus clearly. Format This is a practical workshop where delegates will be given the opportunity to 'try out' tools and approaches, it will be supported with facilitated discussion and a workbook. Presenter Julia Carden, Carden Consulting Click here for the Learning and Development calendar with all available dates. To book a place on this event, please e-mail learninganddevelopment@port.ac.uk Explore HR HomepageMy RoleLeaving My RoleManaging My TeamHR Business Partnering TeamL

Outline This workshop incorporates how to gain maximum value from the PDR process. The aims of the workshop are to: Build skill and confidence in having quality conversations about performance and development Understand the barriers to having an effective PDR and explore how to create an environment for productive conversations Develop confidence in conducting effective PDR’s at the University. Learning Outcomes By the end of this workshop participants will have: Understood and built confidence in how to have meaningful conversations with their staff, about their performance and development Gain confidence in how to engage and motivate staff in completing an effective PDR. Format This is a 3 hour highly practical workshop where group work and facilitated discussion will be used. Pre-event Preparation Delegates should familiarise themselves with the content of the PDR webpages and the People and Performance handout before attending the session. Presenter Julia Carden, Carden Consulting. In the workshops, we provide an overview of the processes involved in managing a research degree student at Portsmouth, as well as providing opportunities for you to pick up many hints and tips and to discuss the 'art' of supervision with experienced supervisors. Explore our supervision events Research supervision events programme Download our events poster Book your place Find out more Online Resources As a research degree supervisor you can take advantage of high quality online training resources provided by the Doctoral College. If you can't come to a scheduled workshop, you can instead complete some of the mandatory training online at a more convenient time. We offer online training for: Update for Experienced Supervisors (coming soon!) Outline The purpose of this workshop is to provide information on the University's Student Complaint Procedure and guidance for staff on dealing with issues at an informal level with the aim of resolving a complaint before it escalates. The aims of the workshop are to: Further understanding of how the Student Complaint Procedure works Understand what this means for staff Learn how to handle complaints at an informal level. body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Learning and Development Human Resources Work Shadowing ‌ Why do work shadowing? Work Shadowing provides an opportunity for staff to network and Shadow other areas of the university within similar or different roles. It offers a ‘fact finding’ opportunity and provides both personal and professional development. Find out more about the benefits How does it work? The most beneficial Shadow can be gained from a well planned visit with clear objectives and goals. All parties including line managers should ideally be fully involved and engaged. Find out more about the process What should I think about before taking part in work shadowing? There are some things you should consider before the work shadow, whether you are the shadow or the host, such as your aims and objectives during the programme. Think about how to make the most out of your work shadowing experience. Find out more about things to consider See top tips Apply for the scheme You can apply to take part in the work shadowing scheme by completing this Google form. Leaving feedback To ensure the successful running of the work shadowing programme, we need your feedback so we know where we can improve and to make sure staff are getting the most out of their experience. Fill out and email the below form to HR-Workshadowing@port.ac.uk and speak to your line manager about your learnings during the programme. Complete the evaluation form Senior staff case study- Bob Nichol and Graham Galbraith Bob Nichol, Pro Vice-Chancellor (Research and Innovation) took part in work shadowing around two years ago. body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Learning and Development Human Resources Learning and Development Title Creating a Coaching Culture - Coaching to Achieve our Goals Target Audience All staff Outline “Coaching is unlocking a person’s potential to maximise their performance, helping them to learn rather than teaching them.” John Whitmore This is a two part, 3.5 hour interactive and participative workshop for all staff. It is an introduction to coaching, what coaching is, where it can be valuable and how you may already be coaching in everyday life without knowing it. Learning Outcomes At the end of this workshop participants will: Understand what coaching is and how it supports the University Mission, Vision and Values Recognise the benefits and value of coaching Understand the skills required to be an effective coach Be able to structure coaching conversations using the GROW model Have had an opportunity to practice coaching in a safe environment Have the foundations of a coaching toolkit and know where to find additional support and resources. Format A two part 3.5 hour interactive and participative workshop. Pre-Event Preparation Please come prepared with a coaching scenario that you are happy to discuss within the workshop. This could be work or home related e.g. you’ve reached a sticking point in a project, you’re exploring your career options or would benefit from coaching around a difficult situation that needs resolving. Presenter Kathryn Hawkley, Learning and Development Adviser Additional Resources The GROW Model and Template Coaching V Mentoring Infographic Active Listening Powerful Questioning UoP Staff Mentoring Scheme This workshop supports the People Stra

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Learning and Development Human Resources Learning and Development Title Leading Change Target Audience The workshop is open to all leaders and managers. It is strongly recommend that delegates have a change project in mind or already involved in one that can be used to apply course concepts during the workshop. Outline This one-day workshop takes the approach that in order to develop commitment to change, we must move beyond seeing change as only a rational process to be managed and see it as an emotional process that requires leadership too. We will use the simple 8-step model created by John Kotter as a framework of principles that can guide leadership focus in times of disruptive change. This framework begins by looking at the need to address the complacency that exists in many teams and how to overcome this with a directed sense of urgency. We will also look at the importance of effective communication and a compelling vision and give delegates the opportunity to develop their own messages and communications related to their own change. Linked to this will be the development of a business case that engages both rationally and emotionally with employees’ needs. The aims of the session are to: Recognise that the leadership and management of change are different processes and that leadership is often overlooked in favour of management tasks Develop an understanding of what change leadership involves and identify how we can be better change leaders. Learning Outcomes By the end of this workshop participants will: Understand the challenges of changing cultures and behaviours at the University Introduce an 8 step model for leading change

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Learning and Development Human Resources Learning and Development Title Preparing to Present Target Audience All staff where there is an expectation that they will deliver presentations. Outline A full day of practical activity and feedback, working with some key concepts on a presentaton that you own. The aim of the workshops is: To help people understand and improve their presentation creation and delivery skills. Learning Outcomes By the end of this workshop participants will have: Understood some key principles of successful presenting Considered different structures and methods for developing the content of a presentation Considered the use of aids such as projections and props Understood and practiced simple approaches to the management of adrenaline, apprehension and techniques for the projection of confidence Understood their strengths and development areas Created or modified, and delivered an aspect of a presentation Afterwards, a video of their final presentation will be distributed confidentially to each participant. Pre-event Preparation Please bring along ideas for a presentation that you wish to work on live. This means bringing any key content information, but ideally not in a finished form. The best way of thinking about this is to answer the following: What do I need to tell a group of people, and why do they need to know? If you seek to work with presentation software, eg. Prezi/Powerpoint, please bring along a device, eg. laptop/tablet, that you can create and or/edit them. Format Some theoretical ideas, mostly practical and interactive, working on your unique presence and material you bring to work on. Presenter(s)

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Learning and Development Human Resources What is the SWIMM Programme? (South West Institutions’ Middle Managers Programme) A regional management programme for middle managers working in HE SWIMM is a unique and innovative regional programme designed to allow middle managers in professional and support services (our grades 6, 7 and 8) working in South West universities, to network with peers in similar roles across the HE sector. It gives delegates the chance to reflect on their role and to further develop the key management skills and techniques needed today. The universities involved recognise that there is huge benefit in working regionally with exposure to a wider range of practices, and developing managers as a future resource for the region. Participants benefit greatly (see feedback below) from this opportunity to network with peers in similar roles across the sector, within a supportive atmosphere, and away from the normal working environment. Who can participate in SWIMM? SWIMM is designed for middle managers (our grades 6,7 and 8 ) who are new to their role or already in post who would like to further develop their management skills. (Please see flyer for more information). The content may also be relevant for project managers. The programme was originally devised for managers of professional staff but over the years there have been a handful of academic staff with people managing responsibilities who have also found it relevant. If you are in an academic role and interested in finding out more about the programme, please contact learninganddevelopment@port.ac.uk initially – we can forward your query onto SWIMM. If you are inv

body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Learning and Development Human Resources Learning & Development Title Promoting Equality, Dignity and Respect in the Workplace Target Audience All those who manage or supervise staff Outline This workshop is provided in addition to the following core eLearning packages, which are a pre-requisite of attending this session: Equality and Diversity in the workplace Unconscious Bias Anti Bullying and Harassment To request access to this training please email james.ross@port.ac.uk The workshops aims to further explore these areas, supporting managers in delivering an inclusive workplace for their staff where all are treated with dignity and respect. The aims & objectives of the workshop are to: Explore further aspects of Equality and Diversity Explore further aspects of Dignity and Respect Understand the responsibilities of everyone with both the Dignity and Respect policy and the Equality and Diversity Policy Statement Identify how to create an inclusive working environment Explore Bias and Micro Behaviours Taking a lead in changing workplace culture Resolving issues at a local level What to do when things go wrong and where to find support and guidance. Pre-event Preparation Apart from completion of the core eLearning packages (links provided above) which are a pre-requisite of attending this session, you are also asked to read the Dignity and Respect Policy Format: A 3.5 hour workshop which includes powerpoint presentation, group activities and Q&A. Presenter(s) Equality and Diversity Manager The Learning & Development calendar lists all available dates. To book a place on this event, please email learninganddevelopment@port.ac.uk Explore

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Learning and Development Human Resources Learning and Development Title Introduction to Islam and the Muslim Culture Course Target Audience Any University Staff member or Associates Affiliated to the University Outline With the increasing number of terrorist attacks associated with Islam and Muslims across the globe, and Europe in particular, the constant negative portrayal of Islam in the Media, and public sector organisations including universities required to report radicalization under the “Prevent” programme, isn’t it time to learn more about Islam and Muslims and from authentic sources? The aims of the workshop are to: Provide a better understanding of the basic beliefs and practices Provide information regarding the background to Muslims in Britain Give confidence in understanding what is Islam and what is not Question the actions of ISIS, Boko Haram, and the Paris Attack, Islamic? Provide confidence to know what “extremism” is and what is not Ensure cultural competency in carrying out your work with the Muslim Community. Learning Outcomes By the end of this workshop participants will understand: What are the push and pull factors of radicalization Who is Prophet Muhammad Islam and Terrorism Why there is segregation of the sexes in Muslim communities Why some Muslim women wear a headscarf or the veil What actually happens inside a Mosque What is the status of women in Islam Why Muslims pray five times a day What is Sharia Law What is the meaning of Jihad What Islam says about Education? Endeavour to answer any questions you have about Islam and Muslims Pre-event Reading Itinerary and Etiquette for visiting a Mosque Format: Prese

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Learning and Development Human Resources Learning and Development Title Introduction to Trans Awareness Target Audience All staff Outline Gendered Intelligence is a not-for-profit Community Interest Company, established in 2008. It works predominantly with the trans community and those who impact on trans lives; it particularly specialises in supporting young trans people aged 8-25. They deliver trans youth programmes, support for parents and carers, professional development and trans awareness training for all sectors and educational workshops for schools, colleges, Universities and other educational settings. The organisations mission is to increase understandings of gender diversity. “Our vision is of a world where people are no longer constrained by narrow perceptions and expectations of gender, and where diverse gender expressions are visible and valued” Everyone can be more intelligent about gender: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=upAX-glEJBM The aims of the workshops are to: Set the wider context for trans identities – how sex, gender and sexual orientation interact Explore key terms and usage of language Offer basic grounding in the legislation around the rights and responsibilities around trans identities; Equality Act 2010, Gender Recognition Act 2004 Begin to explore how an organisation can ensure it is being trans-inclusive Provide links to a wide range of resources. Learning Outcomes Have wider understanding of trans identities, key terms and usage of language Have understanding of the legal framework around trans equality and the responsibilities that places on the University and us as employees Have understanding of how

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Learning and Development Human Resources Learning and Development Title Meeting Support and Minute Writing Target Audience Staff who take notes at various types of meetings who would like to learn more about this activity within the University. Types of meetings can include: Committees, Departmental, Team, Focus Groups, Operational or Working Groups. Outline This workshop is a practical session all about identifying and practicing the skills of supporting a committee or group and taking notes effectively during meetings. It provides the opportunity for discussion with colleagues, sharing good working practices such as; an organised approach, preparing for the meeting, summarising when writing up, using the past tense, looking at the format and templates to make your notes easy to read. The workshop will help delegates to produce a concise and consistent look for documents that can be circulated with confidence. The aims of the workshop are to: Promote collaborative working between Chairs and Minute Takers Look at setting an agenda Provoke thought and reflection on current practice to identify possible improvements Share working practices for supporting a meeting and minute writing Improve the experience of taking minutes at meetings Practice minute writing skills such as listening, organisation of minutes, templates and format for writing up and summarising skills Inspire confidence when supporting a meeting. Please note that this session does not cover Unit Assessment Boards (UAB) and Boards of Examiners (BoE) specifically, although the skills discussed and practiced can be applied to these meetings. Sessions dedicated to UAB and BoE

title: Staff Essentials
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Staff Essentials Welcome to Staff Essentials, providing news, events and useful information for University of Portsmouth staff. Home Staff Services Departments Resources Support/Professional Services Staff Conference 2017 Working Collaboratively to Achieve our Goals The University’s Support/Professional Services Staff Conference took take place on Thursday 5 January 2017 in Richmond Building. The conference aims were to: Celebrate how staff work together across the institution to achieve our strategic goals. Inspire and encourage delegates to continue to make the University a great place to work, both for themselves and others. Share information and resources that can help improve and promote collaborative working. Provide an opportunity for staff to meet with other colleagues they may not normally come into contact with The conference was opened by Graham Galbraith and included two rounds of parallel sessions each on a different subject. As well as parallel sessions we ran a 'speed networking' event and the event closed with a student and staff question panel. We are pleased to share the presentations (internal only) from the day (including the parrallel sessions). The feedback received has been extremely positive, some of the comments can be found below: "The Nifty Networking was presented really well, and met my expectations for obtaining tools to deal with networking. This was then followed by the Speed Networking event which again was really useful, especially having completed the Nifty Networking prior to this event. I found one person I met with that I wouldn't necessarily come in contact with, and although she is new, I think I will be contacting her in the future for possible shadowing" "I lear

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Learning and Development Human Resources Emily Parry - Employability and Enterprise Manager Student Placement and Employability Centre (SPEC) - Technology Learning Centre Employment history I have been at the university for 13 years in a range of admin roles, having previously worked in a call centre and in purchasing for a local sewage treatment company. I joined the uni in 2002, starting as a Course Administrator in the Undergraduate Centre in PBS based at the old Milton Campus. I then moved sideways to the same role within the Post Graduate Centre of PBS, looking after the admin for the AMBA accredited MBA programmes, eventually progressing to Senior Course Administrator with some supervisory responsibility. With my experience of the student records system, I moved to Academic Registry as a Student Records System Adviser where I provided front line support to end users and training on the ‘Discoverer’ reporting system. I then realised I missed working with students, and, given my experience with senior management level staff when working on the MBA programmes, I was able to demonstrate the competencies to secure my current role of Employability and Enterprise Manager. Over that time, I have developed and honed my customer service, relationship management and information advice and guidance skills, working with a range of different students and external companies. Strengths – I am a people person (ok, I like a chat), I like processes and can be very organised (when needs be!). I also like spotting opportunities for closer working relationships between the University and our placement employers. Weaknesses – numbers are not a strong p

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Learning and Development Human Resources Helen Keetch – Registry Support Systems Analyst Academic Registry Employment history Faculty of Science - Course Administrator I started at the University twelve years ago as a temp. My first role was as a Course Administrator for the School of Earth and Environmental Sciences for half the week, the other half was spent in the School of Pharmacy and Biomedical Sciences. A full time role came up in Pharmacy where I stayed for about a year, then a full time role came up in the School of Earth and Environmental Sciences, which although a smaller school was somewhere I felt was a more comfortable fit at that time. Key skills Using the Student Records System Minutes and administration of Boards – Unit Assessment Boards and Board of Examiners, Board of Studies Annotating exam papers Registration of students Pharmacy only- Admin for Admissions interviews - Admin of PhD and Research students Faculty of Technology and Central Project Team Secondment - Jupiter support team Having become well versed in the Student Records system and the role of a C.A, a secondment opportunity came up for a project role to assist with the implementation of a new Student Records system. This would mean liaising between the central project team and one of the Faculties, helping guide the users through the new processes. At the same time an option of applying for a School Manager role in Science came up. I had to think very hard about which path I wanted to take, as both interviews would be within weeks of each other. For me I realised that at that point I was not ready for a role that I felt was an end result from a career pe

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Learning and Development Human Resources Jackie Seymour – Undergraduate Centre Manager Portsmouth Business School Employment history I joined Portsmouth Polytechnic back in 1989 as a course secretary on the Milton site - the main requirement on the person specification was for RSA typewriting grade 3 which I had achieved by attending adult education. I was from Priory School and back then no one from my peer group went on to college, let alone University. My mum said to me when I was about 15 - learn to type and you'll always have a job - and she was right. I was the first Business School employee to have a desktop computer, can you imagine producing transcripts in carbon copy on a typewriter! I spent my early years at the University engaged in course administration, working part time for quite a while whilst raising a family. The excellent family friendly policies of the University enabled me to change my working hours to suit this family life. It was after about 15 years that things started to change for me, mainly because my children were growing up and I returned to work full time. My attitude to work changed and I wanted something more than 'just working'. The University created Senior Course Administrators and I was appointed to be one having worked unofficially in this capacity for quite some time. Taking Opportunities At the same time Portsmouth Business School developed the Foundation degree in Business and Management and I was lucky to be encouraged and funded to be on the first cohort. Then began five years of very hard work, part time study and working full time, achieving firstly the FDA, and then a BA (Hons) Business and

body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Learning and Development Human Resources Lyuda Wade -Employment history Roles/Work + Experience Key skills Strengths/weaknesses PG Course Administrator (Apr 2004 - May 2006) I provided full administrative support to students and academic staff on Postgraduate courses in the Psychology department. I was offered a secondment opportunity to contribute towards an exciting and vital for the University project: implementation of the new Student Records system. Good knowledge of MS Office suite (Word, Excel) is essential, email, knowledge of relevant corporate systems (e.g. Student Records, it was HEMIS when I started in my role), minutes takings Understanding of HE and student life cycle. Good customer service skills, ability to communicate well at all levels through different media and empathy (especially when dealing with students) is also very important. Strengths: Organisational skills Communication skills Weaknesses Working in a single department could be limiting - siloed environment Repetitive experience might become boring with time Jupiter Implementation Specialist Secondment (May 2006 – Mar 2008) Getting involved in the Implementation of new Student Records system. Providing support to administrative staff at the Science faculty in relation to Student Records system, running training events (including preparing training materials) and business analysis work (options analysis, report specifications, testing of changes, etc.) This position was offered on a secondment basis. As the secondment was coming to an end I started looking for opportunities where I could apply my analytical skills and progress my career to new levels. Confiden

I now delegate more Taking Opportunities v Certificate in Team Leading Level 2 v Counselling Skills Level 2 v BSL Level 1 v BSL Level 2 v Mediation v DMS Level 5 Management Studies v Heart Start v Drug Awareness v Disability Awareness v Appraisee workshop v Progressing within a Career in Support Services v Workshop for Appraisers v Data Protection v Health & Safety v Welcome Host v Multi-Cultural Workshop v Employment Law Training v Mental Health & Student Accommodation v Dealing with Difficult Situations v Customer Service v Mental Health First Aid v Aurora – Women in Business HE experience - Key skill areas Current role - what you really enjoy about it: Having a student who is really struggling with their University course, thei

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Learning and Development Human Resources Louisa Caine – Faculty Registrar Faculty of Humanities Employment history I started my career in HE working in SLAS as a Course Administrator looking after the pre-sessional students in September 2003. I moved to become and Senior Course Administrator. In 2007 I became School Manager in SSHLS and in July 2015 Faculty Registrar for FHSS. I love working with students and being a CA/SCA. I believe I have excellent attention to detail, multi-tasking at busy times, willingness to take on additional tasks outside of my job role, all of which have helped me progress my career. Taking opportunities I decided to study a Foundation Degree in Business and Management after working for three years in SLAS, and then proceeded to take the top up and I graduated with a 2:1 three years ago. I have used skills gained whilst studying in my role as School Manager. I have taken on loads of additional training through Staff Development and a key to being promoted is the willingness to undertake training, putting myself forward for working groups outside of my role. Experiences/Learning points My strengths are remaining positive, always smiling and laughing when I can. Nothing is too much, being motivated and willing to learn new things. Good communication with my staff, sharing and pooling ideas has also helped me. I think the University is a great place to work but you need to work to develop yourself, jobs don't just appear. I have worked hard to get where I am by developing myself, taking on additional responsibilities, but also taking ownership and not saying 'That is not my job' has helped me. I hear this all th

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Learning and Development Human Resources Nikki Sowerby – Assistant Registrar (Research Degrees) Academic Registry Employment I have been working with Higher Education (HE) since 1998 and started here at the University of Portsmouth as an Administrator in Admissions in the Faculty of Technology. This role was initially a maternity leave cover, however the role holder did not return and I as was successful in continuing in the role. As I developed my skills, knowledge and understanding of the university I was able to progress in my career. In 1999 I took on the role of Course Administrator in the School of Computer Science and Maths which lead to the opportunity to progress to an Administrative Assistant in the Department of Information Systems in 2001. Due to restructuring of the administration within the Faculty I was successful in gaining the position of Deputy Programme Area Manager for Computing and Maths in November 2001. Further career progression opportunities arose in 2005 and I took on a secondment to the role of Department Manager, covering maternity leave for 9 months for the Technology Extended Campus and then returning back to an Acting Programme Area Manager role covering temporary secondment. In 2006 I was successful in progressing to the role of Faculty Registrar and Validation Officer for the Faculty of Technology. In May 2011 I found out that the role was being made redundant and I was successful in finding a new role within the University and began the role of Principal Administrator (Research) in the Academic Registry in August 2011. During the initial two years I and the role developed with me taking on further or e

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Learning and Development Human Resources Lauren Cummings – School Manager School of Biological Sciences Employment journey Graduated from Southampton Solent University in 2008 with a 2:1 in BA (Hons) Advertising While at University and College I worked part time at Waitrose Ltd. This role allowed me to gain core customer service, presentation, organisational skills and a good store knowledge. At Waitrose I worked closely with department manages and was soon given the responsibility of supervising staff on evening shifts. The role enabled me to obtain a high level of transferrable skills. While at University I actively sought out work experience during my second year and worked for a few months within the Portsmouth Centre for Enterprise at the University of Portsmouth. I worked with a team to develop an enterprise programme for Schools and FE institutions, which allowed me to develop my project management skills and work both as a team and an individual. After finishing University I applied for many jobs, which was tough, especially being fresh out of education; competition for roles was tough. I gained an interview for a course administrator role at the University of Portsmouth and was successful. In September 2008 I started as a course administrator within the Department of Sport and Exercise Science (DSES). I had a steep learning curve, as I was straight into registration period, which is one of the busiest times of the academic year with all the new students arriving at the University. Within my new role I took on a lot of new training with corporate systems and was very eager to take on new tasks within the team. I built a good re

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Learning and Development Human Resources David Bowes – Service Improvement and Problem Manager Information Services Employment history Initially employed as a Trainee in Information Services on a rotating arrangement working within 4 teams over 2 years covering a range of IT service provision (Networks, Desk Side Support, Server Infrastructure and Service Desk) Subsequently employed as a Service Delivery Analyst covering Service Desk and Desk side support roles As part of Service Delivery I took on a Service Management role, working to translate technical capabilities into user facing services, along with advocating for customer priorities in technical conversations initially around File Storage. The Service Delivery Analyst role developed into a Change Manager Position. This involved managing a process overseeing the hundreds of changes that are made each month to the UoP IT infrastructure and ensuring they are carried out with the minimum impact to customers. Appointed to a secondment opportunity as Head of IT at Havant College. This position included line managing 16 Members of staff, strategic planning, financial budgeting and operational management. Essentially being responsible for a full IT provision for a 1100 Student FE College including Library and reprographics services while reporting to the Finance Director. After a year in post at Havant College I returned to my substantive post at UoP where I began working to explore the benefits of a potential Service Improvement function within IS. In Late 2014 I applied for and was appointed to the role of Service Improvement Manager - This position affords the flexibility to pursue a

body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Learning and Development Human Resources Delivering Successful Change ‌ ‌‌ ‌ ‌‌ ‌Every change situation is unique and, although previous experience allows you to identify what has worked and not worked in the past, you’ll want to make sure that the approach you use is the best for your particular circumstances. You can ensure this by taking these actions to design your approach. Consider the reasons why the change is important The University’s mission and vision, values, and the University Strategy. Define your future vision Decide if any formal or statutory procedures need to be followed The University’s Organisational Change, Redeployment and Redundancy Policy Identify the specific resources you will need, and consider the justification for the change The Case for Change, Resource Profile Identify what might go wrong and accompanying corrective actions Risk Assessment Analyse your stakeholders and identify relevant communication actions Stakeholder Analysis and Grid Determine when you expect key change stages and decisions will take place Timeline You can read more detailed information on the Analyse: Extended Guidance page. body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Learning and Development Human Resources Delivering Successful Change ‌ ‌‌ Once you understand the change you want to put in place, you can put the necessary preparations in place, including support for individuals to deal with change, identifying and sequencing change activities, developing your stakeholder management and communications plans, and revising relevant policies and processes ready for your change. You can prepare for your change by considering the series of activities below. You can prepare for your change by considering the series of activities below. Design your change approach, based on an assessment of the change context Context assessment,options assessment Identify where individuals need transition support and agree appropriate change plans Personal Change Plan Identify potential areas of resistance and how you plan to address these Force field assessment Design the interventions you need to make to facilitate the change process Transition curve, interventions plan Plan your communications messages, content and channels Communications plan Build a delivery schedule for each stage, containing the sequence of activities identified in both the Analyse and Prepare stages. Delivery schedule You can read more detailed information on the Prepare:Extended Guidance page. body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Learning and Development Human Resources Delivering Successful Change ‌ ‌‌‌ ‌Although a fundamental factor in the success of any transition is the quality of thought and planning that has gone into it, the implementation is unlikely to progress in a convenient linear fashion in accordance with carefully laid plans. The implementation is more likely to be an evolving process, with uncertain and unpredictable outcomes. The nature of the implementation usually means that active management is needed to resolve problems as they arise and to keep the change process on track. Identify and profile the measures to be used to monitor anticipated change outcomes Success measures Identify who will fulfil the key change roles, and ensure they have the necessary skills and understand the rules of engagement Change roles Deliver the change activities according to the plan Highlight report Take the actions necessary to achieve the anticipated change outcomes Communicate effectively and address relevant feedback throughout the implementation You can read more detailed information on the Deliver:Extended Guidance page. ‌ ‌ Explore HR HomepageMy RoleLeaving My RoleManaging My TeamHR Business Partnering TeamLearning and DevelopmentHR SystemsMy Rewards and BenefitsPensionsPayrollOccupational Health ServiceEquality and DiversityContact usStaff Surveys HR A-Z UniversityofPortsmouth Home Students Staff Contact and departments Home Staff services Departments Resources © University of Portsmouth Cookie policy Modern Slavery Statement Privacy policy Website terms and conditions

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Learning and Development Human Resources Delivering Successful Change ‌ ‌‌ ‌‌Once the change implementation is complete, it is vital to ensure new practices are integrated into day-to-day working. Without appropriate support and integration mechanisms in place, there is a danger that people slip back into old ways of working. To ensure new practices are integrated successfully, there need to be: Mechanisms in place to make new operating practices clear and visible. Mechanisms in place to ensure that those working in the new way are supported through continued clarification and upgrades of skills and knowledge to carry out the new practices. Mechanisms in place to capture and fix failures in new policies, practices or processes. A way for people to deal with situations which require a departure from standard practice. Make sure that new policies and practices are accessible and easy to use, and that all references to old policies and practices are removed. Provide staff with the means to check their work against the new standards, and link this to new reward mechanisms. Introduce appropriate symbolic changes to reinforce new practices. Provide additional guidance or case studies where there is uncertainty, and introduce mechanisms to enforce new practices through coaching, training, and sharing good practice. Identify appropriate mechanisms for staff to identify and fix things when they go wrong. Put your benefits plan into action. Capture the relevant lessons learned and identified how these will be applied in future. Lessons learned You can read more detailed information on the Embed:Extended Guidance page. ‌ ‌ Explore HR HomepageMy R

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Learning and Development Human Resources Background to Apprenticeships In 2012, the Government commissioned a review into Apprenticeships. The Richard Review set out ambitious plans to improve the quality of apprenticeships, making them more employer responsive and making assessment more robust. This has led to a complete reform to apprenticeships, in terms of how they are funded and the content of the programmes apprentices study. In the past, apprenticeships consisted of a “framework” of different qualifications, defined by sector groups as being required for the industry. However, a government review into apprenticeships identified that these did not represent the requirements of employers and needed to be more job role specific. As a result, “standards” have been introduced, which are designed and developed by employers to meet needs of the specific job roles demanded in each sector. Trailblazer groups made up of employers have trialled the new standards to ensure they are fit for purpose and the old frameworks are gradually being phased out as standards for each sector become ready for delivery. Traditional frameworks contained vocational qualifications, which were based around on-going assessment throughout the programme, with skills and knowledge frequently fragmented across the duration of the programme. The Richard Review recommended that to improve robustness of assessment, apprentices should be assessed by an End Point Assessment carried out by an independent organisation upon completion of training for an Apprenticeship standard, that covers all the key skills, knowledge and behaviours required of the job role. This synopti

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Learning and Development Human Resources Funding for Apprenticeships As part of the Government’s reform of apprenticeships, the Apprenticeship Levy was introduced in May 2017. The aim of the Levy is to increase the engagement and investment of large employers in Apprenticeship training. This will encourage development of long term plans to fill skills gaps and support economic growth in this country. All organisations that have an annual payroll of £3 million or more must pay 0.5% of their payroll through the PAYE process into a government-controlled online digital account. The Government adds a further 10% of all payments to the digital account each month. These funds can be drawn down and spent on Apprenticeship training with approved training providers, including Degree Apprenticeships delivered by the University to internal staff. Use of Levy funds is restricted by Apprenticeship Funding Rules, a complex Government document that is occasionally updated. Spending of Levy funds is audited to ensure compliance with Funding Rules. The main points of the Funding Rules are included in this guide, the full documentation can be found here. The University of Portsmouth annual contribution to the Levy is approximately £500,000. As a large employer paying the Levy, we would like to draw down on as much of the training allocation (paid by the government) as possible. Employing apprentices will increase the diversity of the University’s workforce and provide valuable opportunities to the young people of the area, in keeping with the University’s commitment to social inclusion. The Apprenticeship scheme has the Vice Chancellor’s and the UEB’s enthusiastic support. Employing apprentices will increase the diversity of the University’s workforce and provide valuable opportunities to the young people of the area, in keeping with the University’s commitment to social inclusion. Benefits for the University Apprenticeships increase diversity and innovation and enable opportunities to attract new talent. Development of workforce to meet future needs by offering skills gap training for existing staff Employees gain nationally recognised qualifications that are relevant to their role in the workplace and the University gains a highly skilled employee. Apprenticeships improve productivity and staff morale. Evidence shows that staff are incentivised by their manager’s overt commitment to their career development and respond with higher levels of performance in the workplace. This leads to higher levels of motivation and reduced staff turnover. Apprenticeships at all levels offer a real opportunity to enhance social mobility and can provide the first step on the career ladder for young people. They have the potential to break down barriers and increase access to higher education for disadvantaged students, whilst also contributing to loca

title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Learning and Development Human Resources Off the Job Training For an apprenticeship, 20% of the working time must be committed to off the job training. This means time is assigned to training the apprentice to gain additional skills, relevant to their employment sector to enable career progression. Training may be undertaken off - site, at a local college, or on - site by e-learning or by visiting trainers. Off-the-job training is defined as learning which is undertaken outside of the normal day-to-day working environment and leads to the achievement of the Apprenticeship. This can include training that is delivered at the apprentice’s normal place of work but must not be delivered as part of their normal working duties. The off-the-job training must be directly related to the Apprenticeship Framework or Standard and could include the following: The teaching of theory: lectures, role playing, simulation exercise, online learning or manufacturer training Practical training: shadowing, mentoring, industry visits and attendance at competitions Learning support and time spent writing assessments/assignments Off-the-job training does not include: The qualifying in English or Mathematics (up to Level 2) Progress reviews or on-programme assessment needed for an apprenticeship Framework or Standard Training which takes place outside the apprentice’s paid working hours Off the job training can be delivered on a day release (one day a week throughout), or block release basis (one week out of every five or a proportion at the beginning, middle or end), or by spending a proportion of every day on training. title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Learning and Development Human Resources Apprentice Levels Apprenticeships are available at different levels: Intermediate - Level 2: (equivalent to 5 GCSEs A-C) Individuals will be working with some guidance or supervision, this is often the entry level to an industry, for individuals with no experience in the sector. Advanced - Level 3: (equivalent to 2 A’ Levels) Individuals will be able to work independently (or in some cases supervise others), this enables individuals to apply a knowledge and skills at a detailed level. Higher - Level 4, 5 & 6: (equivalent to Foundation Degree or above) Individuals will apply detailed analysis of high level information, involves specialism/expertise in technical, managerial or professional roles. Degree – Level 6 & 7: (includes Batchelor’s or Master’s Degree) Specialist, high level knowledge, applying own ideas and research to complex situations, developing original responses to complicated/ unpredictable problems, professional management roles and senior professionals or managers. title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Learning and Development Human Resources Frequently Asked Questions Will existing staff take a pay cut if they embark on an apprenticeship? Salary levels for existing members of staff undertaking an apprenticeship will not be affected. When the individual starts the programme, an agreement will be signed between the University and the individual, confirming the arrangements. Can part- time staff undertake an apprenticeship? Any staff member may undertake an apprenticeship, however if their normal employment is less than 30 hours per week, the programme will need to be extended proportionately to allow the individual to gain the required experience and develop their skills and knowledge. . Will staff who are undertaking an apprenticeship have their job title amended whilst they are on the programme? There will be no change to job title if an existing member of staff undertakes an apprenticeship. Will staff who undertake an apprenticeship have to remain employed by the University for a period of time or pay back the fees? The University will be keen to retain staff who undertake an apprenticeship or any professional qualification which enhances their knowledge, skills and abilities. However, it is more important for the University that the employee completes the programme whilst working for the University. The Education and Skills Funding Agency specifically prohibits employers from requiring staff to repay fees for their apprenticeship training. title: Human Resources
body: <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> <iframe src="//www.googletagmanager.com/ns.html?id=GTM-P6N6C8K" height="0" width="0" style="display:none;visibility:hidden"></iframe> Go back to: About Us > Departments > Departments > Human Resources > Managing My Team Human Resources Managing Induction Overview These guidelines aim to support you in planning an effective induction programme for new members of your team for whom you have line-management responsibility. New members of staff will have received, along with with their offer letter and contract, a link to the Human Resources (HR) ‘Pre-Employment Welcome’ site https://sites.google.com/a/port.ac.uk/uop-induction/. You should also have received a copy. Virtual Induction: Guidance for Managers The University has a number of staff who are commencing employment over the coming weeks and months, during a period of time when we are not working on campus. For existing staff this is a period of transition but for new starters it is their first experience working at the University and we need to ensure it is welcoming, informative and most importantly, supportive. Please read the Virtual Induction: Guidance for Managers. What do I need to consider? As line manager, you should ensure that all new members of your team receive an appropriate induction, regardless of whether they are new to the University, have moved within the University, or even just within your department. It is your responsibility to ensure that new members of staff: Have an induction coordinator identified. The coordinator should welcome and introduce new staff to the department and be a point of contact for any questions they may have. Complete an Induction Checklist Have an appropriate induction programme organised, which links to work aims and objectives Understand what is required of their role – e.g. immediate work How the role fits in with department aims and

If you would like to embark on a project involving technology, or if you would simply like some support or advice on how to use the various elearning tools available at the University, the TEL team are here to help, contact us via email (elearn@port.ac.uk) or phone on 023 9284 3355. If you looking to book out Chromebooks for a classroom / exam sessions please fill out our TEL Chromebook booking form. Want to know more about the TEL team and what we've been working on recently?
